### ssj3 — 2014-06-03T01:48:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-129392>

This reads very similar to my experience.

I had survived for years on a keto diet (eventually a story for my optimal journal to put in context) but not when supporting high volume/intensity/density weight training. But when I switched to a keto diet again, I didn’t adjust for many factors (loss of calories specifically one - I actually wanted to test the calories don’t matter spin) and training really suffered. Also, my labs tanked, couldn’t sustain let alone add muscle, body comp worsened and I was starting to have to take days off due to poor recovery.

Now about 8 months in, consuming a lot more fat, a few more carbs (about 50-70 grams a day) and about 800-1000 calories per day more, things are starting to turn good. Leaner with weight up about 4kg. Now I don’t know if this is just the calories as I did start doing other things like eating oysters or muscles nearly daily, infrared therapy, 8-10 hours of sleep, etc.

Still some bio-hacking to do as recovery and ability to maintain intensity still needs to improve, but I like where this is going.

### Barry — 2014-06-04T19:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-129654>

Low Carb and even duration are just small parts of the jigsaw here. I've mentioned this before but its not about what you eat, its about how you live. Then the inner workings of the cell are found in the quantum physics as opposed to the biology or physiology. I don't know all the theory but I do know some and I know more about the practice.

So if a ketotic state is optimal for performance - neocortical neurons, electron flow, photons etc...... what I'm saying is that the practice is far more difficult to apply than the theory

here's a post from another thread where I discuss this:
_

Context 4: elite athlete, competing not just for fun but to win, at a high standard, national/world class level

Do they need PPP and can they access it ?

The first part of this question , I'm still on the fence. We simply have nothing to go on other than theory. Am I in PPP and winning races ? I can't say.... I'm winning some races but so is the dude on a SAD diet. If Jack starts winning world championship events, at any sport, in PPP and epi-paleo/CT etc, even if its N=1, then I'll buy into it more. But for now, and I;m not a reductionist, all we have is a hypothesis.
Are olympic gold medallists, marathon world record holders, Tour de France champions etc.... are these type of athletes in PPP ? nowhere near.... and I work in this game so I personally know a lot about these type of athletes. Many of them wouldn't even know what a ketone is. However, what I will say, is that this level of athlete, with their type of living and training, genetics and physiology..... are upregulating and activating many of the pathways that are being discussed here without them even knowing it.

Finally, the second part, possibly the main subject of the debate, can these type of athletes access it ? .... .... as I mentioned, this PPP system still needs more study both theoretically and clinically. Lets assume it does work and can be accessed.

Well, it comes down to what was mentioned in sjoshua's post. Everything needs to be in smooth working order. The right dots need to be in place, the dots need to be connected, and then everything has to work in harmony.

So lets say you're an elite athlete, going to the next world championships or olympic games. First up, you have to travel to get to the destination. Flights, EMF's, maybe poor food. You then are put up in a hotel. More EMFs, maybe poor sleep, no perfect keto/epi-paleo foods available, no where near grass, mountains or sea. Outcome - semiconductors, inner mitchondrial membranne, electron flow, cell signalling, etc etc... downregulated........... what happens ? you simply don't feel as well and don't perform as well.

It all comes down to a very simple thing. The real world is not set up for optimal living. Perfection is not achievable. Therefore embrace the imperfection. Don't try to be the best, just try to be better. Apply that to everything in life.

People here are worrying themselves about something that possibly doesn't work in the muscle cell from a energy production point of view, and if it does, they don't actually need it, but more importantly, it is practically unattainable in the modern world.

I'm off for a beer............_

I like the last paragraph:
_Our 7-million-year evolutionary path was dominated by
two seasonal challenges—calorie scarcity and mild cold stress.
In the last 0.9 inches of our evolutionary mile, we solved them
both. Refrigeration and transportation have fundamentally
changed the food to which we have access and the environ-
ments in which we live. We also sleep less and are exposed to
considerably more artificial light, particularly in the winter
months. Obesity and chronic disease are seen most often in
people and the animals (pets) they keep warm and over-
nourished. Similar to the circadian cycle and like most other
living organisms, it is reasonable to believe we also respond to
the seasons and carry with us the survival genes for winter.
Maybe our problem is that winter never comes._

### sjoshua — 2014-06-24T20:48:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-132057>

> [colt said:](/goto/post?id=131412)
>
> Wait a minute…….Doc explain this quick. Whats the mechanism? Whats the protocol? How often?
>
> _**I have an idea for you Barry to increase performance.......try a nicotine patch right before the race. It is a trick I used with one of the NFL players I did some work with an injury.**_

The mechanism is bending cellular time slightly. You're tricking your cells into working a little faster than normal by changing their reality. (similar to overclocking a computer - it was still designed to work at the native speed, but if you can keep the entropy to a minimum and control the environment the semiconduction is occurring in, then you can achieve faster processing from the same device)

This will only work if your cells can handle the bend though.... i.e. you need a healthy redox

The protocol is simply attaching the patch to the skin to deliver the nicotine slowly. The only time you'd want to do this is when in a race-type environment where you are on the clock... cause, you are 'overclocking' your cells.

### Barry — 2014-07-21T13:59:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-135063>

I'm fasting from technology as much as I am from food these days... I think the adaptations are actually greater...

But I'll try to also keep this thread going :

Race Update: A mountain ultramarathon, 73Km, 5000m climb. Race start: Midnight

So a couple of things to share here. This was a relatively "short" race for me but with the midnight start, and big mountain climbs with a finish at a ski resort, its a hard race.

Race Day:

First thing, early morning, fasted, I ran 30mins barefoot, then sat down in a river, not CT, its hot summer now, but more for grounding. I ate 3 meals before this race, no snacks, Breakfast was eggs. lunch was suasage/bacon and dinner was fatty pork chops/veggies. Took a 45min nap in the evening , in the garden on the grass. Brewed a fresh coffee at 11pm... race start then at 12pm.

It rained. Not cold. Its Italy. Just a proper summer storm, thunder/lightening, heavy downpours. I wore just shorts and a t-shirt. I ran the first few hours easy, no hunger or need to eat. Despite the time , darkness and running during what are "sleeping" hours, I felt great. Rain continued but the more I got soaked, the better I felt. As we ran through the forests and mountain trail, I even made sure to put my hands through as much of the greenery as I could to wet myself even more.

Reached the 40km mark and the first signs of daylight appeared. However, due to the weather and visability, the high peaks were deemed too dangerous and the course was re-routed. This reduced the distance to 55km and also missed out on the big mountain sections. I ran then full gas for the remaining 15km. Arrived at the finish in 10th, in just over 7hrs, but only minutes behind the next 4-5 athletes. Given another 15-20km with more difficult terrain and I felt like I would have been very close to a podium spot. During this 7hrs of running, I ate very little, some pieces of fruit, some cheese/cold meat, some coffee and just water... no gels, sports drinks or bars.

Always difficult to pin-point what makes your performance better.... but my take-outs from this are - fasting, earthing/grounding, fats and rain/electrons

### Barry — 2014-08-13T16:59:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-137755>

Truth Bomb Alert !

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25089149](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25089149)

and here something I've posted on this thread before which puts in the context of athletic performance:

_"Exercise is simple, and the body is clever. Our physiology is not intelligent, it is purely responsive.
I have been learning and experiencing the true meaning of these statements the more and more I practice what I do. You see, we now live in a world where modern technology, science, medicine etc has developed ways to essentially inhibit the human bodies natural responses. This lies true for both general health and fitness. I won't get into the health aspect but in terms of the fitness aspect, just think about the following.

We now have sports drinks and sports foods which are supposed to improve our performance and delay fatigue. However, if you've been following this thread, you'll know that food, in particular carbohydrates, eaten before or even during exercise downregulates fat oxidation, switches off PPP and inhibits ketosis. Yet, the world has been brainwashed into thinking that you cannot exercise unless you are fully carb loaded, constantly consume food during and constantly consume fluid during. In terms of hydration, I applying almost the same strategy I do with food. In other words, let the adaptation occur by allowing your physiology to respond. It doesn't respond if you are constantly drinking and constantly taking in electrolytes. You'll find a good explanation of this if you look up Prof Tim Noakes. He has shown that we can completely self regulate our own hydration, balance our sodium levels and self control our own hydration by drinking to thirst.

The above point holds true for many other things in sport and life. We have heart rate monitors and GPS watches to track our distance,splits, HR zones etc. We have compression garments to "prevent" muscle fatigue and recovery powders to help us "recover". We then have "ice baths" , sleep machines, stress monitors and apps that tell you when you can train. The list goes on. In my opinion, and this is an important point, its all a load of BS.

My reasoning is simple. Our bodies are clever. Let them be clever. This is the key point. It ties in with practically everything Jack is writing about. In order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate. If you want fat adaptation, keto adaptation, PPP activation, semiconductor functioning, electron flow, cell signalling, timing and quantum biology all singing and all dancing... then this is what you need to do, remove the inhibitors i.e. modern foods, modern devices and modern living. EMF's, carbs, blue light, heat, sports foods, sports monitoring devices and machines. Instead, let the body do the work."_

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-04T03:04:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-139864>

If you believe Tim Noakes and his Central Governor theorem, then you will also see that optimal performance and optimal health are one and the same. Here's a link to a recent short paper in 2012 where Noakes lays out his thoughts -- [http://bjsm.bmj.com/content/46/1/1.full](http://bjsm.bmj.com/content/46/1/1.full)

And if you believe the previous sentence, then every single sport will be improved by improving brain energetics, and can be done while eating a ketogenic diet.

With explosive type activities, you will never be able to exhaust the muscles, so the outcome is determined by nervous system health. Example: Olympic lifters rarely miss a lift because of strength limits. A guy who can snatch 140kg can probably overhead squat at least 160kg, and front squat way in excess of 200kg. They miss because they "mis-grooved" the lift -- in other words, nervous system not working optimally => improved by ensuring brain energetics are optimal.

As for the building of the strength base (max voluntary contraction with ability to control both concentric and eccentric), I think there is ample evidence to show that this can be done on any sort of diet (which would include the ketogenic diet).

With higher intensity "strength-endurance" type activities, say an 800m sprint, or one of the shorter track cycling events, or a frame carry in strongman, the common argument is that lactic acidosis stops further force production. Noakes addresses this in the article linked above, but I think it's safe to say that it's all about rate of energy production, and that we have vastly underestimated the amount of energy production possible using fat oxidation (see my note below)

With technique sports that require frequent bouts of high intensity like Basketball, I'd say athletes are limited by their ability to transfer skills practiced during training to the playing field, and by their ability to recover quickly from a bout of high intensity bursts. Again, I think it's the "mental game" that is the most critical here -- attaining the needed physical abilities is not limited by something like a ketogenic diet, and . In many ways, this is a scaled-down version of the sorts of training requirements that elite military personnel need, and these people are the ones finding great benefit from interventions like HBOT, Ketone salts, HRV training, etc ... which do more for the brain than skeletal tissue.

Longer duration Endurance activity is the domain of Noakes, and he contends that the research shows that athletes are not limited by VO2 max, and that controlling "subjective measures" like pacing and Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) seem to be the most critical factors in racing success.

And then of course, there's the research coming out of Volek and Phinney's lab now showing fatty acid oxidation rates of 1.4-1.9g among typical fat adapted athletes. (Ben Greenfield did a post about this awhile ago -- [http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/05/how-much-fat-can-you-burn-2/](http://www.bengreenfieldfitness.com/2014/05/how-much-fat-can-you-burn-2/)) By pure calorie figures alone, that's 1.4 \* 9 \* 60 = 756kcal per hour, and remember that this actually produces twice as much ATP for the same equi-caloric value in carbs. In other words, it is very possible to meet almost all of your energetic needs via the beta-oxidation of fats.

The commonality in all the cases is that performance limits are not dictated by strength limits, rates of waste clearance, etc ..., and instead are subject to the ability of the brain and nervous system to recruit themselves to maximal capacity on a consistent basis. Neural limits are best surpassed by having a constant flow of electrons into a brain and nervous system that is capable of storing and using those electrons (ie: DHA + water + lots of oxygen). In this way, I can see how ketones become a superior fuel.

\---

As for myself, I remain open-minded. What I wrote above is just my own exploration on any plausible mechanism behind achieving optimal athletic performance.

The key phrase is always "if you believe A, then B will be true", and then your job will become to test if this is true in your own personal context. Most athletes are unable or unwilling to apply such tests due to everything from lack of aware, to conflicting incentives, to a destructive environmental pressures.

In information theory we always have a saying, that "if you want to double the rate at which you gain new knowledge, fail twice as fast". So for me personally, my job is to run as many of these experiments as possible. The thing is, you can't run an experiment without first having a hypothesis. A hypothesis is just a belief in something unproven, but in which you have plausible mechanism to explain. But let's always remember that it's the belief that comes first, then you discover plausible mechanism, and then you test that mechanism, and then you formalise the conclusions.

\---

I also remind myself that this journey will take awhile. Personally, I was a pretty decent High School athlete -- easily dunk a basketball on a 10' rim, deadlift double my bodyweight, sub 18min 5km run, etc ... And then I proceeded to lose a lot of those abilities when I moved out to Melbourne Australia from Singapore (at the equator). That's almost 6 years since I made the move now, and I still haven't regained most of my physical abilities from High School. Nutrition has improved dramatically since then, training is better, circadian mismatches are less than before, but the environment here in Melbourne is sapping my health and physical performance.

Only now am I about to make the move to Cairns up in far north Australia (in a few days). I made a trip to Cairns 3 months prior, and stayed for 2 weeks. This proved to have dramatic improvements to my health parameters, as well as physical performance parameters (within a week of being there, a 50km bike ride went from requiring lots of effort, to being EASY).

But it took 6 years from feeling the effects of the problem (and not knowing it), to having both the knowledge and resources to make a major pro-survival decision. That's for a young single guy with all incentives aligned to trying to fix himself. I'd imagine the road to optimal living to be much more difficult for someone like a professional athlete, who is already invested heavily into a particular lifestyle. The relevance for us is that we can't often look to existing examples for guidance on how to reach optimal performance, but instead have to rely on known mechanism (E=mc2) along with logical self experiments to reach where we want to get to. Dr Kruse provides the mechanistic explanation of various topics, and I'll help myself to my own experiments

It was a "ding ding ding" moment. What I experienced went entirely against the grain, but intuitively I knew I was on the right track. When I discovered Dr. Kruse a month ago, I started eating Epi-Paleo, and I can't begin to express how amazing I feel and how my body has changed. This is LIFE at its fullest....which means everything to me as 4 years ago I was battling breast cancer for the second time.

### Danco3636 — 2014-09-06T06:21:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-140091>

Jacks reply.....

> **Administrator**
> 1\. Glad to see your paying attention.......most of the people in paleo need to read that truth bomb you posted in question one.
> 2\. wholly depends upon your redox potential.......which you will learn about in next blog.
> 3\. If you have liver, kidney, or gut issues protein re-feeds can hurt you. Why? Ammonia is produced from dietary amino acids and by catabolism of amino acids, amines, nucleic acids, glutamine and glutamate (nitrogenous wastes) in peripheral tissues (especially skeletal muscle). Gastrointestinal micro-organisms (primarily coliforms and anaerobes in the colon and cecum) convert dietary amino acids and urea into ammonia in the gastrointestinal system. The ammonia is absorbed into the portal circulation, taken up by the liver and converted in the liver, via the urea cycle, into urea. Urea is then excreted into the gastrointestinal system (producing a futile cycle) and into the urine.
> Of the total ammonia produced, 80-90% is shunted into the urea cycle, with the remaining 10-20% metabolised by peripheral tissues, including the kidney, heart, and brain. Everything is connected. The lower your redox potential the less effective your ammonia and urea cycle become to handle protein.
> 4\. Re feeds in the 4-6 PM window are optimal. re read this blog to see why:[http://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](http://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/)

### Barry — 2014-09-12T15:57:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-140734>

Dan and others:

All the answers are in EMF 4. If you look more closely, you will find things explained more simply

"_When timing is off molecular crowding begins to accumulate within a mitochondria and with enough time, chaos ensues in the cell to destroy cellular signaling.
Chaos in space is called entropy, and in chaos in molecular biology is called inflammation. Inflammation is positively charged, and inflammation leads to more mitochondrial inefficiency, and loss of electron chain transport efficiency. When we lose energy we lose the ability to run processes in cells_"

What causes molecular timing to be off ?? All the lifestyle and environmental factors that athletes (and others) are drowned in.... EMFs, Artificial Light, Stress, Non-Grounded etc...

When they lose electrons because of these factors, they lose energy. Now, serious/elite/full-time athletes need a lot of energy. Where's the easiest and quickest place to get this energy ? ATP-PC and Krebs Cycle..... whats the source ? Carbs

So, back to your original question... "is Epi-Paleo Ketogenic Diet still the best" regardless of environment/lifestyle ?

If you are an elite athlete, competitive, high level athlete.... that trains a lot and does big volume and intensity.... I would say NO.

Because there molecular timing will be off, so they will lose electrons and hence energy production capacity. Sure, there are all the adaptation benefits that I have written about extensively here that LCHF, Epi-Paleo, Cyclical Keto etc can bring. But a full ketotic state will not be possible to sustain.

If you work out in your garden a few times a week, go for walk, do some Movnat...... maybe a full ketotic state is possible even with molecular timing off.

But this is the key point that many guru's just don't get...... the energy requirement of a full time cyclist, triathlete, runner, crossfitter..... without getting into the argument whether these forms of exercise are good or bad..... is just so much far greater.... that the trade off between full ketotic state and molecular timing puts them in an energy deficit

I can't make it any more simpler than that.....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-142182>

My own update.

Wow slept almost 8 hours last night very deep sleep. (cranked AC down below 55 degrees F in my motel room) This was after training for over 8 hours with very fit folks that were mostly 1/2 my age doing body movement. And fitting in my own ocean swim at the lunch break and morning beach walk and stretching.

Mostly done in a fasted state (some BP coffee) and with no carbs, ketosis state.

Got up this morning sun on face and got my own minimalist fasted strength workout in before heading home.

Sleep, grounding, CT & lots of seafood (oysters) has kept me strong and healthy.

we got to 4, here's what my 5 were:

1\. Fasted State Training

2\. Cold Thermogensis

3\. Epi-Paleo Ketogenic Diet

4\. Epigenetics

5\. Altitude

What I think now is that "diet" does not have to be epi-paleo keto, it can help , but its not essential. Why ? well, there are now other factors that can just override any of the benefits of food. These factors are the laws of nature and 3 legged stool.... related of course to Epigenetics. In other words, if you are connected to the earth, not using technology/artificial light constantly, have a spiritual well-being..... then it doesn't matter how many oysters you eat.... gene expression will dictate

So fast, get cold, live high and connect to the earth.... of course, do your training.... and this is how you can optimise your athletic, particularly endurance, performance.

### Barry — 2014-10-09T12:20:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-143301>

My thoughts on the current state of play:

Do We Really Need Carbs for Performance 2.0 ;-) ?

This has been a journey spanning over 5yrs. I didn’t just pick up a book; listen to a podcast, read a blog and then start just eating loads of fat, no carbs and doing long runs on empty. I looked into everything I could (and still do), pieced it all together, and then implemented it gradually as I went along. It happened naturally like this due to the fact that I was just learning about the theory/mechanisms and then just trying them out myself. Then I realized the importance of adapting gradually, allowing the body (and mind) to develop almost in a stepwise fashion. This means that everything has time to be “broken in” and it means that all the parts of the machinery get to know each other so that they can work together well. It also means that you reduce the risk of blowing any fuses and breaking the circuits.

The reason I make those points is because of what I am seeing athletes doing today. They don’t give enough time for the parts to be “broken in” or get to know each other. This means the machinery never gets to work as efficiently or optimally as it could. More importantly, what is more likely to happen is people end up blowing fuses and breaking circuits? That’s then how you run yourself down, disrupt hormonal function, create adrenal stress, cause fatigue, illness and basically shoot yourself in the foot. I’ve had more athletes come to me recently for advice related to this rather then how to do it properly.

What you do need to do is first set the foundations. The foundations are found in the food base you choose to eat and where you source that food base from. That can take a bit of getting used to, it means desensitising yourself from certain foods and it means reprogramming your tastes buds and appetite. Just in that process itself there are a lot of hormonal and metabolic changes going on that need time. Once the foundations are laid then it’s about putting the framework and structure in place. That’s related to the types of foods you eat, when you eat them and how you eat them. Lots of enzymatic, transporter, neurotransmitter and more hormonal changes happen here. Again, this should be done gradually and in line with the individual context. You can then start installing the power lines and circuit boards. That means a lot of work behind the scenes. This can be related to nutrition interventions around your training or during your training or after your training. So you start rewiring things and putting in new connections. After that you can start furnishing, flooring and landscaping. This helps to make the house functional but more importantly it creates the right living environment. This is where non nutrition factors come into play such as Circadian Rhythm, Stress, Mindfulness and Epigenetics. Finally you might then make upgrades to the structure and even start building extensions. You can upgrade the power lines and build on to the already existing solid framework. This might mean that you start to upregulate other energy pathways such as the ketotic type and you can start bringing in new foods and timing strategies. Then you might move, to another location. That’s another journey.

But before I go way off track, here’s what some of this refers to:

\- Low Carb, Paleo, Safe Starches, Nutrient Density, Anti-Nutrients, Lectins, Omega3/6 ratio

\- Mono/Poly/Saturated, Grains, Gluten, Grass-Fed, Mycotoxins, Bioavailabilty

\- Insulin, Leptin, Cortisol, Glucagon, Adiponectin, IGF-1, Growth Hormone

\- Nutrient Timing, Nutrient Partitioning

\- Carb Cycling, Carb Backloading

\- Lypolysis, Beta-Oxidation, Fat Adaptation

\- Ketones, Ketosis, Keto Adaptation

\- Gut Microbiome, Brain-Gut Axis

\- Glycogen Depletion, Fasted State Training

\- Cytokines, Ecosanoids, Immunity

\- Inflammation, Ant-Inflammation

\- Vitamin D, Cell Signalling, Circadian Rhythm,

\- SIRT-1, PGC-1, Gene Expression

\- Amino acids, DHA, Neurotransmitters

\- Autophagy, Intermittent Fasting

\- Light, Electromagnetic Frequency, Epigenetics

And that’s just some. So it’s a process, with a big list of inputs that all need to be put together in a certain pattern in order for everything to work. Yet, very few athletes and even the “guru’s” seem to get this. What the general understanding and advice is to simply cut down to 50g a day carbs , get into ketosis and you then become superman or woman. So suddenly the process in this case, has two inputs. Therein lies the problem. Not only that but the inputs are given in their extreme forms. Cutting back to 50g is not what you need to do initially and it can do more harm than good. Ketosis is down the list of inputs and in fact is not even necessary to get many of the benefits/gains. In addition, the advice is not put in any sort of context for the individual. You might have an experienced Ironman or beginner distance runner or crossfitter or rugby player or professional cyclist. Each of these athletes needs a separate set of directions and even different destinations. They don’t all go the same way, in the same bus, to the same place. That’s as simple as I can put it. However, that’s essentially what’s happening. To make matters worse, they’re being told to go the most dangerous route, in a broken down bus, to a place that most of them don’t need to go to !

### Barry — 2014-10-09T12:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-143302>

continued....

It’s because of this that the whole “LCHF” for athletes is getting a bad name. It’s not just low carbs and it’s not just high fats. Then there this the big debate about the benefits of fat adaptation and now keto adaptation, particularly for endurance athletes. Again, the inputs are many, the time needed is long so this is why there is such an uncertainty and also quick conclusions being made. For instance, you have academic studies putting the subjects on 2 week LCHF diets. Without getting into all the other inputs that I have listed above, simply the timeframe is too little to the necessary adaptations to take place. Then you have anecdotal evidence. Athletes doing things maybe for 3 months… maybe longer… but using the extreme version of the inputs and not all of them. The reports I get/see here are that it can work for a very small minority depending on the individual context. But for most, they end up reducing performance, causing fatigue, stress, illness and even depression.

Like I said, I’ve been doing this myself for over 4yrs. In that time I have spent a long time laying the foundations, putting the structure in place, furnishing, landscaping and only recently putting on the extensions. I’m not saying everyone has to do the same and there are ways of cutting corners and getting things done more quickly. I have applied this with athletes I’ve worked with from beginner marathon runners to professional cyclists. What I have seen is a general 3-6 month time needed to set the foundations and put the structure in place. That’s when some improvements to general feel, energy and body composition happen. Another 3-6month period is where the power lines and circuit boards are set. This is when the first signs of performance might start to be seen in terms of efficiency/economy but also general health and wellness. Once the furnishings and landscaping start to happen, and that can go on for a while, maybe another 6-12months, is when bigger performance gains occur at the top end. After that, the extensions can start to go in and you might need some more time to get planning permission { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Having said that, this is still novel territory and there are still some grey area’s. The particular sport is crucial along with the level of performance. There is the general conclusion being made that “it might improve endurance but it doesn’t improve performance for high intensity” according to the studies. But the studies haven’t incorporated all of the contributing variables and unfortunately these types of studies also have a heavy commercial interest. So given the complexity of whats involved, to draw a conclusion without looking deeper is a bit premature. All I’m saying is that when you do look a bit deeper and you to factor the various inputs, things start to work a bit different. This of course is what I am observing in the field and not the lab, so they are N=1 uncontrolled experiments. However the field is often ahead of the lab especially when there are a lot of variables that the lab either can’t or hasn’t yet controlled. Or as Einstein himself put it “I have little respect for the scientists, that take the thinnest board of wood, and drill a great number of holes in it where the drilling is easy”

So , if you’re trying to go low carb, fat adapt, keto adapt, improve performance, compete and stay healthy for the long-term, there are lots of pieces to the jigsaw and it takes a while to put together. The sort of timeframe might be anything from 6-24months or even longer for some. The range is that wide because it varies so much depending on the individual.

It generally happens faster when you are experienced athlete, with say more than 10yrs of regular training, compete at the elite end and follow a healthy lifestyle/diet. That would be the 6 month group. If you are on the opposite end of the scale, then you are closer to the 24 months or longer.

How do I know this for sure ? I don’t but it’s based on what I have learnt, researched, practiced and applied over several years. Maybe call it my hypothesis. We’re all entitled to one or several. I say this for all the clever clogs that will start hassling me about references and studies to prove it. Then again, if you just follow today’s research, you will only know what the others knew several years ago.

There are athletes out there that want to do this correctly. It can improve performance, health and even lengthen an athlete’s career. I am working with a few pro cyclists on this at the moment and I’m going to take on a few more athletes that would be interested. I’m using a 6 month period of nutrition support applying all the principles above I mentioned above. Its primarily aimed at the experienced endurance athlete that can get the majority of the benefits within this timeframe.

This service will be added shortly to my store page. In the meantime, if there are any direct enquiries, please send email me on [barry@optimumnutrition4sport.com](mailto:barry@optimumnutrition4sport.com)

I have mentioned many times on my blog and forums and on our FB groups that the mainstream ancestral people don't get the " atomic scale argument" when it comes to things like iodine and T3 and T4. One of the great things about seafood is that it contains selenium which helps convert thyroid hormones to active T3. While you can eat more carbs to increase free T3 levels (a common paleo recommendation), this increases the need for iodine even more. This creates a huge issue chronically especially in the CSF. ([http://www.medical-hypotheses....](http://www.medical-hypotheses.com/.../S0306-9877.../abstract\)). Most of the T4 is stored in our gut, so by eating seafood, you allow form to meet function as the seafood can permeate the gut where majority of T4 is stored, allowing for the conversion of more T4 to T3." While DHA has pretty amazing quantum effects, as Bill has talked about here before, it needs to be paired with iodine (as that's the HUMAN body's preferred antioxidant system) to protect DHA from oxidation. This occurs in synapses due to this Grotthuss mechanism especially for heat transfers. So, we've come full circle. You can either eat seafood and get a great evolutionary package of things like DHA, iodine and selenium, which will support thyroid health, or you can follow the traditional paleo path lacking in seafood DHA and iodine and continually up your carb intake to offset this loss. The "leadership of ancestral health" needs to tell people this will trigger a need for more iodine from their diet and since you're not getting it in "their" diet, your body will divert to backup antioxidant systems like vitamin C, vitamin E and glutathione. NO one is saying this because it hurts their business models. Now you see why so many young paleo folks follow "their thought leaders" eat lots of carbs and take supplements like glutathione and vitamin C year-round. They're forcing their bodies to rely on a suboptimal pathway which requires this constant band-aid approach.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-144173>

DHA turns light into an electrical signal. When DHA is missing the electrical signal is missing in CM and tissues. What are the implication of this? Mitochondria have two CM and a matrix filled with H+. This is where mass charges are saved. Without DHA you can no longer use them as a super capacitor of electric charges. Why is this big? Without a charge is alters the masses of the subatomic particles within mitochondria. This is physics 101. This also points out why modern obesity researchers fundamentally clueless about fat. None of them are aware that according to Einstein's mass equivalence equation (E=mc2) that the mass of subatomic particles is an energenic variable that is subject to the electric charge of the surrounding environment; that is why the universal constant for gravitation has been shown to consistently inconstant by physics experiments when they have decided to measured it in space. Well that science scales to the surrounding environment in our mitochondria too. These laws are universal. No one seems to realize it. This implies that as our mitochondria lose their electric charge because DHA and iodine are missing in action, masses of all subatomic particles in mitochondria increases as well. At a macroscopic level where biology scale resides, we observe increases in our weight gain because we are losing energy from the interior of the mitochondrial matrix as ECT slows. As ECT slows electric charge drops. Hyperlipid has been pounding this point in his proton series for months. No one gets the implications. This is why we humans get fat fundamentally.

The zeta potential force might be electric, pressure gradient, concentration gradient, or gravity. (bread crumb alert) Astronauts lose their zeta potential just being in space off our planet. You don’t realize that modern humans also lose it, being disconnected from Earth. The further you go away from the planet surface the worse it gets. When you couple this to an ionosphere loaded with microwave energies you have the perfect storm for a pandemic of low vitamin D in humans. For this blog the zeta potential is tied to RBC’s cell membranes that turn sunlight into an electric signal in blood. Recall the zeta potential is how much power is stored in electrons in blood plasma. This can be raised by exciting electrons in blood plasma with photons from the sun’s light. It can also be lowered by loss of photons or electrons from the blood plasma. These effects were studied by Eidinow from 1925-34. Sunlight also has infrared light and this acts to lower our blood pressure by increasing proton flows in arteries, provided the arteries are hydrophilic. Today we see millions of people with hypertension. Chronically this leads to heart disease and stroke. Both are top killers in modern humans. It is not fat in your diet causing this folks.
Arteries are hydrophilic when they are preloaded with electrons while protons flow freely within the plasma because of Dr. Gerald Pollack’s work and the Grotthuss mechanism working in unison. The Grotthuss mechanism describes the way protons rapidly move through water. Sunlight powers both of these things naturally without any other energy needed. Physics is infinitely deep while biology is infinitely complex, where they meet is where your life comes from. Biophysics is the scale where the life sciences must head.

I'm going to throw Dan Koifman under the bus here . His comment -- [https://www.facebook.com/groups/872...id=882949748384587&offset=0&total\_comments=19](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/882734145072814/?comment_id=882949748384587&offset=0&total_comments=19)

I'll just post the 3 comments.

    Thyroid, sex steroids... Lots of hormones have seasonal rhythms.

-   [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)
    AND ALL \[Hormones\] CONTROLLED BY QUANTUM MECHANISMS DANO. Say it. I know it kills ya.

\------

Implied in that statement by Dan is that understanding lego pieces doesn't help you that much in building a lego sculpture. (though it I may be reading that wrong -- damn I hate New York sarcasm)

In any case, there is a core assumption surrounding my pursuit of optimal health:

So long as you provide enough energy to the body's systems, it will harness that energy to generate optimal health.
​

In other words, there is always an energy deficit in our current environment, and it is my job to provide my body with as much energy as possible.

If you believe that, you will start to dig for ways in which the body generates energy. Then you will find out that the water + collagen + electric charge provides a way to both store and transmit energy. Then you will come to the realisation that electrons and light form the currency of energy exchange for the body. Then you will discover DHA, which can broker between electrons and photons.

An electron or photon can be used ANYWHERE where it can be captured and aligned. Doesn't matter if it's just emitted from a supernova, sprayed out of a surgical laser scalpel, or thrown out of a mitochondrial cytochrome.

The programmer in me immediately saw this as a first-class data which could be passed to any function capable of understanding that data. Furthermore, these functions were idempotent, and (ostensibly) pure, making functional composition possible. I say ostensibly pure because even these functions were built on some assumptions. The assumption was that the input data continued to behave in the same fashion. But because electrons were affected by EMF, I couldn't bet on this assumption. What would happen if there was a force messing with my computer's compiler that caused it to throw random errors at times? The body is smart, and isolates failures to a single cell and communicates through message passing, but have enough failures, and the system breaks down.

Once you believe that "an electron is an electron is an electron", then there is almost no distinguishing the lego pieces from the lego sculpture. Taking care of the pieces immediately leads to a good sculpture.

The assumption baked into that last statement is that the body is smart, and can figure out how to take the lego pieces and build up the clusters (hormones) needed to make the right type of sculpture. This is not always true if the body is already completely broken, but most of the time the body knows how to health itself.

Again, if there is any major assumption I am making now, it is that there has been enough trial and error in our native environment to produce the body with the work functions that can take energy and put it to useful work. My job is just to supply that energy, and stop interferences with the work functions.

Boring is a synonym for low stress. Most of need to use meditation to balance stress. Some of us use stress to win but that is a learned trait. Not everyone got that lesson. True character is revealed in the choices a human being makes under pressure - the greater the pressure, the deeper the revelation. Pressure can be a privileges when it is directed. No pressure, no diamonds. As you aim higher in life it seems as though you have more responsibility. This responsibility creates stress and pressure. Without these guiding factors, I can't grow and achieve my vision of greatness. I stay focused and channel my efforts via my tunnel vision and I begin to marvel at the amount of pressure I can create in the innovation of that vision. It's not what you want or think that you can achieve, it's what you can accomplish that really matters. The more prepared your wisdom is, the less pressure you feel in any task you take. That is the lesson I had to teach myself.

### Shijin13 — 2014-10-20T16:25:21+0300

> THIS IS THE BIG HUMAN PROBLEM NO ONE SEES:
>

Why would most humans want to see what's killing them? denial isn't just a river in egypt. I've had conversations with people about this, especially women who are worried about BC - and I asked them if they would change their habits, particularly their artificial light and computer habits, if it could prevent BC. only 1 out of 20 of my friends said they would. They don't want to give up their lifestyle of lights on all the time, tv, computer - on the possibility it could eliminate their chance at BC. Yet these same women would rather hack off their girls as a preventative measure. I asked them why is surgery the solution? What if you could avoid the surgery by just turning off the lights at night?

I'm the person that makes people ask the hard questions. I have a coworker who I turned on to paleo about 4yrs ago. and he's all on board. we recently started working together again.... he started asking my why I wear the blue blockers during the day. when I explained why. He then asked me if he should do that too. my reply was read the current circadian research coming out. and ask yourself if you were designed to live in artificial light all day/ night?

2 days later he came back and told me he started using a red headlamp at night and stopped using his ipad and computer. and started listening to his books on tap in the evenings.

### Danco3636 — 2014-10-20T20:13:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-144431>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=144381)
>
> Boring is a synonym for low stress. Most of need to use meditation to balance stress. Some of us use stress to win but that is a learned trait. Not everyone got that lesson. True character is revealed in the choices a human being makes under pressure - the greater the pressure, the deeper the revelation. Pressure can be a privileges when it is directed. No pressure, no diamonds. As you aim higher in life it seems as though you have more responsibility. This responsibility creates stress and pressure. Without these guiding factors, I can't grow and achieve my vision of greatness. I stay focused and channel my efforts via my tunnel vision and I begin to marvel at the amount of pressure I can create in the innovation of that vision. It's not what you want or think that you can achieve, it's what you can accomplish that really matters. The more prepared your wisdom is, the less pressure you feel in any task you take. That is the lesson I had to teach myself.

"Boring is a synonym for low stress".

For me Boring is stress. Unless I am just in the moment then it's neither stressful or boring.

But being bored is stressful for me. Movement, activities and making it happen is my stress relief.

I just love to be engaged and find I have lots of energy to do "something".

"Star gazing on the grass is being engaged too".

For me energy is life. Life is either moving or dieing and I love moving.

I live with energy.
I sleep deep and hard.
These keep boredom away and life interesting.

### Danco3636 — 2014-11-10T02:12:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-146694>

Barry, interested your take on Bullet proof coffee (coffee with GF butter & MCT's) during a fast or outside of a fast.
Do you do BP coffee or anything in your coffee during your fast?
Do you think there is any advantages/ pro's or con's of BP coffee....
Technically it seems to break a fast but is there benefits for possible ketone up-regulation and fat burning?
Any thoughts on it would be appreciated..... Thanks!

### Barry — 2014-11-19T12:16:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-147802>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=146694)
>
> Barry, interested your take on Bullet proof coffee (coffee with GF butter & MCT's) during a fast or outside of a fast.
> Do you do BP coffee or anything in your coffee during your fast?
> Do you think there is any advantages/ pro's or con's of BP coffee....
> Technically it seems to break a fast but is there benefits for possible ketone up-regulation and fat burning?
> Any thoughts on it would be appreciated..... Thanks!

I wrote a post a while back where I basically said that every aid you give the body, the less it adapts. So in a similar sense, that's my view on this BP coffee stuff.

Whats the best way to go ketotic ?? Low carb, fasting and fasted state training.... no BP coffee required. BP, although carb free , is still putting the body in the "fed" state

Now, like every single thing I write about here.... it all depends on the context. Can SCTs and MCTs help or upregulate an already keto adapted athlete..... maybe yes. Could this be of benefit from simply a energy perspective (lets not get into calories).... for an athlete, with a big training volume, who is only eating 2 meals per day, so BP mitigates the affect but helps to prevent a deficit as well... then maybe also.

I hope you get what I mean here. The problem I see is how people who are no where near being ketotic or fat adapted..... that could benefit hugely by applying the simple principles - LC, Fasting, FST.... CT also..... are not really doing these things.... but Dave Asprey said drinking BP makes you produce loads of ketones... so they do it... with more butter, MCT and other powders that they don't actually need

"In order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### JoeBranca — 2014-11-21T21:06:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-148100>

I've been gradually ratcheting up my version of fasted state training sessions over time... yesterday was: black coffee in the morning, lots of water throughout the day. at lunch break I hit the beach, mobility work / Movnat on the sand, very light swim, some cliff scaling, sprint back to the car.

When I got home from work a few hours later, I did a lifting session: BB squats, weighted pulls, deadlifts (finally squeezed out a couple of reps @ 390 woo hoo!) around 4pm - 5pm, so at that point I was fasted for about 22-23 hours, (truly did not feel any hunger!) Massive meal for dinner. Slept good, and so far good energy today.

I suppose I took one "aid", BCAAs just before and between the lifting sets: no powders, just a few soft boiled pastured egg whites. Curious if I should try skipping these and be truly fasted throughout the resistance training.

### ssj3 — 2014-11-21T23:27:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-148117>

Nice work Joe with that 390 pull... 400 coming up next. As for fasting and lifting, I have many thoughts of this that the individual will need to contemplate. Volume, frequency, intensity and goals all need to be considered if you want to go this route. Also outside stress, sleep and food intake need to be considered. (gasp - yes I am saying that "food/calories" in around about way matter at some point; modern environment and lifestyle are taking 'energy' and eating like a sparrow isn't going to help).

Personally it all feel apart when I pulled intra-workout nutrition, kept food intake low during a narrow window and kept volume and frequency high (7 days, even two a days). Intensity naturally dropped as I couldn't sustain the output. Could I auto-regulate and deviate from plan? Nope. Did I follow my performance as the guide and not just the art of training? Nope.... and this is a critical factor to consider.

### Danco3636 — 2014-12-11T05:47:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-149806>

My notes from Barry's podcast.

Not just ketosis for fuel
Body to burn actual fat Beta Oxidation.
Muscles store fat for fuel instead of glycogen. Up to 3000 calories stored.
Then the fat can now go directly into the mitochondria.
The more you train the body the more you can store.
Fuel is fat into mitochondria. Ketones isn't the best measurement of a fat burner.

Gluconeuogenisis as fuel.
Ketones & ketosis as fuel.
Fat as fuel in the mitochondria.

Adaption:
Long term process. The longer you do it the better you get. Have a good food based to express this adaption. A practical way you can eat long term and enjoy and thrive on.

Need to over come sugar and sweet tooth. Replace with cream - fat cream.
Retrain your taste buds.
Low carb high fat eating.

Low GI foods.
Breakfast eggs and yogurt.
No starch veggies.
Coconut, avocados, lard and fat foods.
Use fibrous veggies.
Add fats to meals.

Ancestral based diet adding fats and taking out higher carb foods. Not just eating sticks of butter but whole foods. Long term project.
Foods you enjoy eating for years/ a way of life.

Step 2 nutrient timing:
Hormone response.
No carbs before going into workouts. Fasted or low GI meal pre or at breakfast.
Some athletes may need to back load some carbs post workout for recovery, glucose signaling in brain and appetite signaling. Context. Who the athlete is and current load or racing needs to be considered. Depends on adaption and how long one has been at it.

Fat burning session.
Ben -
CT - black coffee only - workout - finish up with CT, then a fat based meal or shake.
Keto meals later perhaps later lower GI carbs.
Barry -
Try doing that and having only coffee after 2+ hours out in the mountains running?
Again context.
Barry comes back and later incorporates a bit of mobility and perhaps MovNat and other things.
Barry eats perhaps two meals a day and after his long day his meal will be later in the day.

Fasted state training:
Barry works in research here.
Mitochondria growth.
Biggest adaptions through fasted state training.
Stress response from training sending signaling. Protein signaling and response for adaption. Messengers to mitochondria and fat burning and to be more efficient.
1\. 16-18 fast or longer.
2\. Training without a breakfast. (No BP coffee).
These two are the big ones to create this response and send the signal.
Slowly extend the fasted state training. Not all at once but work on it.
Barry likes to start off people doing this once a week. Learn to extend this and then adapt up to two a week. Barry does it up to 6 days a week. But once or twice a week fasted long workouts are a safe way to adapt.
Ben includes daily but uses shorter sessions and longer ones 1-2 times a week. Keeps him lean and he can eat huge meals.
Most all workouts done fasted. Doing back to back long sessions can dig you into a hole (if not adapted).

Lifestyle (environmental):
Sleep - effects of sleep, cortisol and melatonin. First look to how your sleep is. Work things to improve your sleep to optimize.
Quantim Mechanic's of how it works. Quantium physics - redox and cell signaling.
Electron transportation and how lifestyle all effects this.
EMF disruption and its effects on how this effects this signaling.
How efficient you can become depends on lifestyle factors. EMF is a big one.
No iPads in bed, Wifi and iPhones all day. You wont get the full fat adaption. You need to limit these.
Inflammation from lifestyle you wont make a good adaption. This comes from how you live your life and your environment. It all effects your fat burning signaling. Everything is connected not just the diet.
We are controlling information.
Grounding and connecting - getting barefoot and using the cold. A hippie kind of thing but it has a quantum effect.
Sleep
Cold
Grounding
Non-native EMF limiting.

Competing:
Practical implications and considerations.
Flying and traveling.
Bring your own things. Coconut oil, butter foods etc. Do what you can to minimize damage.
Traveling bring avocados and sardines for traveling meals. Avoiding sugar and crap food. Keep inflammation levels low. Keep it fat adapted with simple fixes.
BCAA's
Magnesium in spray form.
Fueling for racing:
Central Governor
Brain - feeds on ketones and glucose.
Small glucose through natural fruit, honey or natural foods to keep the Central Governor signaling good. Not to fuel the muscles but to keep the brain happy. Don't need to have excess glucose, keep it fat burning.
Lower carb fueling and natural foods for longer distances. No need to throw off fat burning. Not eating all lard when racing but perhaps having a banana for "brain" fueling.
Also all depends on the athlete and context.
20-30 grams of carbs per hour for some adapted athletes and racing pretty much all fuel from fat.
Barry doing steady state long distance racing with not much fuel needed besides his own fat.
Improve what you do when traveling has an effect on how you will race.

Overview:
1\. Work on upping healthy fats.
2\. Nutrient timing.
3\. Fasted training for signaling and adaption.
4\. Environment & lifestyle/ EMF, grounding etc.
5\. Racing ok to use some glucose and mitigate damage when traveling.

Barry does coaching on the Ben Greenfield site. He also has a "Primal Three" workshops coming up in the new year. Will be going in-depth on many of these things discussed.

### Barry — 2014-12-30T21:05:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-151195>

Guru’s and Athletic Performance

I don’t think any one of us knows it all. I think that’s one of the most important things for any self confessed “guru” to acknowledge. Science is infinite so therefore our knowledge should never be and will never be finite. Its this finite approach which leads to reductionism. I see this in the academic world when it comes to research on fat adaptation and low carb performance. They drill many holes in the same place on the same thin piece of wood. Then they draw their conclusions based on that, instead of drilling deeper in other places.

What happens then in the paleosphere is that anyone can call themselves an expert. They read some books, articles and listen to some podcasts , then rehash everything that they have just read and listened to. The worrying thing is that many of these so called experts have no science background either educationally or professionally. Jimmy Moore has a degree in Politics… and now has published two books on complex nutritional/medical topics. Dave Asprey does IT… and now has created his own branded “diet” and supplements. I have nothing against these guys and have never met them so I’m using them simply as an example. There are various people out there selling books, consults, powders and pills with no science of medicine qualifications and have never worked in this field. Sure, lots of these are intelligent people and self educated and self taught but, and a big but, many of them don’t know the deep stuff, the deep science, that you really need to know to truly understand the area. Put it this way, ask Jimmy Moore or any self confessed “guru” what a covalent bond is and to draw one on a sheet of paper… a very very simple bit of chemistry on a deeper level, yet most wouldn’t be able to do this. And therein lies the problem… they might know the technical terms and they might be able to talk the talk… but they don’t comprehend the talk.

Personally, this is what I am seeing in this world of low carb for athletes, paleo, fat/keto adaptation. I see these same guru’s that claim you can turn into a super athlete if you just eat no carbs, and eat loads of coconut oil and grass fed red meat.

That’s a load of bullshit. I started this blog post over 2 years ago. Previous to that I was 3 years studying, researching and practicing this both on myself and in my job. However, I still haven’t come to a conclusion and I am still searching for answers. I am still questioning things and I am still learning. I also haven’t written a book or released a magic pill that turns you into a magical fat burning machine (because there isn’t one by the way). There is then the whole issue of context. Sure, get someone off the donuts and get them on a wholefood diet and they might improve their 4h marathon time. But if you are looking at winning the marathon, running over 1.5hrs quicker, then the context is completely different. This is causing a huge amount of confusion for many athletes today.

What I have realized is that most people don’t know shit about athletic performance when it comes to the world of paleo, low carb and nutrition. They are either in the trenches, reading all the literature, like the clever guys like Paul Jaminet and Chris Kresser… but they’ve never been in the field so they can’t see what really happens. Then you have all the folk in the field, who are getting to grips with the practice, but have no idea how it works.

Where I am is in both places and I’m diving in and out. Each time I do so I bring something from the trenches into the field and vice versa. Things start to take on a different complexion then.

In terms of athletic performance… you take magnetism, electron flow, molecular timing out from the trenches and into the field of ketones, mitochondria and krebs cycle…. And what you have in the trenches then looks a lot different in the field.

So my own field is constantly changing and I’m still bringing in things that are coming from the trenches. This creates lots of battles, some are won, some are lost, but the war still goes on ! I have my own limitations too… my chemistry and biology might be good but my physics isn’t. I have a degree and masters but not a doctorate. So I’m not perfect and nor do I claim to have all the answers.

I like to always keep things simple… so what do I mean by all this ?

Take anything (be it books, plans, consults or supplements) that comes out of those who just live in the trenches or just play in the field… that tells you it can make you into a super fit, all singing, all dancing keto/low carb athlete… with a very big pinch of salt.

Oh, it also means…. FST hasn’t finished ;-)

“Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined”

### CTforlife — 2014-12-30T21:22:15+0200

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=151195)

>
> I don’t think any one of us knows it all. I think that’s one of the most important things for any self confessed “guru” to acknowledge. Science is infinite so therefore our knowledge should never be and will never be finite. Its this finite approach which leads to reductionism. I see this in the academic world when it comes to research on fat adaptation and low carb performance. They drill many holes in the same place on the same thin piece of wood. Then they draw their conclusions based on that, instead of drilling deeper in other places.
>
> What happens then in the paleosphere is that anyone can call themselves an expert. They read some books, articles and listen to some podcasts , then rehash everything that they have just read and listened to. The worrying thing is that many of these so called experts have no science background either educationally or professionally. Jimmy Moore has a degree in Politics… and now has published two books on complex nutritional/medical topics. Dave Asprey does IT… and now has created his own branded “diet” and supplements. I have nothing against these guys and have never met them so I’m using them simply as an example. There are various people out there selling books, consults, powders and pills with no science of medicine qualifications and have never worked in this field. Sure, lots of these are intelligent people and self educated and self taught but, and a big but, many of them don’t know the deep stuff, the deep science, that you really need to know to truly understand the area. Have mercy for me, because I haven't finished. Don't take any of this the wrong way, there's two sides to every coin. We can learn from our past or attempt to run from it.

QED, universal consciousness, god, whatever you call it, has a way of being counter intuitive. For at my solid rock bottom in life, I built a solid foundation to build the biggest monuments. At this point in my life a rat, a rock, a star, could have all the answers. It's never where you're looking but it's exactly where you're not.

Jack kruse, mark sisson, you, me, kris, jimmy , Ben, etc. all apart of a bigger deal. You're more right than you know when you say not one has all the answers. But you must think logically about your statement. If not one person has the answers, the answer can come from questioning anything, anybody, any way.

You don't need a degree in the past world to know something in the aquarian age, that's like actually thinking the world was flat until we looked at it and became it round.(diff day).

There's one difference in life I have never shared until now.
Anything is right for anyone. Anything is right for anyone. For anyone creates what is right for themselves. Now is heaven a place or a time where we all live forever in moral purity.

Enough for today.

### Barry — 2015-01-18T19:18:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153271>

" Some things benefit from shocks; they thrive and grow when exposed to volatility, randomness, disorder, and stressors and love adventure, risk and uncertainty. Yet, in spite of the ubiquity of the phenomenom, there is no word for the exact opposite of fragile. Lets call it AntiFragile. Antifragility is beyond resilience and robustness. The resilient resists shocks and stays the same; the antifragile gets better." from the book AntiFragile by Nassim Taleb.

This underlines practically everything I have written about here since I started the post almost 3yrs ago

### Danco3636 — 2015-01-18T20:00:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153275>

Fractal approach. It is how I aproach my health and fitness/ performance.
Randomness, spikes and intermittency but in an overall organized way. It rhymes but allows spikes and adaptions.

### Josh — 2015-01-19T00:11:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153281>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=153271)
>
> " Some things benefit from shocks; they thrive and grow when exposed to volatility, randomness, disorder, and stressors and love adventure, risk and uncertainty. Yet, in spite of the ubiquity of the phenomenom, there is no word for the exact opposite of fragile. Lets call it AntiFragile. Antifragility is beyond resilience and robustness. The resilient resists shocks and stays the same; the antifragile gets better." from the book AntiFragile by Nassim Taleb.
>
> This underlines practically everything I have written about here since I started the post almost 3yrs ago

I would hazard a guess that if we had better data on an individual's mitochondrial DNA and mitochondrial health as it has expressed epigenetically in a given life, things might seem a lot less random. It might be clear who would benefit from stress when and where. There is no current standard for tracking these things with tools available today. Thus among other things, we do not have any good records to see this in any population. This video suggests that there are very relevant factors within an individual's mitchondrial genetics that will determine what optimal activity, climate, etc. might be for an individual. In my experience, all is well and good in stressing a system as long as it "can" respond. Beyond that, it breaks down. N=1

### ElEva — 2015-01-19T19:00:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153352>

Hello, I apologize in case this has already been discussed here, I seem to have missed it. I just recently started having breakfast at all, and normally would do my workouts in a fasted state (late morning/midday). My workouts are dense (30-40 min) and heavy (DL, squats, lunges, bench press, pullups etc). I observe that I do not have that much power/strength if I have breakfast (70-80% fat) vs. fasted.
Somehow, I can do DL 5x5x1,5 BW fasted and still continue with heavy lunges, but after starting eating breakfast I barely manage 4x5x1,5 BW. I wonder what happens in both cases....

Josh ... That misses my point

You say "more data" .. I say less is more !!

Here's another quite from the book that explains what I mean:

"The antifragile loves randomness and uncertainty, which also means - crucially- a love of errors, a certain class of errors . Antifragility has a singular property of allowing us to deal with the unknown , to do things without understanding them - and to do them well."

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-20T00:29:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153379>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=153372)
>
> Josh ... That misses my point
>
> You say "more data" .. I say less is more !!
>
> Here's another quite from the book that explains what I mean:
>
> "The antifragile loves randomness and uncertainty, which also means - crucially- a love of errors, a certain class of errors . Antifragility has a singular property of allowing us to deal with the unknown , to do things without understanding them - and to do them well."

I think Josh's point is valid. Some people are definitely in a state whereby a little bit of stress from exercise will break their system and cause them to fail. They are already sick to the level that they are not currently Antifragile

Now the question is: How do you tell that you are ready for the upcoming stress?

Of course, this varies on a day-to-day basis in individuals as well. Many individuals can intuitively sense when they are ready for action, but I do see the value of extra measurement devices as well.

For example, there has been success by people like Ben Greenfield in using Heart Rate Variability tracking as a metric for deciding how coherent the system is, and how much stress it is likely able to take. We then hope that the athlete can does the stress appropriately.

Is the intuitive sense enough to achieve peak performance in the modern world? I'm personally biased to think that if one minds their redox potential, then the answer is yes.

Is extra data valuable? I think so, and if I had the choice, I'd have as much data as possible shoved into my face, so that if there is something that I can **safely** and accurately track, I will .........

I will give the data a quick study, and then proceed to ignore it and operate on instinct . Data that doesn't feed instinct and intuitive readiness is not valuable long-term IMO.

.....

"A Complex system, contrary to what people believe, does not require complicated systems and regulations and intricate policies. The simpler, the better. Complications lead to multiplicative chains of unanticipated effects. Because of opacity, an intervention leads to unforseen consequences, followed by apologies about the "unforseen" aspect of the consequences, then to another intervention to correct the secondary effects, leading to an explosive series of branching "unforseen" responses, each one worse than the preceding one"

Fascinating. This is Quantum-mechanics like behavior. I've been suspecting that a certain degree of self-observation collapses what is a soft, _fluid_ system of say performance (or emotional state, or ambiguous situation), into a state-freeze which both defines the state for the observer-self currently _and_ retroactively. Whether the act of self-defining through measurements is accurate or not, the consequence is immediate removal of all other possibilities of one's superposition; greater, perhaps unknowable, potentials.

### JoeBranca — 2015-01-26T22:53:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-153921>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=153784)
>
> what would be more fascinating would be for you to go and read the other 33 pages on this blog post before you start making assumptions...

Honestly, I apologize for thinking aloud in your thread, I'm not qualified or experienced whatsoever in the main topics being discussed so this will be my last comment -- the particular assumption I'm pondering is when and which are the _right questions_ to ask in seeking insight of the self in function and performance. Maybe a stretch, but a quick analogy would be two people in an ambiguous relationship where one is suddenly asked by the other "Do you love me?" Perhaps not the right question at the right time for better situational insight, but then the one being asked immediate reflexes to collapse his/her cognitive state and sense of their history into a binary answer to the question. Say it's a Yes - whether it is an answer in earnest, or panic, or self-deluded selective memory - it ends up retroactively state-freezing the situation the two people are in, and define the path forward as something it was not a moment earlier. Their actions in the relationship by and large may be the same as before and after that question was answered, but the situation has become concrete one way or another, and thus motive, cognitive state, assumptions of potential and direction and boundaries, etc are all different, maybe radically. An act of self observation and measurement nailed down the superposition.

Back to function in performance, riffing off that quote at the top, it does make sense that more complex means of self measurement and assessment can force answers about oneself while otherwise being in a state with more unknowns, less sense of limits, and more dependency on innate sense. Perhaps - the wrong questions at the wrong time, defining the situation that then alters the dynamics from before and after. It's not however an argument against having the right tools at the right time, it's determining right use of tools to answer the right questions at the right times. out

Don't let anyone bring you down. Don't bow, roar. You're a lion. Anytime you have intuition run with it. Science is the art of admitting what we don't know. You don't lose anything when you step beyond science, you gain something. It's Newtons third law, sometimes you must lose something to gain something, and vice versa. One door opens, another closes.

Today, all iphone users will measure something to say its definatively there. They must see it with their own two eyes and have it slap them in the face.
Tomorrow, in a new age, you will not measure anything beyond what your mind already knows to be true.

Some of us have lost all faith and no longer dream. And for that I'm sorry. That's the day they begin to sleep through their entire life. It's sad.

Seeing is not believing. No matter what your mom or teacher told you. Believing is seeing, and that's something so emergent they couldn't ever fathom.

### Barry — 2015-02-03T17:34:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-154622>

Right, back to general proceedings...

So fat adaptation is a continuous process, its a constant augmentation of your ability to oxidize fatty acids and upregulate other pathways like ketosis and gluconeogenesis.

The best way to do that, if I haven't already made it clearly obvious, is to train fasted.

Now, particularly in winter months I don't train as much or as long. So I use simple fasting as my fat adaptation .

Two useful ways is during travel and/or work commitments. For example, when flying, I make the event a fast....airport food and airplane food is crap anyway... so I use this time to fast, better my health and pocket. Simples

The other way is by using work. I do some evenings... where I'm out from 5pm until 10pm. What I do is eat breakfast and lunch, then fast until the following day.

Doing this can also be a useful measure.... if your not hungry during the normal eating times or craving food when others are eating.... then these are very good signs that things are working.

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### rgp888 — 2015-02-09T15:37:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-155130>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=154622)

> Right, back to general proceedings...

>
> The best way to do that, if I haven't already made it clearly obvious, is to train fasted.
>
