>
> The red meat paper was atrocious..........the fact that carb adapted endurance athletes drop like flies is an observation no one can explain.......and when I do your butt hurts.

you clearly haven't done your math...

"From 1966 to 2004 it was reported 1,101 cases of sudden death in athletes younger than 35 years, 90% were due to cardiovascular diseases, 50% presented anatomic congenital cardiac diseases and cardiomyopathies, 10% presented early coronary atherosclerotic disease, 40% occurred in athletes younger than 18 years and 33% below 16 years old, the ratio of female/male was 1/9. SCD was reported for almost all sports, the majority involved soccer (30%), basketball (25%) and runners (15%) "

"In the United States from 1980 through 1995, a total of 1318 deaths were attributed to lightning, (average: 82 deaths per year {range: 53-100 deaths}). Of the 1318 persons who died, 1125 (85%) were male, and 896 (68%) were aged 15-44 years. The annual death rate from lightning was highest among persons aged 15-19 years (6 deaths per 10,000,000 population; crude rate: 3 per 10,000,000). The greatest number of deaths attributable to lightning occurred in Florida and Texas (145 and 91, respectively), but New Mexico, Arizona, Arkansas, and Mississippi had the highest rates (10.0, 9.0, 9.0, and 9.0, respectively)."

so "dropping like flies" is a bit of an overstatement... is getting struke by lightening because of eating carbs too ??

next, highest incidence of SCD is in soccer, not endurance

Finally, that triathlete who died, had already suffered a cardiac arrest last year. He was diagnosed with some neurolgical disorder

shit happens...

there are things known and there are things unknown, and in between are the doors of perception

### René Borg — 2015-11-12T13:00:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181761>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181706)
>
> Metastable is a better term. Anti fragile I am not on board with.

So just to try and put that side by side:

\- Metastable: stable provided it is subjected to no more than small disturbances.
\- Antifragile: has more upside than downside from volatility

The difference seems small - metastable seems to be close to Taleb's definition of robust or resilient.

Is your issue with the term that although organic systems thrive on small shocks generally and thus 'get better' (adaptation), over the course of a life-time the end result is always the same: death (i.e. our bodies may improve in some sense from training but over the course of a lifetime it get's weaker no matter what we do - what may change is the rate of reduction of function of certain systems). if interpret that one step further: is your trouble with specialised endurance athletics that although you can gain beneficial adaptations in some systems - some general and some specific - that an overspecialised pursuit extracts a cost in other systems (shortening of telomeres) and you do not think this trade-off is worth it because it may accelerate the velocity towards terminal station?

Of course, in the end we all end up dead

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-12T14:40:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181763>

His motivations are optimized for transformation of others. He does things for himself first and foremost.

As for his term "anti fragile"; it is a total misnomer to me. We have to be fragile because natural harmony with seasons requires destruction and damage with renewal. We have to come apart to so we don't fall apart. So I think my term is far more accurate for biology. His term is more about stamping financial ideas onto a biologic billboard and saying some interesting. I am not interested in being interesting just accurate.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Now lets talk about why I like metastable. A dose is not a dose across the board Rene. Let me explain. In physics or economics the defintion of dose is similar. In biology the definition of dose is quite different. In physical systems there is never any regeneration. The sun burns hydrogen until it comes to equilibrium with the atoms it has left. Once it gets to iron it burn out and change its physical form. Things in physics fall to equilibrium and gain entropy as they do so. In biology we never fall to equilibrium until death. This means in biologic systems there is never a time where entropy can win. That is because biology controls the fall to randomness by making sure there is always a balance between repair and damage. See in financial markets, like physics they act randomly. Nothing in biology is random. It is ordered at the edge of chaos. That is why I favor the definition of metastability because it imparts the idea that we are designed to change every moment based upon the redox changes we face.

Nature invented biological clocks before we invented mechanical ones. It's just that like everything else, we copied nature and did a relatively poor job. The same is true in financial markets with all the mathematics designed to time the market changes. Nature's oscillators are adaptive and pay attention to the environment--ours are rigid. In financial markets they are contrived by market makers like Taleb. Which is why I find him disingenuous and why his term is so bothersome. But synchronization (clocks) are necessary to coordinate the sequence of complex events that are separated in the distances in space. If biological clocks fail, cellular signaling becomes uncoordinated and breaks down. Basic chronobiology. If human clocks fail, civilization can't function except in very small localities. This is not true in physics or markets.

We die a fraction every day and by degrees we’re reborn into different people, older people in the same clothes, with the same scars. There is no perfection in this life, only beautiful versions of brokenness. The key is realizing we need damage to repair. The DC electric current is the key to all repair and it comes from UV/IR light. Blue light is the ultimate destruction tool of nature because of its ability to carry power and to bend easy. This makes failure or damage the pivot foot in change. That is metastability in my view.

Nature's disruption is always dependable, but we cannot control her, only ourselves.

Some degree of failure is the pivot foot of change in all biologic systems.

Failure in nature = is measured by the redox potential of a surface and this determines how we assimilate UV light

More stress from any cause = more ELF-UV light release. (van wijk 101)

redox = Fe redox in cytochromes........

cytochromes exist in mitochondria.

All that glitters is not gold.........If gold rusted, what would iron do for our cells? There is a reason our bodies use iron in their nyanomachine mitochondria and not gold. Gold is of no use to man because it does not break down well under any circumstances so we cannot use it as a change or as a sign of a electrical change/redox beacon.

The irony, is men like Taleb overvalue it because of its color and rarity..........but it is useless to us biologically. Humans are irrational in their values and this is why they value advice on food and exercise over light.

That is metastability 101.

Stressors are only positive conduits of change if we can use them as information and then learn from them. I know 300 million people with cell phones that stress them, and they don't have more information about the stressor from being exposed, because sometimes you don't understand your own stressor.

I roll with the nature we are built with and connecting to everyday.

I'm revealing the layers to an infinite fabric and you reply stating that I should focus on the trivial ??? Sorry Barry I don't gain understanding from running. Never have.

Stressors are information, but not everyone can speak Chinese.
Infinity speaks without ever talking.

### Optimalbound — 2015-11-12T21:56:44+0200

Hi Barry. I'm not sure if this is of any interest, and if it is, it's probably not news to you. But I thought I might post it, anyway.

Below is the figure that the Stein Ørn, who is the trainer for cyclist Alexander Kristoff, drew during an interview when explaining the philosophy behind Kristoff's training regime.

"_The key is to make the body consume the so-called fat stores during a long cycling race lasting six or seven hours before the body starts to consume the glucose stores. These can then be saved for the final sprint. That is why Kristoff's team has worked systematically over several years to increase his fat burning._" (my translation)

### René Borg — 2015-11-13T12:33:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-181891>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=181867)
>
> Devin sent this to me:[https://www.t-nation.com/training/death-of-steady-state-cardio](https://www.t-nation.com/training/death-of-steady-state-cardio)

The author of the article starts off on the wrong track: no one in running, in athletics or involved in performance coaching for endurance sees endurance training as primarily a fat-loss tool - the aritcle goes nowhere from there. Many bodybuilding and fitness coaches tend to fail to grasp this as the article points out, also failing to control for multiple variables and attacking a simplified strawman - lazy lazy. Runners are well aware that it is more important how you live and eat than how much you exercise once you get beyond the people who just drift into the activity (rather than the sport) pushed there by the old fat-loss paradigm (i.e. 'Coca Cola wants us to believe that doing a workout is great and we cna keep sipping the Coke). Running tends to form a great keystone habit in driving the incentive - I am a point in case - I would still sit in the pub drifting from the two default states I had from 15 to 27 - drunk and hung-over, if I hadn't discovered running. Once I discovered that, it opened up all the other doors. It is a crucial part of our evolutionary heritage (the Running Man hypothesis) and one of the sports were we can actually outperform most other land mammals. Does that mean it cannot be done incorrectly ('chronic cardio' as it is studied is done at the wrong intensity by and large) or that it cannot be done to excess (i.e. 'the Achilles choice - do you want to live a short life and be remembered forever or a long one and be only a brief memory' - obsessive commitment to any art - whether running or otherwise has a price). Great progress - for the species - was often achieved by the price paid by individuals overspecialising, they paid the price and we gained at their expense. When someone goes and breaks the 2 hour marathon barrier should we shake our heads and wonder 'what a stupid guy' or should we say 'marvellous, a new frontier in human performance has been broken'. Should we lament that this 'sub-2 hour man' has cut a few years off his life potentially or should we applaud the risk and sacrifice he made to show us what is possible. You could construct this argument for any pursuit - not just running.

Are there more important pursuits in the world than running? Of course, you can find many that will, on the net, increase human thriving more. But you will find far more pursuits that detract from the sum of human well-being.

As an athletics coach I only need to look around at the smiling faces, the sense of community, the deep connection people feel to this primeval motor pattern, to know that running as a sport is a force for good. This doesn't mean we cannot polish off some edges. It also doesn't mean it is the solution and cure to all ills (it's not).

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-18T02:14:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-182232>

And when you consider the implications of what I just said above, how we do studies has a massive impact on what we all believe. I believe light and temperature are the major factors in biology. I said this in CT 6 blog, long ago and got made fun of by people with limited knowledge of these effects. Biochemists have issues accepting this idea because of what they are taught. When The UK book by John Joy and Al Khalili came out you'd think that would get people looking at this closer. It did not in ancestral health. Barry likes Robb, and I hate half truths, so there in lies Barry's irritation with me. I love discomfort. Not every one does.

Now this: So if Cold Thermogenesis is really hormetic as the Wolf says, why is it that this one variable can cause biochemistry to shift in an experiment. Why? Because biochemistry is thermoplastic just like photosynthesis is. See the biochemists out there, with the exception of the guys mentioned in Jim Al Khalili's book, know that surface temperature changes the biochemistry at deeper tissue levels because it is a quantum effect. It turns out mice have lower inflammation and this is capable of altering scientific data, can you imagine when they finally realized the very same mice are also kept in high color temperature light 24/7, yet they are nocturnal mammals? You think that is a problem our paleo superstars planned for? Well I did. And I said so a long time ago in CT 6. The light environment will have a far bigger impact they never account for in any experiments. This was the point I made in the CT 6 blog post years ago. Biochemistry is thermoplastic and anything that alters temperature has fundamental changes on electrons and protons in mitochondria. Now we have proof I was right and that somebody was selling half truths to lemmings. [http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/44486/title/Cold-Mice-Skewing-Data-/](http://www.the-scientist.com/?articles.view/articleNo/44486/title/Cold-Mice-Skewing-Data-/)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-182350>

> [Hansen Kenimer said:](/goto/post?id=182272)
>
> That study is still comparing low-carb to high-carb, both fed before the testing. I mean like comparing fat-burning rates between two groups of keto-adapted athletes...one group fed a low-carb, high-fat something-or-other beforehand and the other performing totally fasted.

Hansen, the LC grp were LCHF for almost 2yrs and had probably already done lots of fasted state training. This is what upregulates fat/keto adaptation.

So the researchers were not measuring/comparing "breakfasts" or non-fasted v fasted. They were comparing the adaptations between the 2 groups built up over a long period.

hope that makes sense

### Jack Kruse — 2015-11-19T19:53:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-182361>

When people talk diet and food they must talk about light and there is my rub with the ancestral leaders. Color = frequency of light. So what type of sun you train under will give you the idea of what you think it best. When you train outdoors you begin to see a world Barry senses. When you train indoors you are low in dopamine in the eye and they eye sends that message to the frontal lobes and you think you need carbs to perform. IT is a light story. Where you train matters deeply. When hacking color of light for any reason at all know this about the color ratio of the light source: Sun has equal and quite balanced ratio’s of blue, green, and red, while Edison bulbs have massive amounts of red, medium amount of green like the sun, and a much smaller amount of blue compared to the sun. LED’s and fluorescents have massive amounts of blue compared to the sun, the same amount of green, and a very tiny amount of red compared to the sun. This alone should make you question every study done under artificial lighting in science.
Not one nutritional study controls for color temperature.........NOT one.

### Barry — 2015-12-03T16:22:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-183169>

enough squabbling ...

Training Update:

so once you have deeply fat adapted, fasted, LC, keto, etc..... the requirement to do it reduces... yet you can still maintain the adaptations

previously, I would train fasted 5-6 times per week.... big mileage... suffering ... 2 meals a day

I now don't need to do this anymore... yet I still have what I need

now I do it on feel... and for convenience

for example... I am CTing a lot now.... sea swims... cold weather. I like doing it early, pre breakfast. Recently I had a pro cyclist with me, Svien Tuft, a legend in the sport and truly primal [http://cyclingmagazine.ca/sections/...ration-are-matters-of-the-mind-and-the-heart/](http://cyclingmagazine.ca/sections/news/for-svein-tuft-recovery-and-tour-de-france-preparation-are-matters-of-the-mind-and-the-heart/)

so we CT... do some movnat after... then drive straight to the mountains and run for 2hrs. Snow on the summit and we ran barefoot. No foods, no water. We finished up about 2pm and then just sat out on the grass and had a beer.

This isn't being studied in the lab. Nor is it being practiced by the self confessed guru's. They might claim things but very few are doing it.

Stressors are information. I do think a period of unmeasured deep stress is needed to really see the woods from the trees. After that, a punch is just a punch, a kick just a kick

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### Paul Fendler — 2015-12-30T17:26:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-185118>

Barry...excellent article. Your perspective comes through crystal clear. There's nothing like firsthand experience to turn knowledge into wisdom.

Ideally...someone dispensing advice on human performance has not only been a participant...they have also worked with many, many others...either as a coach or advisor or whatever...and has closely observed the experiences of many others. You need a large data base of experiences. That combined with a knowledge of the science involved gives your advice usefulness. But even with all that...you need an open mind...a willingness to dump everything you ever knew.

I'm a big fan of both you and Jack. Phil Maffetone was mentioned a while back. He was my coach and primary health care practitioner back in the 80's. In 1989, I ran a 100 miler on 100 certified 1 mile loops on virtually nothing but water in 14:11 (7:04, 7:07) so I connect with your experiences with running long distances with very little carbohydrate.

The thing about "skin in the game"...it has to be clear what "game" is being talked about. If it's about athletic performance in long-distance endurance events, you have a lot of skin in the game. If it's about how an 85 year old with arrythmia rehabs after a hip operation, you don't. Well, at least I don't think you do. So often the problem is "experts" wander into areas where they really shouldn't be acting like experts....if that makes sense.

I feel if someone wanders into your area and starts making claims that do not jive with your experience, it might be best to simply communicate to them just that. Tell them what they are saying is not jiving...and discuss why that might be. There's no need to let it bother you.

Thank you kindly for all your wonderful contributions.

Likewise .. In terms of brain surgery, I have zero skin in the game. Hence I do not declare I know anything about it.

In fact, this is the only blog where I share my "wisdom"... And I've been writing here for over 4yrs

We need more like you here, so do come along....

Barry, thanks very much for the encouragement.

I had a couple of decent ultras way back when but I hadn't a clue how to recover from them so I drifted away. You are a million miles ahead of me in both your accomplishments and your understanding of things. I did do thousands of miles of fasted state training for several decades which yielded consistently good results for a long time. It wasn't until fairly recently...when my mother had a serious car accident followed by a slew of health problems and emergencies...and my brother imploded with a severe case of OCD...that my stress levels went though the roof, my performance declined and I began to notice compromised health. It has lead me here...not only to help myself...but also to find ways to help my mom and brother and others close to me.

I just watched the video about Wim Hof. Mind-boggling to say the least. I know you are using CT...but are you using any of the breathing techniques he teaches?

### Barry — 2016-02-09T17:20:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-188622>

Training Update:

I see nothing but addition these days in the fitness world. I recently wrote a 30 page report for a client, competitive runner, detailing everything from fat adaptation to mitochondrial biogensis to circadium rhythm to PVN.... I got one question back... "whats the best protein shake to use"...

Its the bullshit society we live in today where everything to improve our health and fitness is about what we can add.... what we can add... that costs money.... that provides comfort.. that removes any effort... that is a one stop magic bullet

I see this everywhere... the quantlet is just another example of this.... I've already discussed this with Jack

Rather... when you look deeper... much deeper... and you aren't afraid of discomfort.. and want to put in effort... and realise that time and patience are required... you realise that substraction brings gains.. it adds to your life

so now, for example... I don't use protein shakes, or D-ribose or exogenous ketones etc etc.... I eat real food, I get cold, I train fasted, I suffer and I wait...

In fact, as each day passes... I am removing way more from my life yet I am gaining much more.

People are blinded to this today.... and I personally think its one of the main reasons for failure.

We're creating fragility.

another rant over.....

### Barry — 2016-02-23T19:06:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-189661>

Similar to my "rant" , what happens when we try to add more

this from Nassim Taleb....

"_When people get rich, they lose control of their preferences, substituting constructed preferences to their own, triggering their own misery. And these are the preferences of those who want to sell them something (a skin-in-the-game problem as their choices are dictated by others who have something to gain, and no side effects, from the sale).

I've mentioned here the following anecdote. I once had dinner in a Michelin-starred restaurant with a fellow who insisted on eating there instead of my selection of a casual Greek place. People in the restaurant were of the very constipated style, that kind of atmosphere. Dinner consisted in a succession of complicated small things. It felt like work. I left the place starving. Now if I had a choice I would have had some time-tested recipe (say a pizza with fresh ingredients, or a juicy hamburger) in a lively place --for a tenth of the price. But because the fellow could afford the expensive restaurant, we ended up the victims of some complicated experiments by a chef judged by some Michelin bureaucrat (one that would fail the Lindy effect, instead of eating some minute local variation around a progress through Sicilian grandmothers).

(Footnote: Hamburgers are tastier than filet mignon, because of the higher fat content, but people have been convinced that the latter is better because it is more expensive to produce).

The same with real estate. Most people, I am convinced, are happier in close quarters, in a real barrio-style neighborhood, where they can feel human warmth, but when they have big bucks they end up pressured to move into a humongous impersonal and silent mansions, far away from the neighbors. Not counting the fact that their house will be professionally managed like a corporation.

I am saying this because, with the passage of time, I am more and more convinced that very few people understand their own choices, and end up being manipulated by those who want to sell them something. Looking at Saudi Arabia which should progressively revert to the pre-oil level of poverty, I wonder if taking away some things from them will make them better off."_

> Ninja - eating breakfast is only important if LR or have other hormonal imbalances. In terms of post training, thats where I consume my daily carbs and varying it depending on training is important e.g.
>
>
>
> Long Endurance Session - 3-5hr run/cycle
>
>
>
> Immediately after: ~50g CHO, fruit/honey with whey protein (in the summer of course ;-)
>
>
>
> HIIT session:
>
>
>
> Immediately after: protein only
>
>
>
> Important to note: you need to be keto adapted for this to work. Possibly also when adapting (remember it takes 24-36month to full adapt), its good to apply a cyclical ketogenic approach. Lyle Mcdonald has a good book on this.
>
>
>
> With all recommendations here, its important to put things in context. I have been keto adapting and doing big fasted state training for 3 years now. Its been a gradual process. >
>
>
> hope that helps

>
>
> astrocytes act as support cells for neurons. They store glycogen and supply glucose to the brain cells. Frequent endurance exercise allows these astrocytes to supercompensate glycogen. This supercompensation occurs after exercise when fed carbs ! This increased fuel reserve in the cortex and hippocampus improves cognitive function.
>
>
>
> Tie this in with BDNF and brain mitochondrial biogensis (2 other adaptations caused by cardio exercise) and endurance exercise with some carbs eaten afterwards looks pretty good for the brain !

### BrainWhisperer — 2016-03-25T20:08:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-191512>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=73178)
>
> Training Update:
>
> The recommendations of feeding during exercise never really sat with me well. If you look at endurance sports such as triathlon, ironman, cycling, running etc... the sports drink companies have created what can only be described as a fallacy in terms of what the body needs to fuel. Your told by them to consume sugars, from 60g per hour to 1g/Kg BW per hour etc, in order to prevent fatigue and hitting the wall. Complete nonsense if you look into the actual energy pathways of ATP production and how they actually work
>
> Very simply, even a "carb adapted" athlete, who is going for a 1-2hr run, even a marathon, or a 2-3hr cycle... can have up to 400-500g of glycogen stored in the muscle and liver. This provides up to 2000kcal. An 1hr medium paced run might use up 500 calories (all depending on several factors of course), a 3hr cycle might use 1200-1500kcal... the point being, even a person, completely unable to oxidise fat (and even carb adapted athletes can), would still not use all of their carbohydrate reserves while exercising.
>
> Now, if you look at the other ATP pathways, there are many. So many experts out there think that the only substrate we have for exercise as a fuel is Glucose. Of course, everyone knows that fatty acids can be oxidised to produce ATP. With lots of advantages if this is done properly in terms of reduced ROS and redox coupling. Not to mention the vast reserve of fatty acids in terms of kcals compared to CHO. But it doesn't stop there. We can produce ketones. We have gluconeogensis, whereby we can produce glucose from amino acids and glycerol. Then we can Cori-cyle to recycle lactic and regenerate glucose, and the same with the Alanine-glucose cycle. We then have electrons and mitochondrial efficiency, but I won't get into that for now. The bottom line is this, we have several ways of acquiring glucose (other than consuming it) and we have several other substrates that can be used to produce ATP , which not only are more abundant, but are actually metabolically superior in terms of cytochrome usage.
>
> But let me just get back to the point I want to make. Because of what I have just described above, and because I know I am very adapted to using these various pathways, I know find it hard to see the point in consuming anything during long exercise. I can do 4-5hr mountain runs, with just water. I recently did a 7hr bike ride through the Tuscany valley on just water. During racing, if it is <5-6hrs in duration, I am actually afraid to eat as I feel that it would disrupt the smooth running of my ATP pathways. There are occasions where I hit a lot, or a rough patch, but I know this is not due to energy depletion. If you have 10Kg of fat to burn, thats 90,000kcal... thats going to last a long time, days, not just hours. So I have worked out what this low patch is now. Its a brain low, simply glucose/ketone deprivation. Glucose is stored in the brain as glycogen, in astrocytes, but an race or event where you are exercising for hours on end is going to deplete these stores. Yes ketones can also be used for brain fuel as we know, but during exercise, glucose is simply quicker in terms of delivery.
>
> So now, when I'm training, I don't eat to fuel the muscle, I eat to fuel the brain. My muscle has plenty of fuel to play with. My brain on the other hand cannot keep up with the demand (remember, 20% of overall EE) and ketones cannot be generated by the liver and supplied rapdily enough. So thats where I think simple sugars actually come in to play in terms of exercise fuelling. They're needed to fuel the brain, not the muscle, as long as you can use all the other reserves ;-)

Bonking ("hitting the wall") is in the brain.

### Barry — 2016-04-11T13:21:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-192496>

Simple, but not Simpler.

How do you know when you are Fat Adapted?

A question I get asked all the time. The “science only” response is to get you RER (Respiratory Exchange Ratio) or RQ (Respiratory Quotient) tested which can be done in any sports physiology lab. Pretty straightforward test and it measures the rate at which you burn fat and glucose as the exercise intensity increases. However, this is done in the lab. It measures 2 variables, oxygen input and carbon dioxide output. Now I’m not going to get into it here, but there is a lot more to this whole fat adaptation thing than just that.

So, based on 6 years of what I have studied and tested (in the field), I have come up with a few practical ways which you can do yourself that will “arguably” tell you a lot more about what’s going on inside your cells. Here we go:

1\. You are not hungry in the morning

If you wake up starving, there are possibly two main things going wrong. One is that you had a bad sleep thereby messing with your circadian rhythm. This is your own internal biological clock that looks after the timing of everything. It can do this by regulating hormone release. If you sleep poorly, melatonin is not released, cortisol is elevated and prolactin is suppressed. This combination increases appetite or offsets your normal eating patterns.

The other thing that causes hunger in the morning is hypoglycaemia whereby your blood sugar levels are low. This can happen if you have some problems with insulin and tolerance of carbohydrates. In other words, you are insulin resistant and eat a high carb diet.

So sleep and being fat adapted…. A) Controls you appetite hormones and B) your blood sugar levels

2\. You can train “fasted”

Following on from No. 1, if you do not feel hungry, the next self test is to do whatever your typical training session is without breakfast. Now I know this can be anything from a 30min yoga session to a 6hr hilly bike ride. So I can’t talk specifically. All I will say is this:

If you can do whatever one of your tougher/longer training sessions is in the morning, without breakfast, and do it as you would have normally, then it’s a pretty good sign you are on the right path.

Example: morning 60-90min steady pace run or 2-3hr cycle or 60min swim sets

No breakfast, no eating during, no hunger during, normal or better performance = on the right path to fat adaptation

Next step up… is if you can do your longest/hardest session of the week, without breakfast.

Example: 4-5hr bike ride or 3-4hr mountain run

No breakfast, minimal eating during, water only, no hunger during, normal or better performance = pretty sure sign you are fat adapted and upregulating your fat adaptation.

3\. No need for snacks

If you are hungry between meals, you are likely to have drops in blood sugar levels. This is caused by insulin insensitivity and/or a high carbohydrate diet. Hunger between meals can also simply be a habitual thing… its 11 o’clock in work and everyone is drinking tea/coffee and eating biscuits. So changing the habit is the first step. However, not feeling the need to eat and simply not being hungry between meals, means that your blood sugar levels are steady and insulin is not misbehaving. Becoming fat adapted allows this.

Example: you eat some meat + avocado salad for lunch at 1pm. You do not eat again until 7pm and feel no hunger in between.

4\. You can intermittent fast without chewing your arm off

So intermittent fasting is becoming a trend. Like most things today in the diet and fitness industry... they try to package it and then sell it. You’ve got 5:2, 16/8 and various other “protocols”. Here’s all I’ll say:

If you have dinner at say 8pm. Get up the next morning and not feel any hunger. Go to work, school or whatever and not feel hungry during the morning. Get to lunch time without counting down the clock and then eat at an unplanned time. Eat a satisfying meal… not a horse… just a decent meal… and not slosh back half a loaf of bread and 3 slices of cake. Then and only then… can you consider you fast to be beneficial and for it to indicate that you’re fat burning mechanisms are intact. You should be comfortably able to skip breakfast and also lunch…. And just eat dinner without any major effort. This would be a much better sign of fat adaptation.

5\. You are not hungry after training

I’ve saved the best till last as its 1. The hardest and 2. IMO, the best indicator.

So you get up and train. Instead of coming back and necking a bowl of cereal, 2 flap jacks and a protein smoothie… you eat nothing. You may have to drive somewhere or you are just showering and distracted with other stuff at home... whatever… but you feel no major hunger and you are not chewing your leg off since you already chewed your arm off during an intermittent fast.

Now I don’t mean a 30min workout in the gym or a yoga class. I mean something where you had to push it and expend a good deal of energy. A long run or bike or hard swim set…. Or a weights/cardio long session in the gym. Something where you out for a while and burnt a decent bit of fuel.

Example: wake up, no breakfast, or just some eggs

Train 9-11am

Drive home 30mins. Shower/change 30mins.

Cook/Eat: 12.30 - 1pm

If at the end of that session you do not feel ravenous or feel no rush to eat… then this is a major sign that you have increased the rate at which you can burn fat as a fuel. Why? As it means you have increased the rate at which you can oxidise fatty acids and convert them to ATP. It means you have increased the storage depot of fatty acids in the muscle called IMTG’s (intramuscular triglycerides). It also possibly means you have increased gluconeogenesis, which is your own glucose manufacturing process. It may also mean you are producing ketones as an addition fuel source. In addition, you may have lowered your overall inflammatory response to the exercise.

All this occurs when you are highly fat adapted.

"In order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### Barry — 2016-04-26T13:53:38+0300

So over 4yrs and 1,000 posts later... what have I learned ?

"i love not man the less, but nature more" Lord Byron

In terms of everything I have discussed here, and almost everything Jack has discussed.... its all about connecting to nature

However, you first have to simplify the complexity. Then keep things simple, but not simpler.

When you do this, you realise that its easy, doesn't cost anything, requires effort, time, patience and suffering..... and goes against modern convention.

As the modern way of doing things is additive, monetary, materialistic, fast, convenient , ordered, fragile/robust/safe, comfortable ... and the big one, disconnected from nature.

The one click of the button world now then causes us to drown in information but starve of wisdom...

So do more instead of talking more, wish for the wind if you want to energize the fire and put your skin in the game...

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate properly with the oscillations in the mitochondrial matrix"

Barry and Jack

### Barry — 2016-06-09T20:55:43+0300

I ran for my longest yet in the fasted state.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-06-09T23:25:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196268>

have you noticed any downsides?

### Barry — 2016-06-10T17:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196313>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=196268)
>
> have you noticed any downsides?

Yes, I have been meaning to write something about this for a while

These days, we can all claim the benefits and call ourselves superhuman without ever really talking about downsides

So I've been doing this thing for over 6yrs now. It got as advanced as living off 2 meals a day for almost 2yrs..... with big training and racing included

Now... things are a bit different. Once the cat is skinned, you don't need to keep skinning it.

The adaptations that have taken place over the last 6yrs are in place... they are not going away... the machinery has been built and the systems have been installed.

During this process, you do have to suffer...... for me that meant disrupted sleep, elevated resting HR, cortisol skewed, adrenal stress.... where in some cases I blew the fuse and was out of action for 3-4 weeks

Over recent months.... i have not been training fasted as much, I am not intermittant fasting as much and I am not counting my carbs

Like I mentioned, I know feel like doing these things too regularly is now counter productive ..... the shoes have been polished.... further polishing is just going to wear the leather down

However, I still like to check in and say hello to the adaptations

So while travelling..... i just fast.... might mean last meal at lunch... travel... then not eat until breakfast or lunch the next day. I can do this without any problem.. so I know things are working

Then I still train fasted now and again... like this recent time.... and push the boat out..... and again, everything works

The rest of the time... I'm eating breakfasts, I'm not skipping meals and I'm not afraid to eat a potato

Like I've said many times....... whats needed for performance is not always healthy.... doing 7hrs runs is not healthy.... doing it with AMPK on fire is not healthy either but it helps performance

What mitigates the damage is the 3 legged stool and laws of nature. I've noticed how I can run long fasted/recover better when I have several hours of grounding daily, full spectrum sunlight, CT every other day.... some fish, mountain water, with close friends in a confident state.... sound familiar ?

### Paul Fendler — 2016-06-10T19:28:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196323>

Barry...thanks very much for sharing some of the downs...as well as the ups. It helps put things in perspective and highlights the experimental...learn by doing...nature of training.

The trade-off between health and performance is an ongoing battle...and one which I'm am starting to understand late in life.

If you don't mind...a while back in this thread you said it was important what you did after CT. I missed it and didn't see this until yesterday. Would you be kind of to describe what you meant by that?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-06-10T19:55:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196325>

In colder temperatures ... the body resorts to more active mechanisms of heat production such as non-shivering thermogenesis which occurs primarily in the brown adipose tissue (BAT). Norepinephrine initiates triglyceride breakdown in the brown adipocytes, leading to the release of fatty acids which activate the UCP1 enzyme leading to increased heat production. Where have I heard that before? My blog years ago. [http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0098076](http://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0098076)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-06-10T19:57:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196326>

sounds familiar Barry.

### sfast — 2016-06-12T21:07:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196454>

Glad to hear an update.

I have similar experience. I did a three year experiment with cyclical keto and pretty intense intervall and crossfit-type training. After that it seems that my adaptions persist. I experimented with higher carb but my performance seems to be mostly independent from exogenous carbs.

Sample day (saturday):

No breakfast.

5h Hiking (no drinking)
1h Break
30min Bike Ride (fairly fast)
1,5h Sprints and Floreio ("flowy" type of bodyweight training)
House work (I hate it so I hurried)
20min Bike Ride (fast! to my girlfriend)

After 25h fast --> BIIIIGG T-Bone Steak with Salat and a handful strawberries.

Normal performance and energy, no hunger (but it came within the first slice of fatty meat in the evening)

### Barry — 2016-06-16T01:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-196653>

If there is something in nature that you can't understand , chances are it works in a far more sophisticated way than you thought . The more I try to figure out human performance , the more I realize it is far more sophisticated and fat more complex than we think.

Definitely the quantum affect Jack is teasing out will certainly expand our knowledge and understanding .
However , like I've said before , doing teaches you more than talking . Today we do too much talking .

The birds don't know the mechanics behind their ability to fly. They just fly . Nor do they know the quantum physics behind their long range migrations . They just go .

Similarly , I don't really know how I can run 200km non stop with minimal fueling , in a fasted state , and win . I'm just doing it .

In the terms of understanding the body and in particular, in elite sport, here’s my own simple formula:

Understanding Human Performance = Theory + Practice+ Skin in the Game

Understanding Elite Human Performance = Theory + Practice + Skin in the Game + Thick Skin in the Game

There is where, in my opinion, the whole debate of athletes nutrition and whether Carbs or Fats (and it’s never that clear cut) are better, has gone wrong.

The academics, for the large part, have zero skin in the game. They are too busy in the lab.

The guru’s, , for the large part, have some skin in the game, but not thick. They are too busy counting their “likes” and responding to tweets.

The top athletes , using a lot of the “unproven” strategies, with lots of thick skin in the game, are too busy winning. Plus they don’t know how or why it’s working for them, they’re just doing it.

The odd few like me, with thick skin in the game while also attempting to be a type of guru, are too busy repairing their skin and then putting more in the game.

That is all...

### Barry — 2016-07-02T00:49:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-197721>

"Train low, Compete low" aka Skin in the Game

Running 17hrs on almost nothing

If there is something in nature you don’t understand, chances are it works in a far more sophisticated way than we thought.

This sums up us Homosapiens.

Yet today, we think we know how everything works, we do the studies, we prove this that and the other.

So I’ve stopped trying to claim I know how everything works, I don’t. Instead I just do…. As I have realised that those who talk just talk and those that do, learn.

Picture this… a small town in the north of Italy, surrounded by rocky peaks. Around which, is an ultra mountain marathon course, 119km long with 6,500m of climbing. The winner completes it in about 13hrs with the last person over the line in 30hrs.

You would think this requires a huge amount of “fuelling” and a constant intake of carbohydrates to keep you going. That’s what all the studies say, so we must do it.

Here’s what I did:

The days leading up to it involved my flying over to Italy, then driving north up to the race location. I use travelling as a chance to fast. Airport food is crap and aeroplane food is worse. So I use what I know helps me i.e. not eating. This allows me to avoid poor quality food while also allowing me to push the fat adaptation switches and even autophagy switches. This time, ate at 1pm… then travelled and didn’t eat again until the next day, after a run, at midday. So almost 24hrs of fasting and I felt good.

An early departure the day before the race meant no breakfast and a 4hr drive. So ate just lunch and dinner that day. Just two meals the day before a 119km race when we are supposed to be “loading”. The following day was race day, and with an 11pm start, this meant the whole day was for “fuelling”.

Here’s what I had

Breakfast: Eggs and pancetta

Lunch: Sausage and polenta with veg

Dinner: mackerel salad, then prosciutto and cheese.

No snacks. No juices, no fruits, no packets of biscuits, no pasta and no rice. In fact, very little carbohydrates, the so c called fuel we all need to exercise.

11pm came around and I ran. I ran for almost 4hrs without even thinking about food. I skipped the first checkpoint and ran through as I had enough water.

For the next 13hrs, I ran, hiked up and ran down some of the biggest mountains in Italy. I never felt really hungry. I was stopping at checkpoints mainly for water and a swig of electrolytes. I ate very little, just a few pieces of cheese and little samples of crostata.

I had in my backpack – 1 x packet of salted cashews, 2 x small bounty bars and 1 x banana and 1 x little pouch of honey.

I finished the race with everything untouched except the banana and 1 x small bounty bar.

I arrived in 79th position out of 1650 starters.

Okay, I didn’t win, but I felt good and finished almost in the top 5% of the field. I also wasn’t “racing” as it was my first time and also a reunion with Italian friends, so the mindset was more about enjoyment.

Getting back to the figures

Roughly, I consumed:

\- 100g CHO

\- ~ 500-700Kcal

Roughly, I expended:

\- 8,500Kcal – 11,500

So I ran for 17hrs, burning 8,000-10,00Kcal, but consumed only ~600kcal. I didn’t do this to make a point. I simply didn’t feel hungry and only consumed what I “felt” I needed. How did I do that?

Fat Adaptation

Very simply, and what I have been researching myself and practising for years, is increasing the body’s ability to burn fat during exercise. I’ve written about this extensively before.

I’ll just outline the simple numbers for now

10% Body Fat and 70Kg = 7,000g Fat = 69,000kcal

If you have 69,000kcal to burn, that’s more than enough for most ultra endurance events.

The myth of fuelling during:

I used to follow this 60-90g/CHO for competition. Its baloney. Before I even get into the science, I bet anyone reading this that has competed in any endurance race, and performed well, probably has been nowhere near that amount.

If you are fat adapted, and you are competing in an event like ironman, marathon, ultrarunning, cycling …. Then you can provide the ATP (energy) required through the oxidation of fatty acids in the mitochondria.

But you’ll say “but during the Tour de France, they take gels and even cans of coke”

Okay, there is one big reason that has been overlooked as to why they do this:

The Central Governor

The brain fuels primarily on glucose. During times of glucose scarcity or fasting, the liver can produce another fuel source for the brain called Ketones.

During a race, particularly a stressful race like the TdF, the brain is going to burn glucose quickly. If the other fuel source Ketones cannot be manufactured/supplied to the brain quickly enough, the brain is going into a fuel deficit.

What happens when the command centre goes low on fuel? It sends out signals to the rest of the body that it needs to conserve energy and slow down. Or sometimes, bonk. So the key during races is to prevent these fuel deficits and the easiest/most practical way to do this, is to eat something sugary.

A swig of coke, half a gel, a handful of sweets…. That’s all most of these pro cyclists or Ironman consume every now and again. You’ll see a cyclist in the last hour, before a climb, tear at a gel, suck down half and throw the rest away. He rides hard for 1hr and wins the race. He consumed 15g of carbohydrate from the gel. Was it these 15g that fuelled the muscle to provide the energy he needed??? 60mins at 3g/min CHO.. That’s 180g of CHO needed, apparently. I’ll let you decide.

Hence, during exercise, we do not need to fuel the muscle, we need to simply fuel the brain.

The other aspect of simple sugars for the brain during exercise is the stimulation of reward or pleasure centres. Studies using carbohydrate drinks as a mouth rinse have shown improved performance. What’s happening here is that there are receptors in the mouth (and gut) for carbohydrates that trigger the pleasure/reward centres in the brain. This helps reduce perception of perceived exertion and gives you the “feel good” factor which is desperately needed during times of racing and suffering during an event.

Fear Factor

There is one other major reason why people think they need to constantly stuff themselves with foods/drink during races. It’s called fear. People are afraid. Its natural. You are going out to do something that requires a lot of courage and self belief. It could be your first marathon or ironman or the longest ultramarathon you’ve ever done. Over the years, you’ve been bombasted with the need to fuel, the need to consume carbohydrates every hour or you die, the need to drink constantly, the need to take x amount of sodium and potassium.

Fear leads to forced protection. It leads to panic buying because you are afraid of the consequences. This is generally how modern society works too…. Life insurance, sun cream, pensions, smartphone upgrades, breakfasts, orange juice, probiotics, statins,parking sensors, gym memberships and so on. You are scared into thinking you need all these things, so you buy them.

In running terms… sports drinks, gels, electrolytes, powders, compression shorts, tops, socks, ankle protectors, GPS watches, running poles and special supported running shoes. I don’t use any of these things because I am not afraid. I’m not afraid because I understand. It’s not because I’m trying to be the tough guy. I just understand how things work and what I need and what suits me best.

I understand, through 8yrs of research and competing, that my body does not need sports drinks, gels and bars in order to fuel itself. I have programmed my fat burning engine and I know what my central governor needs and when.

This is why, in my opinion, why anyone lining up for an endurance event is loaded with all these drinks and foods. Mainly through fear. Fear that is delivered by the very companies that supply them.

Optimal Performance

I don’t think I performed to my best during this race. It wasn’t because I didn’t eat much. It was other factors such as mindset. I felt good, ran strong towards the end and didn’t even feel empty at the finish line.

Recap – How to run 17hrs on almost nothing

\- Become fat adapted

\- Ignore the Fear

\- Control the central governor

Last thing I will say is that I think there are other things going on that allow us to produce energy. Potentially free energy. But that’s something that works in a far more sophisticated way than we can understand.

Cites

**Can Carbohydrate Mouth Rinse Improve Performance during Exercise? A Systematic Review**

Thays di Ataide e Silva et al, 2014

**Is fatigue all in your head? A critical review of the central governor model**
JP Weir et al, 2006.

**Fatigue is a Brain-Derived Emotion that Regulates the Exercise Behaviour to Ensure the Protection of Whole Body Homeostasis**
Timothy Noakes, 2012.

Training Update

I now find, through my own practice, that food is down the priority list. Don't get me wrong, I still follow my principles of fasting, ketotic, LCHF, paleoeque with an epi. But I'm not rigid and I allow changes. Its when these changes happen , that it reinforces how its not all about "the food"

example:

night before: eating out , food not great, some poor meat and soft vegetables. And a beer.

Next Day: no breakfast, 3hr mountain run. Felt great.

This has happened numerous times now. Once the dots are connected, the food is only the glossing. It does take a very long time for the dots to connect though. And the laws of nature need to be intertwined.

What happens today is people go food first. And stop at that when it doesn't work. This is the fat/keto adaptation debacle at the moment. I could actually coach an athlete to be fat/keto adapted, without them really being strict with their foods. I am all about good food of course . But, its actually not essential.

The truth is self evident, collective fear stimulates herd instinct, and tends to produce ferocity towards those who are not regarded as part of the herd

### Barry — 2017-02-04T20:07:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-207948>

New part of the Fasted State Training Adaptation quilt

Found a new beer for Jack, called Imperial Entanglement IPA, which maybe we’ll drink together some day

But did you know that hops contain alpha acids called Humulones. During the brewing process, humulone degrades to cis and trans isohumulones. These iso-alpha acids survive the boiling process but many oxidized derivitives are formed. What this causes is a a massive increase in the proton-motive force. This is what drives our most important nanomachine, ATP synthase. The increase in the proton motive forces improves the chemiosmotic coupling. Isohumulones thus increase the electrical potential of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Electrons are shuttled from complex I, III and IV and this current powers the extrusion of protons (H+) through the three respiratory complexes. However, the cis from of the isohumulone from beer with a high hop content, provides more readily available H+ protons . Thus as each pair of electrons passes down the respiratory chain, 12 protons instead of 10 are pumped at complex I, III and IV. The flux of protons back through the ATP synthase drives the synthesis of ATP from ADP and phosphate.

Hence, why I drink beer, it gives you more energy !!

And that my friends, is the illusion of knowledge ;-)

### Barry — 2017-02-17T21:13:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-208787>

Something has been bothering me for the past few years and I could never really put my finger on it. I read a lot, study a lot and follow a lot of “guru’s” and academics. There are lots of very intelligent people out there today and their information is instantly accessible. The paradox of choice perhaps…. Which we have perhaps too much of these days plus its instantly accessible, maybe it’s best therefore to create your own fishbowl.

But back to my bother. If you dig a bit deeper, the picture looks different. Let’s take a quick example, very quick… so butter has more calories and fats than sunflower oil. Without reading further, based on that info, you choose sunflower spread over butter. Do a bit more reading and you see that butter is mainly saturated fat with very little polyunsaturates. Whereas sunflower is more poly, less saturated. Look a bit further, and you find that the polyunsaturated fats are easily oxidisable, creating free radicals which are harmful to the body. Hence, butter is better for you.

**Bias**

So you can do this with almost every nutrition and health debate. Generally, I am surmising here, but generally, most of the conventional nutrition, health and medicine recommendations are based on shallow evidence, not deep. Not only that, but the evidence has almost every type of bias attached to it from commercial bias to statistical bias to conformational bias.

Then when something is common, it is usually wrong.

So what are we to do ? What do we believe? How do we decide ?

So the scholars will tell you to look for peer-reviewed studies and double blind placebo controlled trials and intervention studies etc etc. This brings us back to the two types of knowledge.

The academic knowledge versus the innate, unexplainable knowledge.

There is one big difference between the two. One massive difference in fact.

When you want to find out something, particularly in the health,sport,and fitness area… there are many variables, many conditions and many unknowns. Therefore, it is generally very difficult to control for and replicate this in the lab. Hence why almost every study on humans, which is looking into health and performance, is not only linked to some bias, but is not 100% reality for your particular context. How could it be. You could be black or white, tall or short, live north equator or south, drink from a well or tap, watch TV or play the piano…. I could go on for pages. The point is, we are extremely sensitive beings, and we react to and respond to almost everything. Even a thought can change our DNA. So how the hell are we supposed to really accept the outcomes of studies on a group of people that are not you ??

Now that that I’ve pissed off the academics and scientists, I’ll move on quickly. But I’ll first try to get them back on board. I love science and I love information. I picked up a book when I was 12 called “The Biology of the Cell”. It was my mothers, she’s a pharmacist. So I started reading it and started learning about cytoplasm and what not. I hadn’t even started secondary school. And that hasn’t stopped. I still read books, papers, journals almost every day. But like I said, I feel bothered now by something.

I thought it was the fact that we are hearing more and more about the bias attached to so many of today’s “peer reviewed” scientific studies. We have professors being paid by Coca-Cola for god’s sake.

**Reductionism**

Then I thought it was because of reductionism. This is where investigations or studies are done in a way to isolate what they are examining and omit the complexity of interactions. A little example. – They want to study the effect of a high carb diet vs a high fat diet on performance. So they get 2 groups of athletes, feed one group a load of carbs, the other a load of fat. Then put them in the lab, get them to exercise and do some tests. Results come out – high carb athletes performed better than high fat athletes. Based on these findings, text books are written, coaches dish out the advice, and food companies make tonnes of special carbohydrate sports foods. Sound familiar ? But look at what they have just reduced… the body’s ability to burn fat is influenced my many things e.g. duration of eating this way, frequency of eating, type of exercise, source of the food, season, sleep, stress, the way you tie your shoelace. A huge amount of things dictate this outcome and that’s not even considering the complexity then of the interactions. Yet, the study showed so we all must follow.

Or should we ? Well no… simply on that premise, you should be saying to yourself that the study is not looking at the big picture. It’s not based on your circumstances so you essentially take it with a big pinch of salt. In a lot of cases, you should probably discard it entirely.

But again, how do you make your mind up on whets right ?

Well this is where the information age and online everything comes into play. You can now read, listen, watch others interpretation and opinion of the research. We have now truck loads of books on diet, low carb, paleo this and that. We have various “how to” books on everything from weight training to running marathons. Then we have blogs by self confessed gurus with podcasts, webinars and forums.

This then leads to another area. They may or may not be academics. They are also maybe not reducing everything. They can also be very intelligent individuals. So I’ve learnt a lot myself from these types of people. And in the midst of all this, over years, through my own endeavours both on the field and off…. I was getting bothered. It’s only recently that I’ve actually realised what it is that’s been bothering me.

**Skin in the Game**

I’ve just been reading a book called AntiFragile by Nassim Taleb. I call it my bible now. Many of my ways of thinking are exactly what this philosophy stands for.

And it was while reading a chapter of the book that highlighted what was bothering me:

“In the old days, privilege came with obligations … You want to be a feudal lord – you will be the first to die. You want war? First in battle. Caesar, Alexander, and Hannibal were on the battle field – the last, according to Livy, was first in, last-out of combat zones. They were in it, they believed in it.”

They were in it, they believed in it….. This made me think. Today we have a lot of knowledge and opinion flying around. It is the information and technology age. So now the intellectual is a lot more powerful and dangerous than before. The “knowledge world” – as quoted by Taleb – causes separation of knowing and doing (within the same person) and leads to the fragility of society.

What does this mean ? I let Taleb explain here with another extract:

“I am stating here that I find it profoundly unethical to talk without doing, without exposure to harm, without having one’s skin in the game, without having something at risk.”

What struck me here was what was bothering me all along…. Skin in the Game.

This means putting your money where your mouth is. It means sticking your neck on the line. It means learning from doing by taking some risk.

And this is where the problem lies today, in my opinion, when it comes to all the latest in fitness and performance. We have far too many talkers. We have far too many delusions of grandeur. You can make yourself look great with a selfie and you can get 1.000’s of followers on facebook. You can even be a very learned academic or expert in whatever field, write very scientific articles , talk very geeky etc etc…. but lack one very very important thing, Skin in the Game.

Because, when it comes to understanding the body, particularly from an exercise and performance point of view…… we still don’t know exactly how it works. Therefore, what we have to do is to try and understand the inexplicable. The best way of actually doing this is by experiencing it, feeling it, suffering it… this is done by putting your own skin in the game.

So we have this big “Carbs vs Fat” debate…. Apart from the bias associated with many of the studies not to mention the reductionism , there is a huge lack of skin in the game also.

You have guru’s and academics looking at the “evidence” and theory that claim high fat diets don’t work for performance or that if you don’t eat 8-10g/kg of carbs a day during “intense long” training… then you will die.

So almost 5yrs ago now, when I was really getting the grasp of the whole fat adaptation thing… I was really putting it to test in the field. I travelled to the Lake District in the UK for a training weekend with some fellow ultrarunners (other lads with complete skin in the game).

Friday was travel and I skipped breakfast and just ate lunch and dinner. Saturday was no breakfast, just coffee, then a 10hr mountain run. It was a course recce. I was with the course record holder and two other international athletes. We ran hard. 10hrs. Minimal eating during.

We arrived back in our place that evening and cooked a meal together. 1 meal.

The next day, Sunday, we got up and I chose again, to have no breakfast. We ran for 6hrs this time, over mountains in the Lake District.

So a 10hr run, and a 6hr run, back to back on low carb, no breakfasts.

A few years later, I was living in Italy. I was working with BMC pro cycling team and I went out with 4 of the riders one day. I was riding a few times a week with local pro riders based there at the time. So usual arrangement was meet at the café in the piazza for 10… drink some espresso’s, then head off. I again arrived having eaten nothing for breakfast before, with a steak and some veg the previous night. . Drank a couple of espressos, then headed off with the pro’s. We did 3hrs pretty fast, averaging 35-40km/hr. I kept up, felt it, but got round fine. I went out one time for a 230km ride, in the Tuscany mountains, again no breakfast, a bottle of water and 1-2 trivial snacks… and completed it fine in about 8hrs.

I was doing all these sorts of things while I was digging my head in all the literature and trying to learn the academic knowledge. But the more I read and the more I did… the more the two separated.

Not one study has studied someone running for 10hrs in a low carb state followed the next day by 6hrs. Not one study has looked at pro cyclists training for 3hrs with no breakfast and a Ketotic diet and no-one is looking at anyone doing 8hr bike rides in this state.

Then I was practising it more and at a deeper level. I was racing, ultramarathons, and doing so in a very low carb/high fat state… and not feeding on the recommended amounts for “elite” during either. And I have been winning several races . I’m not beating the best in the world or challenging Killian Jornet… but I’m performing and doing stuff that has not been written about anywhere.

And it hasn’t been all results and success either. I’ve gone through a good deal of hurt in the process also. After the 10hr/6hr weekend run, I was wiped out for about a month. Some races and other big training days have caused serious stress for the body. Disturbed sleep, disrupted heart rates. But all part of the learning process. Not reading about it, experiencing it, suffering it.

So I’m exposing myself to harm… physical harm from the training, racing on this type of diet… over years. I’m taking risk as I am preaching this and making a living from it. But I’m not out to just make a buck, I want to first do it well and second do it with integrity. However, It’s an area where you can have powders, potions, pills for this that and the other. I have none of these. Then it’s a job where you can flaunt yourself and create “how to” plans for the perfect body or on how to become a superhuman athlete. I don’t have any of these either. Yet, the very ones selling you powders, potions, pills and superhuman plans are those without any skin in the game, for the most part.

**Skin Thickness**

This is my own terminology here, not Talebs. But in order to truly find out about something through doing, you need to put a lot of skin in the game and you need to have thick skin. So let’s just take an example

Guru comes out with a low carb or ketogenic diet for athletes. The guru runs one marathon in 3.30hrs or does an Ironman in 12hrs. And spent a whole 6 weeks himself on this type of diet. He or she looks good, has a nice website or is a respected academic. Very intelligent person too.

That’s all fine. But if the body works in ways we yet don’t understand, chances are that the mechanisms are far more sophisticated that we thought. These mechanisms haven’t actually been researched yet. So to work them out, you have to do things that are not the norm. You have to push the boat out and you have to put some thick pieces of skin in the game.

As I’ve mentioned, I’ve been living in this world for several years now. I’ve raced 14 ultramarathons on a Low Carb/High Fat diet and I have started most of these races in the fasted state. I’ve won 5 of these ranging from 70km to 200km in length. Doing this has taught me a lot . Far more than I have read or studied. Imagine turning up to a 200km ultramarathon without carb loading, without eating breakfast, without sports drinks or electrolytes, without my gram’s of CHO/hr calculated or “hydration” needs calculated … in fact, without doing what most of the books tell you to do or without what my qualifications had taught me ? I have lost a lot of skin mind you and put myself in a box on a few occasions. But this is how you really learn about the body, as if you don’t figure out how to get yourself out of the box, than you are really screwed. The information helps of course, and the theories can be help the comprehension but the doing at this extreme is where you get to understand things.

I’m only doing my thing. There are others doing theirs. In fact, I have found that many of the best athletes I have worked with or come across are those that are also have thick skin, thicker than mine. What makes things different for me is that I have studied it and continue to study it. But if you look at the training, nutrition, mindset etc of any world class athletes… you will find that they are doing things at a level that is not in the books either. Look at Ironman champions, marathon runners, cyclists and other athletes of the past and you’ll find that they were doing things that was way outside the “science” of the time. Example – Emil Zatopek, set 18 world records in distance running during the 1950’s. He won the gold in the 1952 Olympics at 5km, 10km and marathon. One of his training sessions was 80 times x 400m. This was deemed crazy and stupid at the time. Interval running for distance athletes was not typically used. Now the “science” has shown that interval running improves endurance.

‘Any individual destined to achieve greatness finds himself doing things that most people call ‘mad’, ‘killing’ or, most kindly, ‘eccentric’. – Percy Cerutty

I even have my own friends, ultrarunners that are running multiday events. One guy I know, ran 3 day and 6 day events. This isn’t 30km a day. This is continuous stuff. Sleeping is an option. Over 3 days he ran 300miles, slept for a few hours. The winner of the 6 day race ran 550miles. The books say this can’t be done without bonking, muscle loss and heart damage.

But these guys are doing it and coming out without these problems.

Now for the science bit. An interesting comment that my mate made was “by the end of the first day, you feel dead, the 2nd day you start to come round and by the 3rd day you are cruising”

The surface shows harmful effects of endurance exercise to be caused by raised cortisol and heart damage and inflammation. Stop after day 1, and all you can report is hurt and pain. However, through skin in the game, you learn more than the standard researchers learn. The reason is there is what’s called a Positive Feedback Loop when it comes to the immune response and inflammation.

Dig into this and you find adaptations triggered by transcription factors STAT3, nf-Kb and c-jun. Look further and you’ll see the upstream and downstream molecular responses of these factors, such as p53 and various MAP kinases. These proteins direct cellular responses to stimuli such as proinflammatory cytokines. This leads to genetic expression which controls apoptosis, cell signalling, inflammation and immune cell growth.

In other words, the body is more sophisticated than we thought. It actually calms the body down so to speak when there is immense damage instead of overreacting to it.

Now, it’s important for me to say that I’m not suggesting that this extreme form of exercise is necessarily wise or suited to everybody. I don’t want people to think that I am recommending everyone to go out and run for 6 days continuously if you really want to do your homework. There is the whole context of the situation that is crucial. By that I mean, when doing some form of extreme event, that you have the right framework in place. I ran 10hrs with no food… but I had put 2yrs of work into my nutrition/training/lifestyle before hand in order to make my body able for it. What this translates to is that there is a sort of logic to my risk taking. I am doing something at risk (running big mileage against “normal” recommendations) but I am doing so with experience and adaptations built up that I know will aid me.

### Barry — 2017-02-17T21:13:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-208788>

continued...........

**At the Click of a Button**

What we have today is a huge disconnect between the talker and the doer, between the intellectual and the practitioner. This is being driven by this instant communication age, the touch of a button access to information. We then have too many short cuts, too many unsupported declarations, too much telling without doing, and too little skin in the game.

Simply comparing how science was delivered and how the information spread before to modern day should make this clear. Pre “one touch” communication, when something new was born in the lab or field, it travelled slowly not exponentially. Academics spent years writing the papers. Those in the field, spent time practicing it. Gradually more academics learnt and theorized and gradually more practitioners carried out the . Guru’s and preachers could only spread their word locally or to other members of their tribes. The big difference here is that time is of the essence. This allows the theory to be properly challenged, it allows the practice to be properly tried out, and it prevents the preachers from preaching to the masses. Along the way, there will at least be a greater chance for some to put some skin in the game. Thus, when it finally does reach the masses, the information had a better chance of being relevant and having credibility.

Today, before a paper is even published, you can have academics tweeting and recording podcasts about the study. The practitioners then are diving straight in and sharing what they are doing with the world, without practising over time. Then guru’s then package the information and sell it, to the world, without actually having any skin in the game. Ecco il problema.

**A Punch is just a Punch**

In the terms of understanding the body and in particular, in elite sport, here’s my own simple formula:

Understanding Human Performance = Theory + Practice+ Skin in the Game

Understanding Elite Human Performance = Theory + Practice + Skin in the Game + Thick Skin in the Game

There is where, in my opinion, the whole debate of athletes nutrition and whether Carbs or Fats (and it’s never that clear cut) are better, has gone wrong.

The academics, for the large part, have zero skin in the game. They are too busy in the lab.

The guru’s, , for the large part, have some skin in the game, but not thick. They are too busy counting their “likes” and responding to tweets.

The top athletes , using a lot of the “unproven” strategies, with lots of thick skin in the game, are too busy winning. Plus they don’t know how or why it’s working for them, they’re just doing it.

The odd few like me, with thick skin in the game while also attempting to be a type of guru, are too busy repairing their skin and then putting more in the game.

I am in it, and I believe in it.

That’s what has been bothering me.

### Barry — 2017-03-03T15:49:27+0200

Reality Check:

You do not need to be "keto" in order to become fat adapted.

Biochemistry 101 : beta oxidation does not need ketones.

Don't get sucked in by the Joe Rogans and co....
