### Barry — 2017-03-05T18:36:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-209691>

How do you know when you are Fat Adapted?

A question I get asked all the time. The “science only” response is to get you RER (Respiratory Exchange Ratio) or RQ (Respiratory Quotient) tested which can be done in any sports physiology lab. Pretty straightforward test and it measures the rate at which you burn fat and glucose as the exercise intensity increases. However, this is done in the lab. It measures 2 variables, oxygen input and carbon dioxide output. Now I’m not going to get into it here, but there is a lot more to this whole fat adaptation thing than just that.

So, based on 8 years of what I have studied and tested (in the field), I have come up with a few practical ways which you can do yourself that will “arguably” tell you a lot more about what’s going on inside your cells. Here we go:

1\. You are not hungry in the morning

If you wake up starving, there are possibly two main things going wrong. One is that you had a bad sleep thereby messing with your circadian rhythm. This is your own internal biological clock that looks after the timing of everything. It can do this by regulating hormone release. If you sleep poorly, melatonin is not released, cortisol is elevated and prolactin is suppressed. This combination increases appetite or offsets your normal eating patterns.

The other thing that causes hunger in the morning is hypoglycaemia whereby your blood sugar levels are low. This can happen if you have some problems with insulin and tolerance of carbohydrates. In other words, you are insulin resistant and eat a high carb diet.

So sleep and being fat adapted…. A) Controls you appetite hormones and B) your blood sugar levels

2\. You can train “fasted”

Following on from No. 1, if you do not feel hungry, the next self test is to do whatever your typical training session is without breakfast. Now I know this can be anything from a 30min yoga session to a 6hr hilly bike ride. So I can’t talk specifically. All I will say is this:

If you can do whatever one of your tougher/longer training sessions is in the morning, without breakfast, and do it as you would have normally, then it’s a pretty good sign you are on the right path.

Example: morning 60-90min steady pace run or 2-3hr cycle or 60min swim sets

No breakfast, no eating during, no hunger during, normal or better performance = on the right path to fat adaptation

Next step up… is if you can do your longest/hardest session of the week, without breakfast.

Example: 4-5hr bike ride or 3-4hr mountain run

No breakfast, minimal eating during, water only, no hunger during, normal or better performance = pretty sure sign you are fat adapted and upregulating your fat adaptation.

3\. No need for snacks

If you are hungry between meals, you are likely to have drops in blood sugar levels. This is caused by insulin insensitivity and/or a high carbohydrate diet. Hunger between meals can also simply be a habitual thing… its 11 o’clock in work and everyone is drinking tea/coffee and eating biscuits. So changing the habit is the first step. However, not feeling the need to eat and simply not being hungry between meals, means that your blood sugar levels are steady and insulin is not misbehaving. Becoming fat adapted allows this.

Example: you eat some meat + avocado salad for lunch at 1pm. You do not eat again until 7pm and feel no hunger in between.

4\. You can intermittent fast without chewing your arm off

So intermittent fasting is becoming a trend. Like most things today in the diet and fitness industry... they try to package it and then sell it. You’ve got 5:2, 16/8 and various other “protocols”. Here’s all I’ll say:

If you have dinner at say 8pm. Get up the next morning and not feel any hunger. Go to work, school or whatever and not feel hungry during the morning. Get to lunch time without counting down the clock and then eat at an unplanned time. Eat a satisfying meal… not a horse… just a decent meal… and not slosh back half a loaf of bread and 3 slices of cake. Then and only then… can you consider you fast to be beneficial and for it to indicate that you’re fat burning mechanisms are intact. You should be comfortably able to skip breakfast and also lunch…. And just eat dinner without any major effort. This would be a much better sign of fat adaptation.

5\. You are not hungry after training

I’ve saved the best till last as its 1. The hardest and 2. IMO, the best indicator.

So you get up and train. Instead of coming back and necking a bowl of cereal, 2 flap jacks and a protein smoothie… you eat nothing. You may have to drive somewhere or you are just showering and distracted with other stuff at home... whatever… but you feel no major hunger and you are not chewing your leg off since you already chewed your arm off during an intermittent fast.

Now I don’t mean a 30min workout in the gym or a yoga class. I mean something where you had to push it and expend a good deal of energy. A long run or bike or hard swim set…. Or a weights/cardio long session in the gym. Something where you out for a while and burnt a decent bit of fuel.

Example: wake up, no breakfast, or just some eggs

Train 9-11am

Drive home 30mins. Shower/change 30mins.

Cook/Eat: 12.30 - 1pm

If at the end of that session you do not feel ravenous or feel no rush to eat… then this is a major sign that you have increased the rate at which you can burn fat as a fuel. Why? As it means you have increased the rate at which you can oxidise fatty acids and convert them to ATP. It means you have increased the storage depot of fatty acids in the muscle called IMTG’s (intramuscular triglycerides). It also possibly means you have increased gluconeogenesis, which is your own glucose manufacturing process. It "may" also mean you are producing ketones as an addition fuel source. In addition, you may have lowered your overall inflammatory response to the exercise.

All this occurs when you are highly fat adapted.

> If you have dinner at say 8pm. Get up the next morning and not feel any hunger. Go to work, school or whatever and not feel hungry during the morning. Get to lunch time without counting down the clock and then eat at an unplanned time. Eat a satisfying meal… not a horse… just a decent meal… and not slosh back half a loaf of bread and 3 slices of cake. Then and only then… can you consider you fast to be beneficial and for it to indicate that you’re fat burning mechanisms are intact. You should be comfortably able to skip breakfast and also lunch…. And just eat dinner without any major effort. This would be a much better sign of fat adaptation.
>
> 5\. You are not hungry after training
>
> I’ve saved the best till last as its 1. The hardest and 2. IMO, the best indicator.
>
> So you get up and train. Instead of coming back and necking a bowl of cereal, 2 flap jacks and a protein smoothie… you eat nothing. You may have to drive somewhere or you are just showering and distracted with other stuff at home... whatever… but you feel no major hunger and you are not chewing your leg off since you already chewed your arm off during an intermittent fast.
>
> Now I don’t mean a 30min workout in the gym or a yoga class. I mean something where you had to push it and expend a good deal of energy. A long run or bike or hard swim set…. Or a weights/cardio long session in the gym. Something where you out for a while and burnt a decent bit of fuel.
>
> Example: wake up, no breakfast, or just some eggs
>
> Train 9-11am
>
> Drive home 30mins. Shower/change 30mins.
>
> Cook/Eat: 12.30 - 1pm
>
> If at the end of that session you do not feel ravenous or feel no rush to eat… then this is a major sign that you have increased the rate at which you can burn fat as a fuel. Why? As it means you have increased the rate at which you can oxidise fatty acids and convert them to ATP. It means you have increased the storage depot of fatty acids in the muscle called IMTG’s (intramuscular triglycerides). It also possibly means you have increased gluconeogenesis, which is your own glucose manufacturing process. It "may" also mean you are producing ketones as an addition fuel source. In addition, you may have lowered your overall inflammatory response to the exercise.
>
> All this occurs when you are highly fat adapted.

Thank you Barry... I believe I've read some or most of the above from your earlier posts in this thread, which I had found extremely helpful.

When you mentioned being fat adapted without being in ketosis, I was not able to fully grasp the idea. My question was more directed at the 'without ketones' part, rather than the 'being fat adapted' part. Sorry for the confusion. So let me rephrase my question, if you don't mind: Do you eat a low fat diet and remain fat adapted? I believe you are currently in a high latitude environment, so you are not getting any extra help from the sun in the fat burning department?

### Carole traole — 2017-03-05T18:57:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-209698>

Barry, do you think it matters when you do intermittent fasting to best help with becoming fat adapted? I typically eat breakfast and lunch and intermittent fast for 17 hours until the next day's breakfast. Would it be better to skip breakfast? Thanks.

I believe you are currently in a high latitude environment, so you are not getting any extra help from the sun in the fat burning department?

not sure where you got "low fat diet" and fat adaptation. Its the fats that drive PPAR, LPL and CPT-1

### Barry — 2017-03-06T16:50:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-209753>

> [Carole traole said:](/goto/post?id=209698)
>
> Barry, do you think it matters when you do intermittent fasting to best help with becoming fat adapted? I typically eat breakfast and lunch and intermittent fast for 17 hours until the next day's breakfast. Would it be better to skip breakfast? Thanks.

good question. I'm not entirely sure. It's a case of context. If circadian rhythm is good, cortisol pattern, sleep etc... then I would favour skipping breakfast given that morning daylight stimulates the pineal gland.

I would also base it on time of the year. Maybe winter season is better to do what you are doing, with daylight hours finishing at 3-4pm in the northern hesisphere. But then I would change it in the summer to skipping breakfast, again due to light and UVA/B

And like all things, I would use my qoute "in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"...... so depending on exactly what you are trying to do, apply the stimulus that gives corresponding adaptations

### lohd2015 — 2017-03-06T17:08:20+0200

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=209751)

> not sure where you got "low fat diet" and fat adaptation. Its the fats that drive PPAR, LPL and CPT-1

My simple understanding of fat adaptation is the ability to burn both fat directly via beta-oxidation and glucose via glycolysis. And I thought the process of beta-oxidation involves the production of ketones. So when you said "You do not need to be "keto" in order to become fat adapted" I got quite confused. And it appears the whole thing is getting more convoluted... And I do appreciate your patience!

### Barry — 2017-03-06T18:24:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-209764>

> [lohd2015 said:](/goto/post?id=209757)
>
> My simple understanding of fat adaptation is the ability to burn both fat directly via beta-oxidation and glucose via glycolysis. And I thought the process of beta-oxidation involves the production of ketones. So when you said "You do not need to be "keto" in order to become fat adapted" I got quite confused. And it appears the whole thing is getting more convoluted... And I do appreciate your patience!

the process of beta-oxidation does NOT involve production of ketones.... thats what is confusing you

do a simple google search/wikipedia search..... beta-oxidation is simply conversion of fatty acids to Acetly-CoA

### Barry — 2017-03-28T17:09:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-211292>

Race Update

This year I'm leaving the ultra's to the side and focusing on the short-medium stuff.

Race: 25km , mountain trail

Start: 12noon

Before: 2 x soft boiled eggs

During: nothing, just 2 x swigs of water

Result: 2nd in 2hrs flat

This is the fastest I've run over this distance. When I finished, I wasn't hungry. Didn't eat until about 2hrs after.

Lots going on here. But yet again, the debate on "carbs for intensity/performance" goes out the window is this case.

### Barry — 2017-04-26T17:25:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-212967>

Reality #10:

this is the type of modern world athlete I see more of these days:

"So you set your alarm on your Bluetooth radio to go off at 6am, down your smoothie and pre work out energy drink, set your Garmin, hrm or gps, do your session which at the end you realized you needed an extra 2km, so you go around the block 4 times, get in and down a recovery shake to replenish glycogen, rush off to work to sit at your desk, update your blog and tag your photo’s on facebook, upload your training data, drive home, go the gym to work your core, come home and down another recovery shake, eat your delivered paleo meal, respond to emails, get into a discussion on your groups whatsapp, take your nighttime recovery shake, set your app on your phone for the sleep tracker, fall asleep looking at instagram photos. And this was an “easy” day.What does this produce ? Maybe…. Maybe a body that can complete the event and even do somewhat well. But a body that is weak and fragile. And a mind that races ahead yet wallows in the past."

So "light" , yes , but there are always other contributing factors. One is the dissappearance of the "less is more" approach.

Today we add. A simple example. In 1960's, the average marathon finishing time was 3-3.5hrs. They had no gels, no sports drinks, no compression socks, no special trainers, no hr monitor, no GPS tracker. They just ran.

Now the average finishing time is 4-4.5hrs. And they are covered in head to toe with gadgets and sports supplements and devices.

What we are doing is creating a society of soft and fragile humans that are devoid of adaptations.

And as I have said many times before

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

### Danco3636 — 2017-04-28T15:59:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213042>

> [kris90 said:](/goto/post?id=213039)
>
> Just some thoughts on fasting:
>
> 1) Works well when we are disconnected/low quantum yield (i.e. winter, lack of sunlight/stuck indoors working, etc) because this is a time we are losing energy to our environment quickly. Fasting forces use of our backup system (mammalian battery) and increases autophagy, allowing us to thrive in a low quantum yield by learning to recycle our energy
>
> 2) Works well when you are connected to the earth and sun, because you are harnessing your energy the way all living things were intended to by being plugged directly into our chargers, rather than using our backup system. It is still important to drink plenty of water in this environment and carbs are good too, since they are mostly water, and we need water to capture the sun's energy
>
> It would be appropriate to load up on carbs throughout the day in a high quantum yield environment, since insulin sensitivity is very high, and these carbs will refill the backup system, but to me it is not essential. We all have MORE than enough fat storage to forego eating for an entire day consistently if we choose to.
>
> I personally prefer eating my main meal when my insulin sensitivity drops. People fail to realise that insulin resistance is not a bad thing. It is essential for fat burning, and keeping glucose high in circulation for the brain as we preferentially burn fat. If I eat all my carbs at night before bed when insulin resistant, then I will have high blood sugar upon sleep. To me, this is beneficial, because insulin resistance + high blood sugar (a temporary diabetic state) signals sleep/hibernation, which is what I want at night. The following morning after my fast, blood sugar should return to baseline, and if not, then a few hours of morning sun should bring it back down due to the increased insulin sensitivity from sunlight.
>
> Fasting seems foolproof, of course, assuming you respect circadian rhythm and light cycles.

> [kris90 said:](/goto/post?id=213039)
>
> Just some thoughts on fasting:
>
> 1) Works well when we are disconnected/low quantum yield (i.e. winter, lack of sunlight/stuck indoors working, etc) because this is a time we are losing energy to our environment quickly. It is still important to drink plenty of water in this environment and carbs are good too, since they are mostly water, and we need water to capture the sun's energy

We all have MORE than enough fat storage to forego eating for an entire day consistently if we choose to.

here's what I think:

everything works better when you are fully connected with nature. No surprises there.

What you then have is the illusion of knowledge. Quantum yield, insulin resistance, sleep, late carbs, etc.... all have merit... but all he has done is pieced together numeruous blogs written by Jack which then gives him an exaggerated sense of his own intelligence.

I've fasted at night , ate no carbs and slept like a baby. This was done as it improves my chemiostic coupling , electron tunnelling and downregulates my PVN which in turn increases my melatonin production.

And there you have my illusion of knowledge.

Its happening all over the place now the more information that is available and the more we think we know.

But what we always have to do is simply connect as much as we can with nature and go on feel. And if it works for you, that is all.

I like the studies and the theory that Bill presents. Thing is, as you mentioned, these outcomes change with contextual issues. Practically every study does.

In fact , and this relates to my own practice, a recent paper showed how exercising fasted increases gene expression of HSL, FAT/CD36, IRS2 , PDK4 . What this then does is increase insulin sensitivity.

[http://ajpendo.physiology.org/content/ajpendo/early/2017/03/08/ajpendo.00006.2017.full.pdf](http://ajpendo.physiology.org/content/ajpendo/early/2017/03/08/ajpendo.00006.2017.full.pdf)

So this is where Bills recommendations are wrong based on context.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-05-02T15:14:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213214>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=212967)
>
> Reality #10:
>
> this is the type of modern world athlete I see more of these days:
>
> "So you set your alarm on your Bluetooth radio to go off at 6am, down your smoothie and pre work out energy drink, set your Garmin, hrm or gps, do your session which at the end you realized you needed an extra 2km, so you go around the block 4 times, get in and down a recovery shake to replenish glycogen, rush off to work to sit at your desk, update your blog and tag your photo’s on facebook, upload your training data, drive home, go the gym to work your core, come home and down another recovery shake, eat your delivered paleo meal, respond to emails, get into a discussion on your groups whatsapp, take your nighttime recovery shake, set your app on your phone for the sleep tracker, fall asleep looking at instagram photos. And this was an “easy” day.What does this produce ? Maybe…. Maybe a body that can complete the event and even do somewhat well. But a body that is weak and fragile. And a mind that races ahead yet wallows in the past."
>
> So "light" , yes , but there are always other contributing factors. One is the dissappearance of the "less is more" approach.
>
> Today we add. A simple example. In 1960's, the average marathon finishing time was 3-3.5hrs. They had no gels, no sports drinks, no compression socks, no special trainers, no hr monitor, no GPS tracker. They just ran.
>
> Now the average finishing time is 4-4.5hrs. And they are covered in head to toe with gadgets and sports supplements and devices.
>
> What we are doing is creating a society of soft and fragile humans that are devoid of adaptations.
>
> And as I have said many times before
>
> "in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

Be careful about the experts you listen too. Your decisions of life are only as good as the data you collect and accept. [https://medium.com/@daynebarkley/can-you-out-eat-a-bad-environment-9e958b86802a](https://medium.com/@daynebarkley/can-you-out-eat-a-bad-environment-9e958b86802a)

### kris90 — 2017-05-06T20:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213518>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=213516)
>
> @[kris90](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19157/)
> You must be smarter from this exchange.
> There must have been answer there, somewhere. I missed.
> Can you share it?
>
> You have shared your thoughts on seasonal eating elsewhere.
> Are you going to change what you do?
>
> .

In general, I don't eat many seasonal foods. I don't include a lot of vegetables in my diet in general. Probably moreso in summer than winter though. I eat good quality multi-grain bread often, because I've never had issues with gluten. I plan to eat it year round, and don't see any issues with that. Potatoes and rice I will also eat year round. Fruit, I am never really big on. Sometimes an apple during the day in the summer, or berries, but never in the winter.

So basically, my main carb sources are: bread, rice and potatoes, and I eat them year round. Fruits & veggies in general aren't included abundantly in my diet, and definitely not in winter. I don't plan to eat much fish this summer, but I will eat a little more in the winter. I keep PUFAs low now (including Omega-3). Diet is mostly saturated fat, minimal protein and some starch.

### kris90 — 2017-05-07T02:22:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213544>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=213523)
>
> So your diet isn't optimal then or Epi-paleo ..... Then again , does it need to be ?

Diet is not really that important to me. Getting sun, and optimizing my backup system is, so my focus is on environment, and when it comes to food, I eat what I enjoy. But 50% of my diet is fats (mostly saturated) so I am providing the electrons to my body. I'm also using carbs in addition to, moreso to keep exogenous glucose where it means to be, but still let my liver practice gluconeogenesis during my 18 hour fast. It's enjoyable, and effective.

As for DHA, it's still very important, but I think the amount that we need is fractional in comparison to what is preached, and excessive intake of DHA is a slippery slope in my experience.

### Dextery — 2017-05-07T04:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213558>

Since I started to follow this thread, I think I saw someone or several say they eat one meal a day...always in the evening. Jack posted this today on Facebook today about eating after sundown. For you athletes something to consider. I eat BAB one meal a day only but I am not a extreme athlete. Jack talks about obesity which you guys certainly are not. But does his teaching here even apply to fit athletes?

"What happens when you eat after the sun goes down? Your liver gets larger. Anything that gets larger by defintion is losing energy. Don't believe it? What happens to a star as it dies? It becomes a red giant. What happens to your ankle when you sprain it? It gets larger by swelling doesn't it? What happens in heart failure? Does the heart get smaller or enlarge on the chest Xray? It gets bigger. So why do food guru's continue to tell us that fat people, who are larger, are in positive energy balance? Is it actually the opposite? Yes, it is.......and this paper proves it. Anything that loses energy back to the environment by definition gets larger by allometric scaling laws. This is Dr. Jack Kruse 101 . Bio-physics for the win. When you eat post sunset your liver gets larger and you get leptin resistant at the liver and this causes metabolic changes in the leptin receptor in your hypothalamus. If you read my reality blog on the long loop of DHA replacement in the liver........you'll being (begin) to understand how light signals via your eyes make obesity manifest. Mitochondrial medicine 101. Share this with your smart ass food guru friends. It is time to stop the madness. [https://www.sciencedaily.com/releas…/2017/…/170504121737.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/05/170504121737.htm) And you still think it is about the fuel? Get some sun, a lot of it."

### JanSz — 2017-05-07T05:07:47+0300

I did not mean storing fat is body tissue.
I meant, what to do to keep fatty acids in the blood at desirable level (so when I test it, it would look good).
And that goes for all fatty acids, saturated or not.
Eating fatty acids identified as being at low levels, goes only so far.
There are other variables that influence final fatty acids profile.
One of them are peroxisomes that are involved in biosynthesis of fatty acids.
I am sure there are many other.
Neolithic diseases are one big factor.

### kris90 — 2017-05-07T14:08:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213585>

I also yoke my last meal to just before sunset. Right now in Ontario, sunset is around 7:30 PM to 8:00 PM. I eat dinner with my family at 5:30, so although I am eating a good amount of carbs with dinner, I still have about 2 hours post dinner of sunlight before it's dark. In the winter unfortunately, I do eat in the darkness, because 5:30 PM is already pitch black. I usually go easy on the carbs then, but even so, won't over-stress. Dining with my family is an important part of my wellbeing, and the last thing I want is to stress over it. I have never been fat. If what I am doing does some temporary damage, I believe it is reversed the following day through my fasting and exercise, and general peaceful mindset. It's not like I'm binging all night on twinkies, or demolishing a bowl of popcorn while watching TV.

We need to find pleasure in life, and do so in ways that we feel are not harmful. The more rules we set for ourselves, I believe we jeopardize our quality of life. Knowledge and awareness are key, so you can manipulate your health and make the most of your life.

### kris90 — 2017-05-07T14:18:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213586>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=213565)
>
> I did not mean storing fat is body tissue.
> I meant, what to do to keep fatty acids in the blood at desirable level (so when I test it, it would look good).
> And that goes for all fatty acids, saturated or not.
> Eating fatty acids identified as being at low levels, goes only so far.
> There are other variables that influence final fatty acids profile.
> One of them are peroxisomes that are involved in biosynthesis of fatty acids.
> I am sure there are many other.
> Neolithic diseases are one big factor.
>
> ....

When you say fatty acids in the blood, do you mean triglycerides, or FFA? If triglycerides, the key would be to optimize metabolism. If you have high triglycerides in serum, I think you have 2 main choices:

1) Limit carbs, and go ketotic, since insulin resistance is only helpful in the absence of exogenous glucose in the diet, and this will force triglycerides to be broken down for beta oxidation & ketogenesis
2) Limit fats, and eat more sugar, to reverse insulin resistance, improving insulin sensitivity which will mobilize triglycerides

You could do a mixed diet with caloric restriction, or long fasts that should help mobilize triglycerides as well. As long as food intake is controlled, you won't "back up" your body's own energy substrates (from body fat).

Dextery, look, I like where you're coming from but this is just a primal example of "context matters"

We simple cannot recommend a specific wholesale approach to how we eat..... or how we do any thing for that matter

I'll use what you just posted as an example. Its also in this context that Jacks theory and recommendations get mixed up.

So I train at 5-6.30pm... come home and have dinner at 7-7.30pm , its dark.

First, what has the exercise done for me... increased my leptin and insulin sensitivty. Not only that but a host of other hormonal changes and gene expression. These changes than resonate with my eating post exercise in terms of appetite hormones, mitochondrial biogenesis, para/sympathetic nervous system, etc etc . So I don't get obese, I get healthier.

I then go to sleep, wake up and make like a sphinx.

We are all confusing ourselves with reductionist recommendations and out of context theories.

No man is an island.

### kris90 — 2017-05-07T20:19:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213609>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=213597)
>
> Dextery, look, I like where you're coming from but this is just a primal example of "context matters"
>

>
> I'll use what you just posted as an example. Its also in this context that Jacks theory and recommendations get mixed up.
>
> So I train at 5-6.30pm... come home and have dinner at 7-7.30pm , its dark.
>
> First, what has the exercise done for me... increased my leptin and insulin sensitivty. Not only that but a host of other hormonal changes and gene expression. These changes than resonate with my eating post exercise in terms of appetite hormones, mitochondrial biogenesis, para/sympathetic nervous system, etc etc . So I don't get obese, I get healthier.
>
> I then go to sleep, wake up and make like a sphinx.
>
> We are all confusing ourselves with reductionist recommendations and out of context theories.
>
> No man is an island.

Great post. I believe both eating habits, and exercise habits are part of the peripheral systems/clocks that can be phase shifted, or phase delayed within a day/night cycle. For example, someone might argue eating breakfast and skipping dinner is superior to skipping breakfast and eating supper. But honestly, when your central clock is synchronized to your light cycle, it should not matter when you time your meal.

Cortisol and melatonin are like metronomes. They guide us through the day, and transition us into night, and repeat the cycle. If you eat breakfast, you will temporarily disrupt the Cortisol wave, thus lowering stress on your body and reversing catabolism. But due to the increased insulin sensitivity, that food will be shuttled away quickly into storage, so Cortisol levels can return to where they were in the natural, diurnal cycle. If you skip breakfast, you will begin to shift towards insulin resistance as FFA levels in the blood rise allowing your body to use fat as a fuel. You are now phase shifting the insulin sensitivity towards the latter half of the day, so when you eat dinner, insulin should work just as well as earlier in the day. That's why I believe total fasting time is all that matters, but actual time of day to eat your meal is irrelevant. Same goes with exercise, if you do it at night and then eat, you are essentially reversing the normal insulin resistance that occurs in preparation for the nightly fast.

I think the key is to synchronize your own circadian rhythm that gives you full exposure to the amount of daylight (i.e. don't be asleep during daylight). You want to try to utilize every damn minute of sunlight that's available to you.

### kris90 — 2017-05-08T12:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213644>

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) important key note which you have stated before is that metabolism is coupled to sleep. So long as we aren't idiotic enough to use blue light at night, and eat "the munchies" like a lot of modern humans do.

I like to overeating my large meal at night. But, I'm done my last bite before 6 PM, and am asleep by 10 PM. I feel full, sometimes bloated, but very satisfied and relaxed after my meal. I wake up as if I hadn't eaten anything for dinner. It's as if the excess calories I consumed were dissipated as heat (kind of reminds me of what you taught us leptin does for us in the middle of the night).

### Barry — 2017-05-09T16:33:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213735>

some thoughts on what being connected to nature means in my context:

\- when its sunny, being indoors for any length of time, makes you feel uneasy

\- if the air is cool, but the sun is out, others cover up but you remove more clothing to expose more skin

\- same with training, when cycling, others are zipped up if air temp is fresh, but as long as the radiant heat from the sun is there, you wear less

\- you prefer to eat your meals outside, the food on the plate looks even better

\- after waking, the first thing you want to do is walk outside barefoot

\- like a kid, you don't like wearing shoes, and at every opportunity, kick them off

\- when its grey and cloudy, you feel uneasy

\- you notice the change of colour and growth in the plants, flowers and vegetation almost daily

\- being in a mall/shopping center is torture

\- you fall naturally asleep almost at the exact same time every night and wake without an alarm clock at the exact same time

\- when you meet someone for the first time, you know within 5mins if they are disconnected, if they take their smartphone out for no apparent reason

there are more, that is all for now

some thoughts on what being connected to nature means in my context:

\- when its sunny, being indoors for any length of time, makes you feel uneasy
✔️ I crave being in the sun!

\- if the air is cool, but the sun is out, others cover up but you remove more clothing to expose more skin
✔️yep

\- same with training, when cycling, others are zipped up if air temp is fresh, but as long as the radiant heat from the sun is there, you wear less
✔️yep. Always less

\- you prefer to eat your meals outside, the food on the plate looks even better
✔️ cooking outside too.

\- after waking, the first thing you want to do is walk outside barefoot
✔️ my morning pee is outside in my undies or naked.

\- like a kid, you don't like wearing shoes, and at every opportunity, kick them off
✔️when home shoes are always off. At every opportunity.

\- when its grey and cloudy, you feel uneasy
✔️ just like my cats we get anxious lol

\- you notice the change of colour and growth in the plants, flowers and vegetation almost daily
✔️I do my daily walk inspection of my trees and shrubs.

\- being in a mall/shopping center is torture
✔️I avoid the mall and very rarely go.

\- you fall naturally asleep almost at the exact same time every night and wake without an alarm clock at the exact same time
✔️yep no alarm clock in years.

\- when you meet someone for the first time, you know within 5mins if they are disconnected, if they take their smartphone out for no apparent reason
✔️for sure. People do it all the time. Even when visiting each other they have their phones on. I intentionally leave my phone off or at home when visiting family and friends.

there are more, that is all for now
⚫️➖ Essential Items For Healing
Cold - Showers and ice bathsWater - Drinking and soakingSunlight - Outside on the groundDHA - SeafoodSleep - Deep long sleep

★彡 Nature Provides the tools. We just need to use them.

Or ➖

HEALERS I KNOW:

1\. Sunlight.

2\. Pure clean water.

3\. Earth, magnetism, grounding, CT (cold).

4\. Seafood/ DHA

5\. Self confidence in your choices.

6\. Family & friends.

7\. Laughter, fun & Play.

8\. Connecting to nature.

9\. Sleep.

### kris90 — 2017-05-09T18:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213754>

Just wondering, has this link been posted here before (sorry if it has)?

[https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/11/151117091234.htm](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/11/151117091234.htm)

> Another key finding: Despite their low intake of carbs, these fat-burning athletes had normal muscle glycogen levels -- the storage form of carbohydrates -- at rest. They also broke down roughly the same level of glycogen as the high-carb runners during the long run, and synthesized the same amount of glycogen in their muscles during recovery as the high-carb athletes.
>
> "This was completely unexpected, but now that we have observed it we have some novel ideas why this is the case. We can only speculate on the mechanism behind it," Volek said.
>
> Muscle glycogen was discovered in the 1960s to be a critical energy source for athletes, which led to decades of emphasis on high-carb diets to support energy needs during intense exercise. But Volek said the body has an elegant system to support glycogen levels even when carbohydrates are limited in the diet.
>
> "The blue print for becoming 'fat- or keto-adapted' is hard wired into our genetic code. However, traditional 'healthy' diets with carbohydrates as the dominant nutrient prevent this alternative metabolic operating system from ever booting up.
>
> "Restricting carbs allows the program to reboot and enable many athletes to achieve improved levels of health and performance" he said.

Is the reason the low-carb athletes had comparable muscle glycogen level to the high carb athletes because of the ability of the PPP to replenish glycogen without carbs?

### Barry — 2017-05-10T16:52:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213815>

other signs of being connected:

\- a la Dan, yes, always prefer to pee outside (not just in the morning) even if there is an option to pee indoors

\- a disregard for material things

\- in winter, still wanting to do CT

\- no matter how many times you CT, I mean outdoors in sea,lakes, river,... ..... always having anxiety and a friendly fear... but a lust for both

\- having tried every CT - ice bath, river, lake, shower etc...... feeling that the best CT is the sea

\- going for a 6hr run in the mountains with nothing

\- feeling better barefoot on the grass is better than concrete

\- Feeling better the less people there are around

\- Feeling your best when you are by the sea and close to the mountains

### malc0088 — 2017-05-10T18:33:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213820>

Love this 'Signs of being Connected'

\- barefoot run with no shirt and tiny running shorts in bright sun

\- jumping into the local lake to CT any time of year - and getting strange looks or ignorant comments like 'hows the water?' with a sarcastic remark - reminds me when my action makes others uncomfortable i'm headed in the right direction.

\- having a sense of urgency to get outside and strip down to reconnect when i've been indoors too long

\- the constant draw to natural running water, rocks, and grass on my exposed skin

\- best CT ever at 9k ft. in snowmelt river in full spectrum sun, fully submerged is a serious invigorating shot of life.

\- grilling some fish and veggies outside as the sun sets...feeling of fullness and completeness

### Barry — 2017-05-11T22:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213876>

okay, saving the best for last...... being connected:

\- you can tell the time without wearing a watch

\- you prefer the suffering in every aspect of life, acute that is.

\- you are drawn to the color purple

\- you cannot hold your smartphone in your hand for too long

\- eating a "light" meal never really happens. You either eat well or you don't

\- each day, you find it more and more difficult to live in the modern world

\- your sport is not done for numbers, distances asthetic purposes or even accomplisments. The priority for you is simply to be outdoors in nature

Finally, this for me is how it all clicks together:

you meet someone, and become close to them, be it a friend, a spouse, a relation or a neighbour...... and you find out that they have never heard of Becker, Ling, Pollack, Lane, Marino..... or even Kruse or Murray...

yet, they are doing almost all the shit I have just listed without even thinking about it

I rest my case.

### caroline — 2017-05-12T05:12:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213902>

Yes ....all of the above! When I met my partner 3 years ago - he had never heard about Jack Kruse! He was doing so much right by instinct.

I just can't figure out how to get the house even moderately clean because I want to be outside all the time!

Being away with our camper trailer is a huge game changer - outside from sunrise to sunset. Just throw some shrimp and salmon on the barbie.

I now hate shopping malls and crowds of people with a passion ....being out in the bush is a priority ....and being in the ocean is the biggest priority of all.

One downside \[I suppose\] .....I am running out of patience for those who cant be bothered to even give it a go ...... I need to use my time much more wisely now.

Now that I know better .....I can and will do so much better.

### kris90 — 2017-05-12T14:15:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-213920>

It's true. I've always loved camping and outdoors. I would be one of the only ones going up to a cottage who enjoyed not having cell reception, and took full advantage of spending the entire day outdoors, in the sun and swimming in the lake, exploring islands barefoot, cliffdiving, fishing, and enjoying water sports, and then of course bonfires at night.

I always had an enhanced sense of wellbeing during a weekend at the cottage, and then upon returning back to the city, it was gone. I never really thought much of it, only that I knew I felt good, but wasn't sure why. And now I know.

I'm glad I moved to the country. I have that feeling every evening when I get home from work, and on weekends. Only thing missing is waterfront, but I'm only a 5 min drive from the river.

### Barry — 2017-05-18T21:16:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-214261>

Race Update

So I'm racing shorter stuff now, ~10-20km mountain trail.

Race series I am currently doing takes place every Wed night. 7.30pm.

These races are up a mountain and down, full gas. I am racing well , finishing in top 1o usually.

I am still LCHF/Ketotic/IF/SITG/Dots Connected etc.

However, sleep is destroyed after these races. Finish is usually 8.30pm, return home 9.30-10pm.

Body feels like it has just been in mild trauma, and going to sleep is difficult.

Never asked this before in the 5yrs I have been blogging..... but

Any suggestions ?? (bear in mind I know the real answer is to not do these races, but thats not what I'm looking for)

### Ski coach — 2017-05-18T21:58:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-214264>

After an overly long ski day and late return home, I find mild CT helps me both recover a bit and calm down. By mild I mean maybe 20 minutes with cool fat burner or a few minutes in a 60 to 65 degree tub. If I know it is going to be one of those days, I might even take a bunch of icepacks and put them on my legs and knees in the car for the drive home.

### malc0088 — 2017-05-19T00:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-214272>

Maybe it has to do with sympathetic activation being ramped up at the time melatonin should be secreted. i'll make some traditional kava root, turmeric, black pepper, or valerian to aid in slowing my mind down (the endorphins after the race typically can cause me to stay awake somewhat). I close my eyes in bed and all i see is the trail and the constant alertness of dodging obstacles (i've been known to jerk my body in bed). i wear blue blockers after dark and when i'm driving home (the headlights can really jack up my sleep). i too like to use cold after races and before bed, massage, and getting in a decent meal all aid in proper sleep for me.

Barry...

No suggestions. I never was able to sleep after evening race. Not once. Elevating legs helped me feel better but still couldn't sleep. 7:30PM is pretty late for an effort that hard.

If I was ever in that situation again...things I might try would be stuff like the following: lying on the ground, avoid going to bed until tired, activities that stimulate parasympathetic nervous system....breathing, meditation, walking. I'm sure you've tried all kinds of things.

PS It pains me to say it but...you were so right about the Quantlet. Downfall.

> Maybe it has to do with sympathetic activation being ramped up at the time melatonin should be secreted. i'll make some traditional kava root, turmeric, black pepper, or valerian to aid in slowing my mind down (the endorphins after the race typically can cause me to stay awake somewhat). I close my eyes in bed and all i see is the trail and the constant alertness of dodging obstacles (i've been known to jerk my body in bed). i wear blue blockers after dark and when i'm driving home (the headlights can really jack up my sleep). i too like to use cold after races and before bed, massage, and getting in a decent meal all aid in proper sleep for me.

as Paul mentioned too, it is all about ramping the sympathetic system down.

I've done CT in rivers and lakes before post race... but also sea , at night, a couple of times, and remember sleeping well. Might have to get in post race at night and see.

### htw — 2017-05-21T12:38:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-214368>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=214261)
>
> However, sleep is destroyed after these races. Finish is usually 8.30pm, return home 9.30-10pm.
>
> Body feels like it has just been in mild trauma, and going to sleep is difficult.
>
> Never asked this before in the 5yrs I have been blogging..... but
>
> Any suggestions ?? (bear in mind I know the real answer is to not do these races, but thats not what I'm looking for)

I am also KETO 2 years and HFLC for about 2-3 years. My peersonal expirience is when I am training HARD and LONG (2h streight 5x5 for glycogen depletion) and I have one day for rest before this trainig I have to eat more high-glycemic carb pre and poste workout. I tried a lot of sfuff - D-RIBOSIS and others - fenugreek but my sleep was crappy (I have cortisol PEAK at 2 at night) . I knew that for keto adapted person advice eat some carb is trivial advice but in my expirince ony working.
Other days my sleep is brilient, my eyes are closing automaticly (9-10), red glasses works - SCN is key, but one day I have to carb up little I assume for faster glycogen resynthesis.

KETO for athletic performance is great, in normal scenario, I would say 90-95%, but there is this 5-10%. You are fat adapted so **you are metabolically flexible** so you can experiments with glucose in your personal context.

### malc0088 — 2017-05-22T17:11:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-214423>

carbs post race/hard workout + CT (cold river, pool, ice vest) = happy sleep

I've definitely been a zero carb guy and got bit in the butt by it...felt like crap. I tend to struggle (generally speaking) with balance. I've decided (after a tibial stress fracture this past winter (incredibly overtrained during the late summer/fall 2016, low libido, poor thyroid function, burn out) that I need to loosen the grip on being strict with my trail running training programming and prioritize my energy to the following.
1\. light/water/magnetism
2\. emotional/spiritual/social practices to maintain life balance
3\. training - when 1&2 are getting the energy they deserve i'm finding i don't need to train as often and i'm a lot less prone to burn out.

I took 3 months off from running and I've refocused my energy, routines, and education (when you know better, you do better) on light, water, magnetism. here are a few changes though a lot are 2nd nature and are automatic.
1\. prioritized AM sun seeking, grounding, qi gong routine. Prioritize noon sun (get out of the office), and spend 2-4 hours in the afternoon/PM sun.
2\. blocking blue light at all times while indoors or on tech, and keep up on my hydration.
3\. Dminder tracking - my goals used to involve hours or miles per week, and are now hours per week in the sun or hitting 100% of my UV requirement for the day.
4\. less time sedentary after dinner (go walk, swim, read outside) or watching the latest Cycling tour races outdoors.
5\. Regular social engagement with friends who are invested in my life
6\. recognition that running 3-4 50k-50 mile races per spring/fall is not conducive to all the efforts i'm putting into my health. (new goal: 1 ultra per spring/fall in the mountains, more social cycling events and short 5-10k trail racing)
7\. Having a monthly book challenge with my wife ( we are reading 'going somewhere by Dr. Marino' outloud in 30 days (15 pages per day) outside) we will do a new book together each month to 'know better, do better'. I couldn't help but crack open 'cross currents by dr. becker' because there are so many overlaps. both men are amazing writers.
8\. Some kind of CT everyday: face dunks, cold river, or cold pool plunge
9\. Bulk order oysters so i can eat 10 per day without breaking the bank.
10\. after all of that, run or cycle with intensity based on my morning HRV score.

My ah-ha with nature and training: I don't need to call upon my Will when it comes to connecting with nature, it's like my body/mind already know what to do, but the last 3 years of ultra running i've been relying on my will (in many cases forcibly) too much to try to meet some training/racing expectation, fill some void (while red flag after red flag warning), that only leaves me drained and broken. 1-9 is now filling the void completely, and 10 is now more enjoyable than ever (more fitness with less training).

### JanSz — 2017-05-29T19:29:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-215002>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=215001)
>
> Jansz you asking me this upset me. IT HAS NOTHING TO DO WITH THE FUEL.

This subject is a crucial part of what is discussed in the article that you posted.
\-------------------
Science confirmed that when Uphill Athlete sent Ballinger to the sports performance lab at UC Davis, **where he found out that his body shifted from burning fat for fuel to burning carbs** when his heart rate hit 115 beats per minute (bpm). “So, pretty much if I’m doing anything above a walk, I’m burning carbohydrates,” Ballinger says.
\-----
The numbers confirmed it: In mid-April, right before Ballinger flew to Tibet to begin the 2017 Everest season, he received new UC Davis test results for how efficiently his body was working. **“I’d shifted my metabolism from starting to burn carbs at a heart rate of 115 all the way up to 141** — a pretty dramatic change for five months of work,” he says.

///

This builds your solar callus in your skin and makes it more likely to work with the more powerful light waves in UVB light that make Vitamin D3. UVA light makes the dopamine and melatonin and these two limbs of electromagnetic waves are needed to make your mitochondria hum at 100 Hz and fat burn. Fat burning is never tied to the fuel you give it. It is tied to the oscillation we create in our mitochondria. Quantum biology 101. This is why Type 1 and type 2 diabetics blood glucose is so high. ### Jack Kruse — 2017-05-29T19:38:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-215005>

This is the basis of the heart rate variability (HRV)/blood glucose index. A healthy and well-rested circulatory system produces lower heart rates with a greater variability of oscillations in the heart and blood plasma (high HRV), while a stressed and overtrained body, produces higher heart rates with a lower variability of the oscillations (low HRV). The lowered HRV means that the 100Hz oscillation cannot manifest so glucose rises in blood to fuel the heteroplastic mitochondria. Chronic sympathetic stimulation comes from chronic activation of the PVN in the brain stem and it goes by another well known acronm called LEPTIN RESISTANCE. What is adrenal fatigue due too? > [https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/)

Barry.........I told you 6 years ago it was both. CT6. And the June 2017 webinar will bring that point to bear for every member who hears it. It appears you have missed some of the quantum keys.

Evolutionary thinking is always the king of the mountain. The literature, in my view, takes a back seat to Mother Nature in the new paradigm of healthcare. Nothing trumps the laws of nature, especially man's beliefs. The use of any form of light that is not natural, espcially those created on an AC transformer, is toxic to cells because it induces dirty electricity and it creates flicker effects in those manufactured light waves. These things act to decrease the amount of water a mitochondria creates. This lack of water means sunlight, the natural light form, can not turn the water made in the mitochondria into a liquid crystal called the exclusion zone (EZ) from this water. This is why man made light is toxic to our system. Artifical light of all types decrease the amount of water a mitochondria makes and dumps into the cytoplasm around the mitochondria and nucleus. This is what dehydration and a high BUN creatine ratio really are all about, from a quantum perspective. Cell water is powered up by sunlight to make a capacitor from this water. This is a cell's ultimate battery source. This solar battery that makes water electricity ultimately powers the entire biosphere because of these connections. I think the Universe is powered by water electricity and I believe every cell is powered in the same way. Water is the universal electron and proton donor when it is energized by solar light. When you understand this quantum connections you begin to see that proton and electron currents in water made by sunlight create currents inside of cells and over extracellular distances that are capable of delivering physical an chemical messages concerning the redox status of all parts of the cell that are communicated by water networks. This sets in motion the requisite core of chemical reactions that restore the local and global energy balance of all living things. Cooling helps augment all these effects because cooling changes the density of water and more electrons are present in water as it cools and this helps form a massive redox pile of electrons that are used to run biochemical reactions in a cell. Cooling has another critical effect on proteins in the cell. The peptide linkage is composed of 6 atoms that lie in a plane like a rigid rod. This is a requirement for a pendulum oscillator system that the eye clock uses to control the SCN. The central C-N bond has some double bond attributes and is chemically very rigid. It is made more rigid by cooling. This process is known as resonance stabilization and is critcally important in determining the 3-D conformation of polypeptides in cells. Cooling stimulates heat shock protein 70. HSP 70 is key in maintaining this rigid relationship between the peptide bond while allowing side chains to be frely moveable in the frictionless sea of water inside a cell during activities of daily living when the sun is out. This is critical in the timing mechanism in cells, especially the SCN. Both cold thermogenesis and heat stress are capable of stimulating HSP 70 to maintain this pendulum function in protein side chains to make the timing mechanism link to the sun and dark cycle. It points out why temperature is closely related to the control mechanisms built into circadian biology oscillation and why using cold and heat (Fournier effect) can help reset or entrain a broken oscillator. That is the biophysical basis of this blog post. [https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway/)

### Jack Kruse — 2017-06-01T15:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-215248>

I mentioned the eye...........what affects the eye? Light. I just have taught you the details now.

For nature everything is about light. That is what E=mC^2 says. Look at it carefully. Light and things with mass are equivalent. The only thing that defines the differences is the environment that light is in. In a cell, light is slowed down and it turns into mass that a cell uses to make all the things it needs.

That is the story of this picture.

Look at it carefully. Nature is a reductionist for light. It is obvious to those who look it. Few do.
**Why is change hard? Because some people prefer their problem to the real solution.**

like this, again from CT4:

" No one fueling performance with carbohydrates can match the feats of fat adapted athletes. NO ONE. Perhaps useful device for those that have not been able to structure their life to access sunlight and are stuck indoors.
But this still is only a small bandaid on a huge problem.

However if you have access to daily sunshine than I don't think there is anything to gain.
Especially since you can easily exceed the CT benefits with other means. Even with performance. Perhaps it may create more harm for these folks just because now it is an artificial light, emf and wireless emitting defice. The very thing we are trying to stay away from.

Nature wins from my observation of implementing (and eliminating) things.

Best to work on restructuring ones life.
Especially for health and not rely on yet another device or gadget.

Just my thoughts.

I wore it for a week or so and noticed my sleep was effected when usually I sleep sound deep and long. Also made me anxious/ stress.
Turned it off and put it back in the box, broken band and all.

I'll stick to using nature as my first line of defense.

Still would like to hear others thoughts.

From my notes:

⚫️➖ Essential Items For Healing
Cold - Showers and ice baths
Water -Drinking and soaking
Sunlight - Outside on the ground
DHA -Seafood
Sleep -Deep long sleep

★彡 Nature Provides the tools. We just need to use them.

Or ➖

HEALERS I KNOW:

1\. Sunlight.

2\. Pure clean water.

3\. Earth, magnetism, grounding, CT (cold).

4\. Seafood/ DHA

5\. Self confidence in your choices.

6\. Family & friends.

7\. Laughter, fun & Play.

8\. Connecting to nature.

9\. Sleep.

> These races are up a mountain and down, full gas. I am racing well , finishing in top 1o usually.
>
> I am still LCHF/Ketotic/IF/SITG/Dots Connected etc.
>
> However, sleep is destroyed after these races. Finish is usually 8.30pm, return home 9.30-10pm.
>
> Body feels like it has just been in mild trauma, and going to sleep is difficult.
>
>
> Never asked this before in the 5yrs I have been blogging..... but
>
> Any suggestions ?? (bear in mind I know the real answer is to not do these races, but thats not what I'm looking for)

Have you tried habituation at all, Barry? Moving your typical training window to the end of the day, closer to bed time, in order to help your body get used to it.

I don't know what your usual schedule looks like, but I suspect it is mostly earlier in the day. I run exclusively first thing in the morning, and every race I've done thus far, has had a morning start. So I'd almost certainly have to get used to an evening starting gun. Particularly for shorter, higher intensity efforts.

### Barry — 2017-06-09T15:55:53+0300

I run exclusively first thing in the morning, and every race I've done thus far, has had a morning start. So I'd almost certainly have to get used to an evening starting gun. Particularly for shorter, higher intensity efforts.

I like what you're saying as it's the Law of Specificity ... I've done 4 of these races already so by default I feel more habituated . But normally all my training is am

Best recovery so far has been sea dip after ... Carbs post race also . Recently did hot bath , seaweed and salts.. Cold shower after... That worked quite well too ... Sleep still not optimal though ..

### JoeBranca — 2017-06-11T18:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-215827>

This passage in a book caught my attention recently.

> I was in a public library rummaging through the free box of magazines when I stumbled upon an article about the Tarahumara Indians in the May 1976 issue of National Geographic. The story focused on their ability to effortlessly run long distances. In a two-day period, the best runners could cover 435 miles, which is equal to sixteen marathons. Even an average Tarahumara could run more than 100 miles without stopping or suffering sore joints.
> To this day, the Tarahumara are considered the greatest runners in the world. Running has been part of the Tarahumara culture since the 1500s. It was a means of both athletic expression and survival. They delivered mail and intervillage communications and hunted for food on foot. While the article was inspirational for someone interested in long-distance running, what was most interesting to me was that the Tarahumara ran in handmade sandals rather than shoes.
> ...
> I spent most of my time watching the men run and observing how they interacted. One thing was true: they were always running. The trails were constantly in use, and I seldom saw the men walking.
> I spent ten days running, seeing the land, getting in shape, and orienting myself mentally to the running techniques of the Tarahumara. Watching them was a beautiful sight. It looked like the way a human should run. Their backs were ramrod straight, and they pointed their toes down. This caused them to land midfoot rather than striking with their heel first, which is a result of having the toes up. What was most noticeable was that the looks on their faces showed that they loved to run. There was no grimacing or grunting. They were _actually smiling_ as they traversed the land.

Copper Canyon Mexico where the uncivilized Tarahuma Indians live is at 27 degree N latitude, and fairly high elevation (2100ft - 8000ft). Distance runners seem to have robust health at least whats been studied in male runners age 18 - 48.

### JanSz — 2017-06-11T19:07:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-215830>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=215827)
>
> T
> Their backs were ramrod straight, and they **pointed their toes down. This caused them to land midfoot rather than striking with their heel first,** which is a result of having the toes up.

I had a VCR video on which Russian trainer was describing similar method, but he was aiming at landing at the toes (without using heel or hardly using them).
To visualize, he compared first human leg to animal's.
Animals run on their toes only, their heel is not able to touch ground.
Additionally he was after creating circular motion of the feet where part of the (I think) upward motion would happen as a spring recoil, without using energy to accomplish it.
He suggested to give that motion time to happen, saving energy in the process.

I think my plantar fasciitis problem came from incorrect application of this method.

/

**Stuck at a high latitude?** Get some altitude. Solar irradiance increases 6-10% for every 1,000 meters (3,280 feet) you climb. [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1011134496000188](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1011134496000188)

### Danny — 2017-06-13T22:48:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-216100>

Why would hunter gatherers in primitive times run just for the hell of it and late in the day to boot. They wouldn't. they do it to hunt. What do you do after a hunt? You eat by a fire if the hunt is successful or you go cold and hungry if it's not a success. Maybe this is how we are evolutionarily hardwired to recover. Eat w/ fire or fast and shiver.

### Barry — 2017-06-14T17:42:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-216160>

its the "Peter Attia" diet.... apparently:

[https://www.trainingpeaks.com/blog/...h-to-dieting-for-endurance-athletes-part-one/](https://www.trainingpeaks.com/blog/the-peter-attia-approach-to-dieting-for-endurance-athletes-part-one/)

what is interesting to note is as follows:

\- The word is still slowly getting out to the masses. I think the main delay in its progress is the time element required. Many of the sell out books and sites like Mark Scissons Primal Endurance say "3 weeks" to fat/keto adaptation.

That ain't true. No-one wants to know that it is a 24-36month transition for most. Personally I have found this can be shortened based on context e.g. level of athlete, body type and haloptype, time already on a LCHF type diet etc......... but it still can take 1-2yrs.

This is why

1\. you don't see the research

2\. you don't have too many success stories

3\. not many elite athletes take it up... they simply don't have that amount of time to wait

4\. the other dots are rarely properly connected

5\. very few put their skin in the game

That is all.

### Barry — 2017-06-27T17:48:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217250>

> [SatoriHeart said:](/goto/post?id=217235)
>
> Yes, but Ben Greenfield is also promoting [this](http://neurohacker.com/cp/greenfield/ref/18/?rfsn=629007.55ce1bac7&utm_source=refersion&utm_medium=influencers&utm_campaign=629007.55ce1bac7), which IMHO makes either his powers of reasoning suspect or his wallet thirsty. In any case, the type of stimulation used by HaloSport is _not safe_ for either short-term or long-term applications due to the potential for calcium ion overload and neuronal membrane de-stabilization. LTP (Long-term potentiation) as a sustainable practice is not grounded in firm scientific principles. Just ask anyone that has used CILTEP for any prolonged period of time. There is a reason the commercial variety contains L-Phenylalanine and ALCAR.
>
> Being superhuman, in theory, is an ambitious idea. In practice, you can't fool evolution.

### HenrikB — 2017-06-28T17:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217347>

Barry,
It's so great and encouraging to see your pro athletes/friends coming on the top this last weekend. Steve Cummings (36) winning UK's road race, and Svein Tuft Canadian's time trial (and he is 40). I love how Svein breaks the norms of traditional training, riding less but connecting more. On the other hand, I followed Western States 100 this last weekend, where you could witness fragility of young and "fit" ultras. I see it more and more what you, Barry, have been talking over and over—it takes time. People ("dietitians") say it doesn't work because everyone wants the quickest solution. Instead of walking barefoot on the grass, they do sauna training thinking they'll adapt to the sun's heat.
Anyway, just wanted so say thanks for standing tall, Barry, and going against the "mainstream."

### Barry — 2017-06-29T01:37:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217393>

> [HenrikB said:](/goto/post?id=217347)
>
> Barry,
> It's so great and encouraging to see your pro athletes/friends coming on the top this last weekend. Steve Cummings (36) winning UK's road race, and Svein Tuft Canadian's time trial (and he is 40). I love how Svein breaks the norms of traditional training, riding less but connecting more. On the other hand, I followed Western States 100 this last weekend, where you could witness fragility of young and "fit" ultras. I see it more and more what you, Barry, have been talking over and over—it takes time. People ("dietitians") say it doesn't work because everyone wants the quickest solution. Instead of walking barefoot on the grass, they do sauna training thinking they'll adapt to the sun's heat.
> Anyway, just wanted so say thanks for standing tall, Barry, and going against the "mainstream."

Thanks Henrik, really appreciate that. Its hard sticking to your guns so I'm glad you can see that.

Steve and Svein are prime examples of everything I have been practicing and preaching. They are first in battle and last out. Plus they have both simplified the complexity of everything they have to do in the pro cycling world.

People don't like when you go against the norm and don't behave like the rest of the herd. They want you to come back and adhere to their ways. I've just been stubborn and brave enough (or call it stupid) to stick to my ways and not listen to the herd.

The most challenging part is the timeframe like you pointed out. The connection of the dots takes several years. Nobody today, particularly in the athlete world, has got several years. They want to click a button and buy something shiny and then expect to take over the world.

The connected athlete is a different breed both physiologically and mentally...... I've got a few more in development.

Then we're going take over the world. Our world ;-)

### Barry — 2017-07-03T15:27:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217841>

The Rate Determining Step

there is no perfection in life. You can't have all the perfect dots joined in the most perfect way.

you can, if you are lucky, get what is the most important dot perfected.

I now don't always eat perfectly, CT perfectly, fast perfectly, train perfectly, earth perfectly, sleep perfectly etc

But I do, every morning, get outside in the summer sun for at least 2hrs.

And at the moment, I am running my fastest ever.

"in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

> "in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

"once flow begins, light enhances that flow" G.Pollack

a lot to be said there....

### Danco3636 — 2017-07-03T18:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217859>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=217841)
>
> The Rate Determining Step
>
> there is no perfection in life. You can't have all the perfect dots joined in the most perfect way.
>
> you can, if you are lucky, get what is the most important dot perfected.
>
> I now don't always eat perfectly, CT perfectly, fast perfectly, train perfectly, earth perfectly, sleep perfectly etc
>
> But I do, every morning, get outside in the summer sun for at least 2hrs.
>
> And at the moment, I am running my fastest ever.
>
> "in order for the adaptation to occur, the stimulus must resonate"

Great insights.
Though I still like my diet, fasting and things still dialed in (I just now enjoy and crave a seafood epi-paleo, keto based, seasonal diet) I too have found a profound effect from daily sun.

One thing I have found that really enhances and quantifies the effect of the sun is being barefoot. I crave and love being barefoot. Especially in the sun. My feet have really toughened up from me always going barefoot. I always feel better kicking off my shoes and even go barefoot in public.

And my feet stay in nice condition. They don't look all jacked up like you see some that go barefoot. My feel look better than most guys that wear shoes or flip-flops.

The more I connect the more profound the effect seems.

Kick the shoes off, let the sunshine in and add some movement.

### Barry — 2017-07-03T21:38:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-217869>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=217859)
>
> Great insights.
> Though I still like my diet, fasting and things still dialed in (I just now enjoy and crave a seafood epi-paleo, keto based, seasonal diet) I too have found a profound effect from daily sun.
>
> One thing I have found that really enhances and quantifies the effect of the sun is being barefoot. I crave and love being barefoot. Especially in the sun. My feet have really toughened up from me always going barefoot. I always feel better kicking off my shoes and even go barefoot in public.
>
> And my feet stay in nice condition. They don't look all jacked up like you see some that go barefoot. My feel look better than most guys that wear shoes or flip-flops.
>
> The more I connect the more profound the effect seems.
>
> Kick the shoes off, let the sunshine in and add some movement.

that all goes back a few pages to our "how do you know you are connected"...... so I'm with you there for sure

what's interesting is when you connect the dots and marry it up with Jacks work.... that's what has driven me to continue here over the past few years.

For example, from his latest Warburg blog:

_When mitochondria are stressed calcium comes rushing in to it. Why? It is trying to re-tune the inner mitochondrial membrane to oscillate at 100Hz so it can return to fat burning. When it cannot, mitochondrial stress increases, its size increases and heteroplasmy rates rise. Influx of mitochondrial calcium increases the rate of oxygen consumption to create a realtive pseudohypoxia at the tissue level because of the activation of calcium-activated dehydrogenases (GDH mentioned above), and by activating mitochondrial nitric oxide synthase (mtNOS), decreases the rates of oxygen consumption because nitric oxide is a competitive inhibitor of cytochrome oxidase.

Cells need mechanisms that ensure that the rate of formation of ATP matches tissue requirements for this molecule. In most mammalian cells, ATP is mainly formed through mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation. Changes in ATP synthesis (ATPase) can be completed through alterations in the activities of the mitochondrial dehydrogenases (**IR-A red light does this!**), which generate NADH and FADH for the respiratory chain. People do not realize IR-A light can replenish these things with no FOOD intake needed. Three of these dehydrogenases are of particular importance (i.e., pyruvate dehydrogenase, NAD+-dependent isocitrate dehydrogenase, and 2-oxoglutarate dehydrogenase) because they can be activated by increases in calcium, (NAD+)/(NADH) or (ADP)/(ATP) ratios. **nnEMF is well know to cause calcium imbalance in cells, especially mitochondria.
**_
So how do I marry that up with what I'm doing ?

I want to destress my mitchondria = go barefoot as much as possible

I want to generate NADH and FADH for my respiratory chain = get morning sunlight

Everything is simple when you know how. And the body is more clever than you think it is, if you let it be.

### Barry — 2017-07-18T13:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-219247>

Race Update

so my Wednesday races finished last week. These weren't ultra's.... they were ~10km mountain trail races.

These were 10km , high intensity, flat out running.

My highest placing was 3rd. My overall standing after 7 races was 5th. Typically 200 competing.

I haven't changed my diet or fasted state stuff.

So I was running faster than ever at a much higher intensity.

But guys like Chris MasterJohn don't think this is biochemically possible.

I have nothing personally against him and I was more of a biochemistry guy when I first started here. Thing is, I knew there was more to it. My inkling came from my doing. It's what I keep in referring to.... Skin in the Game.

" I have no responsibility to be like they expect me to be. It is their mistake, not my failing"

### drezy — 2017-07-31T23:25:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-220561>

Concise and very pertinent. I always like your Hemingway style.

If you'd let me take a swing at the dissonance I'd start with the fact that humans are social animals. I'd also say that having raised a kid, good old Lev Vygotsky was on to something when he surmised that:

> human psychological development emerges through interpersonal connections and actions with the social environment

If I were to keep my connections face to face, here is who I would have to choose from:

-   A father that is convinced I'll be giving myself cancer from sun exposure
-   A neighbor, on their 5th double knee replacement, also concerned about my UV exposure and very eager to discuss TV shows
-   Another neighbor that lives for politics whilst taking supplemental D for his major prostate issue at 49
-   Multiple coworkers pats and present who are either obese, alcoholic, or both.

I think you are way more connected/coupled than the majority here. I also think many, me included, require the social interaction to process all this. But if I can bend your question a little and hopefully keep it still on target, is the social interaction maybe sometimes used to avoid action? I think so.

In my own mother's shouting metaphorical words (while driving behind a highly indecisive driver when I was 5) "Shit or get off the pot!"

Race Update

so getting back to exercise and performance

2 weeks ago, I won my first ever "short" race. 7.5km, forest trail. 28.40mins. Flat out running.

almost exactly 2yrs ago, I won a 200km race in 26hrs.

Yet, I am still following my same principles. And they still say this can't be done.

I am just a bird that knows how to fly, while the others who have never flown lecture to me about how it should be done.

### Barry — 2017-08-26T21:39:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-222311>

I've got lots of saying and qoutes and statements I like...

this is one of my favourites:

"I want you to know that you're getting better.
I don't care what everyone else has been saying.
You're getting better.
They're the ones who are getting worse.
They don't like what you've been doing, understandably.
They wouldn't be saying what they've been saying, if they didn't want you to stop.
People want you to be the way they are, and to stay that way.
So don't get nervous and start asking for help, that's exactly what they want you to do.
Make it on your own, the way you've been doing."
