### Jack Kruse — 2017-09-07T16:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-223072>

> [peter jesop said:](/goto/post?id=223050)
>
> Ow and by the way.
>
> We have a free patreon account.
>
> Because beast things in life are for free

Life is too short to waste your time on people who don’t respect, appreciate, and value you.

When people question my value it immediately gives me the opportunity to let them test their hypothesis of seeing how life is without me in it. After all this is what they are implying.
Standing alone is better than being around people who don't value you. You have to cut people like this in your network when they tell you truly how they feel.
Great creativity is not price sensitive. I now spare my advice for those who value it, by using it.
If you do great work and charge what you must charge what you need for your time. Einstein or Bohr would have never filled in time sheets, nor did they do anything for free. People who question the price/value of dissemination of the first detailed foray into quantum biology belong in Monty Python sketches about lion tamers. Our task must be to free ourselves from poor thinking.........so if you think you can go at it alone, be my guest Peter. Just do not stand in my door way too long here on my free website and block the traffic.

Even if you cannot change all the people around you, you can change the people **_you choose to be around_**.

Sometimes you just need to distance yourself from people. If they care, they'll notice. If they don't, you’ll know where you stand with them and you'll allocate your time with them properly with how they value your information. A lesson here for mitochondriac's: Don't hesitate to walk away from those who fail to recognize your value and worth. You have to have skin in the game too to help yourself avoid energy vampires.

### HenrikB — 2017-09-15T05:47:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-223675>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=223608)
>
> We are killing ourselves with technology.... most people here know this
>
> I'm going to try and rebuild the athlete model.
>
> But I'm up against things like this:
>
> [https://en.enko-running-shoes.com/](https://en.enko-running-shoes.com/)

free shipping and your joints will thank you — what's not to like?!

I watched recently a Netflix movie "Icarus" and then read an interview with that movie director, [https://cyclingtips.com/2017/09/doping-documentary-interview-icarus-director-bryan-fogel/](https://cyclingtips.com/2017/09/doping-documentary-interview-icarus-director-bryan-fogel/)
When asked what did he notice regarding doping himself with all kinds of things, the guy responded that the biggest gain was ability to recover faster (so he could train harder more often).
What I've been learning (and experiencing) from you, Barry, and from Dr K and this site, is that connecting is super-charging yourself so effective I can literally feel that process as it's happening (so fast, in the matter of hours). It's so different than what is being preached to "stay off your feet; lay on a couch and watch TV; put some recovery boots and other crazy things, ending on get some EPO."

I've no doubt, you'll have success "rebuilding the athlete model" — there're plenty thirsty for that knowledge people.

Also, wanted to ask you, there's so much talk recently on Froome winning Vuelta after winning the Tour, what do you think? You know way more insights than a regular follower, but telling the truth, Froome comes as someone trustworthy. I know his team, Sky, while being over the top for the clean sport, done many shitty things with TUEs for example, and so Froome being part of it suffers that negative association ... well, I don't know really what to think when I look at it from the perspective that he's beating people that do drugs ... but then, what I said first in this post, if you do things right in life, supercharge has no limits that we know of. What do you think, if you don't mind sharing?

### Penny — 2017-09-28T00:12:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-224396>

Are we supposed to be fasted if we swim in the ocean in the early afternoon? Most of the time I am, but sometimes my feet lock up - and after that dumping potassium post about triathletes... I was assuming dehydration or lack of magnesium, but the water is heading down to 62 degrees - at the end of this current heat wave I've sure... so just didn't want to have heart failure out there I know, not funny - I'm also doing a lot of breath holding exercises - which is really easy to do in the cold... I think I have way more problems if I eat a bunch of carbs, then go in - it's textbook really -

### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-01T17:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-224603>

Barry here is a Patreon target for you: [https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fphys.2017.00806/abstract](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fphys.2017.00806/abstract)

### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-01T18:36:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-224611>

Here is another one to write about:
In this groundbreaking study of 33 professional and amateur cyclists, Peterson et al (2017) found a high abundance of the genus, Prevotella (≥2.5%) in their gut microbiomes which correlated significantly with total weekly exercise.

Its abundance was highest for cyclists reporting over 16 hours of exercise per week (14.75%) and lowest for those reporting 6–10 hours per week (0.15%). Prevotella was found to significantly correlate to multiple pathways involved in carbohydrate metabolism, and the metabolism of cofactors and vitamins (r = 0.6-0.8).

High volumes of low to moderate intensity endurance exercise may confer health benefits via our gut biomes as opposed to our muscles.

[https://www.dropbox.com/s/lmsmsm770nm7of0/AthletesGutBiome.pdf?dl=0](https://www.dropbox.com/s/lmsmsm770nm7of0/AthletesGutBiome.pdf?dl=0)

### sfast — 2017-10-04T10:13:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-224918>

@[Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/)

Did you read _Peak_ by Ericsson? Nice read and summarises his research on expertise. One part reminded me of you and the imperative of skin in the game:

\> And fourth, these fields have a subset of **performers who also serve as teachers** **and coaches** and who, over time have developed increasingly sophisticated sets of training techniques that make possible the field's steadily increasing skill level. (...) This joint development of skills and training techniques has -- up to now at least -- always been carried out through **trial and error**, with a **field's practitioners** experimenting with various ways to improve, keeping what works and discarding what doesn't. (p.86)

A fine read. And also could serve as a bird's eye perspective on how performance is developed.

### Barry — 2017-11-08T13:48:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-227562>

So this new study is doing the rounds now for the "keto" brand

**Keto-adaptation enhances exercise performance and body composition responses to training in endurance athletes [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0026049517302986](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0026049517302986)**

the keto bandwagon is bigging this study up...... I wish I could too, but that's not the case.

Firstly, they are pushing this "keto-adaptation" when the main physiological goal is beta oxidation. In other words, fat adaptation. Ketosis is just a side show. I think its Volek and Phinney that have coined the expression "keto-adaptation" and they want to hold on to it.

More importantly, its not the ketotic pathway an athlete needs to upregulate. Its all the other ones.... lipolysis, beta oxidation, cori-cycle, PPP, redox, and all pathways quantum which haven't even been studied yet. Apart from this, they would be probably better off doing some sort of "circadian rhythm adaptation" or "light adaptation" if they really wanted to improve athletic performance.

also, we are back to just poor research. I have some insider knowledge on this study. The testing was done at a university in Ireland. And lets just say the testing wasn't great. 12 weeks is nowhere near 24-36months as has been discussed here on this blog many times. No idea what foods they actually used with the athletes, or even adhered to. Just macro's stated. Then the researcher himself also doesn't know much about the in's and out's of biochemistry never mind connecting the dots.

Anyway, more exogenous keto supplements were probably sold..... so the ketards are happy

I want to put this here because I hop eBarry does a Patreon blog on it. Here is my take. Read this paper. [https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/st...fasting-may-be-center-of-increasing-lifespan/](https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2017/11/intermittent-fasting-may-be-center-of-increasing-lifespan/)

My comments:

Ferris and Patrick tell people coffee and tea are fine with IFing on a 16/8 hack. I don't. Why?

I would go the other way because insulin response is only one small factor and it is not foundational with energy as the focl point. It is the darling of the day for LCHF and PhD bio-chemists that is not as important the food guru's make it out to be. Why? They only take into account biochemical pathways and they are not even on the top of th elist of importance when you consiider quantum mechanisms in cells as the pic below shows. Be careful who you listen to on this page. Many parrots for the food guru's show up because they have those "experts" context and have no idea how cells really work.

Becker listened carefully to Charlie Bauman and ignored the PhD's in bio-chemistry at his medical school thank god.

This brings up a point about this paper. linked above......the comments above are trying to make food the key. What does the paper really say? A LACK of food is more critical. This is why Barry and I are entangled on this topic. That is what IFing is fundamental to wellness. Why? Because a lack of food shows you the underbelly of how cells really operate.
^^^the incident light EMF controls it all. Not only does it excite the electrons as the left of the slide shows but the light controls protons movements in those biochemical pathways below. It also makes the free radical signals from the incomplete oxidation of food stuffs and this is tied to the deuterium fractionation of what you are eating and the water you are drinking.
The most important source of H+ is water not food. This is why water does not screw up IFing unless it is got a ton of deuterium in it. But the source of fat you eat matters most in the food equation because of the photosynthetic mechanisms in the food web. Animals are natural deuterium depleters. The best depleters are in the marine web. The other part to get right: The diurnal Noble 2017 for medicine credo: Nature's kitchen’s always open when the sun is up, and she mandates it shuts down when the sun sets. Why? Red light is what controls the flow of H+ in the mitochondria. This is what the food guru is unaware of and it never is filtered in to experimental design or methods so they never see the effect in their data so they never get the proper insight.

The researchers found that restricting the worms’ diet, or mimicking dietary restriction through genetic manipulation of an energy-sensing protein called AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), maintained the mitochondrial networks in a fused or “youthful” state. In addition, they found that these youthful networks increased lifespan **by communicating with organelles called peroxisomes to modulate fat metabolism.

\--**

### Imogen Skye — 2017-11-08T18:59:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-227596>

Re: Thirst
Going back a bit on this page, it is my 2nd season of CT, this time no ice-bathing, just environmental adaptation (I live in the frigid north), and I also noticed that my thirst and water intake have drastically declined. Last yr with CT, it cut by half (6L/day to 3L), and now I'm down to 2L with some days even just a bit less. I'm just not thirsty and I am also not dehydrated, or at least I don't seem to be. I am now physically aware of the serious detriment from loss of light (I live at 60\*N until Nov 28 when I move "south" to 48\*N). I rush outside at blue light time to soak it in, and it actually feels like drinking cold water when parched, but through my eyes and into my whole body. I also have a TBI and some of my sensations are crossed, so that may be why, but the benefit is that I can actually feel it and, thereby, track it.

I am so very grateful for this forum and the work you do, Jack, and _everyone_ who contributes. Thank you all SO MUCH!!! Your generous sharing of information changed the direction of my life in every way. I now know what matters truly, for optimal health- or I'm learning progressively.

Its a massive brain melter along with shock and fear. To be honest, at the moment, I still think most people need baby steps on the biochemical/physiological pathways before they even get to ATPase tunneling and dueterium and superpositoin etc etc

get them hooked on something they know, then reel them in. What you're now fishing with is a bazooka

Well Barry I got a new blog for you to chew on today: "We live by a small trickle of electricity from the sun.” The green of our garden, the algae in our water, the trees, grasses and herbs on our lands are the transforming agents that harvest the sun’s light via the process of photosynthesis. ### JKLearner123 — 2017-11-12T13:02:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-227836>

Any thoughts on my general routine would be really appreciated. Thanks a lot.

\* AM; get up, out the door before the sun rises. Swim in cold sea water. "Make like the Sphinx" pose on shoreline. (All done on a 21 hour fasted stomach.)
\* Afterwards, eat and drink for 3 hours, then IF for the remaining 21 hours of each day. Try to maximise my sun exposure by eating outside. Lots of marine food. I do begin eating with quite a bit of fruit though.
\* When sun goes down, blue light blockers go on, and home lighting is \*very\* minimal.

(Rinse and repeat.)

Curiously, I'm finding that my Raynaud's syndrome symptoms are clearing up, which I'm very pleased about.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-11-13T15:28:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-227903>

Sounds good but you need to hack the plumbing in your house and office with Raynaud's. Why????
**YOUR DRINKING WATER AND PLUMBING:** The PVC pipes we use to carry drinking water are a polymer of vinyl chloride—one of the most biotoxic substances known to man. In the very first study about the dangers of vinyl chloride, published by Patty in 1930, it was disclosed that exposure of test animals to just a single short-term high dose of vinyl chloride caused liver damage. The damage in the liver likely affects how you can recycle protons in the key 3 metabolic pathways. This effects how deuterium is placed into ribose so it could effect epigenetic programming and mitochondrial function to lead to some agressive diseases.

In 1997 the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) concluded that the development and acceptance by the PVC industry of a closed loop polymerization process in the late 1970s "**almost completely eliminated worker exposures**" and that "new cases of hepatic angiosarcoma in vinyl chloride polymerization workers have been **virtually eliminated**." The liver is a hydrgeon furnace and it really re-creates the sun in our body when you understand it. Wallace has several video where actually says this and agree with my theory.

Monomer leaching is possible and Vinyl chloride can have acute dermal and ocular effects. Dermal exposure effects are thickening of skin, edema, decreased elasticity, local frostbites, blistering, and irritation. The complete loss of skin elasticity expresses itself in **Raynaud’s Phenomenon.** The US EPA and FDA limit vinyl chloride in drinking water to 0.002 ppm. Chronic exposure leads to common forms of respiratory failure (emphysema, **pulmonary fibrosis)** and focused hepatotoxicity (hepatomegaly, **hepatic fibrosis**). Continuous exposure can cause CNS depression including euphoria and disorientation. Most drinking water pipes are made with PVC in modern home construction. Consider [findaspring.com](http://findaspring.com) or use bottled spring water.

One of the most challenging aspects has been adapting to a ketogenic diet plan for the row which means ditching carbohydrates and sugars and focusing on eating as much fat as we can. I’m a bit of a foodie so this has been a real struggle! Having the odd bag of pork scratchings at the pub as a naughty snack is all very well but when you are force feeding yourself 3 packets a day between meals or **whole blocks of cheddar**, it’s slightly less enjoyable! That said, when we arrive in Antigua and plough into burgers and chips, it will be worth it!

### Barry — 2018-06-20T00:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-243825>

I've said it before and I'll say it again, we are drowning in knowledge but starving of wisdom

I think this sums up a lot in terms of where we are with modern human health and human performance. This lack of wisdom comes as a result of time, not enough of it. As time allows natural development through trail and error. Time also allows more intellectual study of the topic area and a more diverse spread of opinion and interpretation. Today we don't give time to things so we don't apply enough trail and error, the spread of opinion and interpretation is narrow, and the length of intellectual study is short. It also means that real life experiences are fewer. This means that real time observations are less. We therefore do not endure mistakes and nor suffer the consequences from them. In other words, we don't learn the hard way.

But the data and the knowledge is there, so it must be right ?

Must it ?

I say no. And I am saying this from experience.

I think what the human body needs in terms of health and performance is best understood through wisdom. This comes down to the workings of time but also from something else I have mentioned before, Skin in the Game.

I keep coming back to this the more knowledge and data that is thrown about by experts and guru's.

The very book, Skin in the Game, has a chapter that sums it all up very nicely in one go. Maybe those that swear by their knowledge and data need to read it. It goes like this:

Antaeus was a giant, or rather a semi-giant of sorts, the literal son of Mother Earth, Gaea, and Poseidon, the god of the sea. He had a strange occupation, which consisted of forcing passersby in his country, Libya, to wrestle; his thing was to pin his victims to the ground and crush them. This macabre hobby was apparently the expression of filial devotion; Antaeus aimed at building a temple to his father, Poseidon, using for raw material the skulls of his victims.

Antaeus was deemed to be invincible, but there was a trick. He derived his strength from contact with his mother, Earth. Physically seperated from contact with Earth, he lost all his powers. Hercules, as part of his twelve labors, had for homework to whack Antaeus. He managed to lift him off the ground and terminated him by crushing him as his feet remained out of contact with his mother, Earth.

We retain from this first vignette that, just like Antaeus, you cannot seperate knowledge from contact with the ground. Actually, you cannot separate anything from contact with the ground. And the contact with the real world is done via skin in the game – having exposure to the real world, and paying the price for its consequences, good or bad. The abrasions on your skin guide your learning and discovery, a mechanism of organic signaling, what the Greeks call _pathemata mathemata_ (“guide your learning through pain”) something mothers of children know very well. I have shown in _Antifragile_ that most things that we believe were “invented” by universities were actually discovered by tinkering and later legitimized by some type of formalization. The knowledge we get by tinkering, via trail and error, experience, and the workings of time, in other words, _contact with the earth_, is vastly superior to that obtained through reasoning , something self-serving institutions have been very busy hiding from us.

### sfast — 2018-08-24T15:29:33+0300

In case you missed it: I L Fahrenholtz, A Sjödin, D Benardot, Å B Tornberg, S Skouby, J Faber, J K Sundgot-Borgen, and A K Melin (2018): Within-day energy deficiency and reproductive function in female endurance athletes, Scand J Med Sci Sports 3, 2018, Vol. 28, S. 1139-1146.

I use the within day deficit to track the intensity of fasting. I think that it is useful just up to a point where intermittent fasting meets just fasting (I suppose they meet at ~36h of just water).

### Barry — 2018-12-21T12:52:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-258624>

Taken from my Patreon..... read between the lines...
**
Would you like to know why Quantum beer is good for you ?**

Let me explain:

Did you know that hops contain alpha acids called Humulones ?. During the brewing process, humulone degrades to cis and trans isohumulones. These iso-alpha acids survive the boiling process but many oxidized derivitives are formed. What this causes is a a massive increase in the proton-motive force. This is what drives our most important nanomachine, ATP synthase.

The increase in the proton motive forces improves the chemiosmotic coupling. Isohumulones thus increase the electrical potential of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Electrons are shuttled from respiratory complex I, III and IV and this electron current powers the extrusion of protons (H+) through the three respiratory complexes. However, the cis from of the isohumulone from beer with a high hop content, provides more readily available H+ protons . Thus as each pair of electrons passes down the respiratory chain, 12 protons instead of 10 are pumped at complex I, III and IV. The flux of protons back through the ATP synthase drives the synthesis of ATP from ADP and phosphate.

more ATP = more Energy.

So drink more beer !

right ?

well sorry to ruin it all for you but, no. not right. I just made all that up. Most of the terms and the mechanisms I refer to are real, I learnt them from a book called "The Vital Question" by Nick Lane..... but the story and how isohumulones work was just fabricated by me.

I just wanted to highlight something called **"The Illusion of Knowledge"** .

I believe we are riddled with it today in the health and fitness world. Knowledge you see is easy to gain these days. You can read and study anything you want online. You can even get access to science journals and do as much of your own research as you like. What you can then do is write something very scientific and even 90% valid to give the illusion of knowledge. Do this enough times and people start believing anything you say or write. I've observed this happen with the "keto" movement over the last few years. There is a huge amount of some very good information, very believable and even valid... or lets say sort of valid. Off the back of all that came the keto supplements which is a multi-million dollar industry now. But ultimately it is all an illusion of knowledge built to control the consumer.

Its actually quite difficult to avoid this illusion of knowledge strategy especially when something is written in very complex language and actually contains lots of complex science.

If you are very studious and do enough of your own reading and research, you might uncover some flaws that would cause you to raise your eyebrow.

However, I like to use two simple heuristic techniques. One is time. Whatever it is, see how long the recommendation or advice has been in existence. Time will tell whether it is something that has been tried and tested. The other is Skin in the Game. Find out if the author has taken risk and applied whatever it is they are talking about, in the field. Even find out if the object or strategy or recommendation has skin in the game. Something can sound intellectual but still be idiotic if it hasn't been used in real life under real conditions.

So there is no such thing as Quantum beer I'm afraid. Its been consumed for hundreds of years and people still get drunk and even the brewers who make it, get drunk ;-)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-21T13:32:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-258628>

**"The Illusion of Knowledge"**

Wisdom and knowledge are not synonyms. Those who seek wisdom must seek many truths to gain insight. They must be tenacious. Knowledge is a part of ignorance that man arranges and classify. Knowledge has the shelf life as fish do in a market. It is not sustainable and does not always lead to truth. If knowledge is information, wisdom is the understanding and application of that knowledge and insight is the awareness of the underlying essence of a truth.

Sadly we can gain a lifetime of knowledge, yet never see the wisdom in it. We can be wise but still miss the deeper meaning of facts. Today medicine is a good example of this.

**_There are many who know things among us but lacking in wisdom._** Your mentors should be filled with wisdom because they will be the connectors of life.

Their insight will be indefatigable for you. **Not even ‘father time’ can destroy the natural truth of Mother Nature.**

Knowledge is knowing a human life is roughly 80 years.
Wisdom is saving enough resources to navigate that journey
Insight is building a life with people around you at 35 years to sustain the onslaught of the journey

Knowledge is measuring that a desert path is 12.4 miles long.
Wisdom is packing enough water for the hike.
Insight is building a lemonade stand at mile 6.

Knowledge is knowing how to manage your money, budgeting, spending, saving.
Wisdom is understanding how money impacts the quality of your life and your future.
Insight is realizing that money is simply a tool to be used, that it has no inherent meaning beyond its usefulness.

Knowledge is learning how to paint and using that skill to cultivate a livelihood.
Wisdom is expressing your passion through painting and understanding that art is a form of communication that touches the lives of others.
Insight is perceiving that all things can be art and that creating your art contributes to the understanding and the expression of the essence of the world around you.

Knowledge is knowing which things, practices, people, and pleasures make you happy.
Wisdom is knowing that while those things may bring you pleasure, happiness is not derived from things or situations or people. It’s understanding that happiness comes from within and that it’s a temporary state of mind.
Insight is knowing that happiness is not the purpose of life, that it’s not the marker of the quality of life—it’s merely one of the many fleeting states of mind in the spectrum of full emotions. Those emotions don’t make up our lives; they are merely experiences.

Knowledge, wisdom, and insight all are valuable and all have a place in our lives. The difficulty lies in the fact that many of us are unclear as to their differences, often perceiving the terms and their application to be interchangeable. Being clear and consciously aware of how our minds are engaged may be important to getting the most out of all three. While acquiring and applying information is valuable in and of itself, we also need to distill and judge that information, and ultimately find the deeper meaning and relevance to the whole of our lives. Perhaps the truest form of knowing is in acquiring all three, and understanding how they each enhance the quality and experience of life.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-12-23T00:42:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-258792>

**FASTED TRAINING WISDOM ALERT:** You're not hungry in the morning \*because\* you ate most of your food later in the day the day before. Try skipping dinner. You'll be hungry for breakfast the next morning I promise.

Exercising while fasted induces adaptations to mitochondria in muscle and adipose tissue including increased fatty acid metabolism that is blunted by pre-exercise feeding (meta-analysis of 46 clinical studies to show you the veracity). [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29315892](https://t.co/XBWwVCaFrc)

### Barry — 2019-02-05T22:16:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262360>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=213876)
>
> okay, saving the best for last...... being connected:
>
> \- you can tell the time without wearing a watch
>
> \- you prefer the suffering in every aspect of life, acute that is.
>
> \- you are drawn to the color purple
>
> \- you cannot hold your smartphone in your hand for too long
>
> \- eating a "light" meal never really happens. You either eat well or you don't
>
> \- each day, you find it more and more difficult to live in the modern world
>
> \- your sport is not done for numbers, distances asthetic purposes or even accomplisments. The priority for you is simply to be outdoors in nature
>
> Finally, this for me is how it all clicks together:
>
> you meet someone, and become close to them, be it a friend, a spouse, a relation or a neighbour...... and you find out that they have never heard of Becker, Ling, Pollack, Lane, Marino..... or even Kruse or Murray...
>
> yet, they are doing almost all the shit I have just listed without even thinking about it
>
>
> I rest my case.

To rekindle this discussion.

Winter here in Ireland.... two big things I notice

\- dark cloudy days change my mood detrimentally, any sort of brightness/sunlight/blue skies has the opposite affect

\- being indoors for longer than 30mins, in an office type environment , has a negative affect on me . The minute I walk outside, I feel better

It seems the more connected you become with nature, the more difficult it is to live in the modern world

### Inger — 2019-02-06T10:47:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262391>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=262360)
>
> It seems the more connected you become with nature, the more difficult it is to live in the modern world

I totally know what you mean.....

I thought about why this is.

My guess is, because when we know how "good" feels, and so we also feel it fast when "good" disappears (through too much time indoor etc).

The same might apply to food, cigarettes and so on. I have smoked on and off in my life and when I did it with very much awareness, the quitting and starting again, I could feel a subtle difference in my "good/wellbeing" feeling. It disappeared when I smoked. So I could not ignore it how it distroyed me. It was very subtle, no real symptoms but it was there.

I feel the same about food... If I am very aware of how things make me feel it becomes a guide to what is good for me or not.

I guess the same goes for everything... also people we connect with.....

So fascinating getting more and more aware about ones feelings, as subtle as they are

I had to work yesterday, indoors, and it was sun outside. I had to tell myself in the car driving to work,

_Hey do not be sad. The sun WILL come out again! Do not worry, it always comes. You can do this. You are a strong girl!
_
I just felt so bad about not being able to be naked in the sun, but having to work indoors instead I almost wanted to cry...lol (or turn back home and tell the boss I could not come today  )
I did manage to get about 20 minutes of naked tanning before leaveing though.. but I craved way more. Felt sooo wrong, having to put on clothes and leave, huh.

### Danco3636 — 2019-02-06T15:20:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262399>

>
> \- being indoors for longer than 30mins, in an office type environment , has a negative affect on me . The minute I walk outside, I feel better
>
> It seems the more connected you become with nature, the more difficult it is to live in the modern world

I hear yea. Being in doors makes me anxious. I feel like a caged animal. Once outside it’s a breath of fresh air - freedom. Even on the cold rainy days it feels good just to be out. But when the sun hits amazing. I feel like I need to run to the sun. I go outside in the cold and just let the rays hit me.

I find I drink more water when I am inside and less when I’m outside.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-06T19:22:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262417>

Winter in New Orleans is pretty good. 77F here and I am swimming.

Buds are already on trees here.

### Barry — 2019-02-07T17:18:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262495>

Thats a good summers day here in Ireland Jack.

But it seems the main inhibitor of being outdoors is work . Although I like what I do, its 90% indoors, online.

When you live where I live, you see simple outdoor jobs... farmers, fishermen, roofers, painters, builders. The irony of life is that they look at the indoor office type job as some sort of "ideal" or "dream" .... is it a case of the grass is always greenr ?

big part of me looking at ways of redirecting my career or combining it with something more outdoors

### Bob Stirling — 2019-02-07T18:46:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262502>

I found that self-employment and diversification worked well. Four or 5 good skills gave the opportunity to move around and do different things, inside and outside, in all seasons. It also helped in economic down-turns. I can't imagine doing just one thing.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-02-08T02:23:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262542>

Barry I have been redirecting my life toward outside now for 15 years. It is not easy but must be done.

### Barry — 2019-02-08T12:41:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-262579>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=262542)
>
> Barry I have been redirecting my life toward outside now for 15 years. It is not easy but must be done.

Its arguably the biggest challenge we have. You can know all about PPP, DDW, EMF, CCG, etc etc

but if you are not spending the main part of your life outdoors.... then its a case of "I'd rather be dumb and antifragile, then extremely smart and fragile" ... or simply being a doer rather than a talker

Bob makes a good point ... diversify... even if I have to maintain a 2 day per week office type job... it would be better with 3 days outdoors

### Barry — 2019-02-20T16:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-263624>

Apart from the general public spending more time inside than out... it is happening with athletes who now opt to train more indoors than outdoors.

There are many causes, one is the advancement of technology and the other is the drive of fear factor in order to control society. Then there is the comfort zone that we continue to make bigger as consumers. So we have fancy computerized training equipment and we have the fear in our veins and the lack of ability or motivation to go outside of our comfort zone.

Think of a cyclist... who for most of his/her life has trained outdoors all year round. However, now, they have a Zwift or a Wahoo. These are indoor bike trainers linked to a computer screen that gives you a "virtual" training experience.

You can do the same with a treadmill. What it allows then is for you to live in a city, but ride up the Alps or run in some exotic place. It also allows you to race other "virtual" trainers.

First, let me outline what massively important adaptations you miss out on if you don't train outdoors:

\- Sunlight and Daylight which control basically everything in your body

\- Grounding/Magnetism

\- Cold Exposure

\- Zeitgebers - Circadian Rhythm

\- Quantum biology effects

\- Balance of modern stressors i.e. blue light and nnEMF are now everywhere , just training outside at least subtracts some of this exposure.

These adaptations each have a huge impact beneficially in terms of performance and health. I have already written about some of them in depth and we have discussed them in the podcast.

So if you train indoors, despite doing what you consider to be a hard programmed session with your power measured and climbs and even virtual racing.... what you have missed out on is far more significant.

What is even more disturbing is how us homosapiens are able to justify our choices to convince ourselves that we are doing the right thing.

have a read of this: [https://www.bicycling.com/training/a20043122/why-i-love-to-zwift/](https://www.bicycling.com/training/a20043122/why-i-love-to-zwift/)

I've been reading the latest book by Yuval Noah Harari - "21 lessons for the 21st Century". He makes a very good point about modern times - "we are upgrading our computers but downgrading humans".

Likewise, I think we are upgrading our training equipment but downgrading the athlete.

If you don't get sunlight, don't get cold, don't feel the earth, don't hear the wind or feel the rain..... you are losing control of your circadian rhythm, raising inflammation, deactivating neurotransmitters and not expressing certain genes. That's just some of the things.

I have stated this point from the very beginning, its on my "about" page.

"I want to help make you a better athlete, the current model is flawed."

Continued........on The Connected Athlete [https://www.patreon.com/connectedathlete/posts](https://www.patreon.com/connectedathlete/posts)

### Solidsilverteeth — 2019-05-11T11:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-270182>

Boil it all down to fewest possible words. This is a typical days routine that can be adapted to your own personal situations.

The body likes routine. Getting used to the same times. Having got to sleep around 10pm, you can wake fully refreshed and rested around sunrise.

Head straight outside as soon as wake up wearing as little as possible, get down on the ground skin touching the earth start doing 100 burpees. Build up a sweat and lay on your back looking up at the sunrise breathing correctly.

Next have a cold water shower outside in the sun. Dry off have a good bowel movement about 8am then get dressed and go to work or whatever you do. Loop all your metabolic water all day. Try to be outside as much as possible look at the sun as much as you can through the day. Fast only looping orin all day until 5pm consume your OMAD until 6pm.

Watch the sunset, and from then on wear blue blocking red glasses until 10 when you go to sleep inside a silver faraday canopy.

### kris90 — 2020-01-02T19:29:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-284152>

> [ssj3 said:](/goto/post?id=129392)
>
> This reads very similar to my experience.
>
> I had survived for years on a keto diet (eventually a story for my optimal journal to put in context) but not when supporting high volume/intensity/density weight training. But when I switched to a keto diet again, I didn’t adjust for many factors (loss of calories specifically one - I actually wanted to test the calories don’t matter spin) and training really suffered. Also, my labs tanked, couldn’t sustain let alone add muscle, body comp worsened and I was starting to have to take days off due to poor recovery.
>
> Now about 8 months in, consuming a lot more fat, a few more carbs (about 50-70 grams a day) and about 800-1000 calories per day more, things are starting to turn good. Leaner with weight up about 4kg. Now I don’t know if this is just the calories as I did start doing other things like eating oysters or muscles nearly daily, infrared therapy, 8-10 hours of sleep, etc.
>
> Still some bio-hacking to do as recovery and ability to maintain intensity still needs to improve, but I like where this is going.

This has been my experience also. I have found CT/keto adapted works wonders for staying slim as if hibernating and maximizing autophagy. Keeps hunger levels down so fasting becomes a breeze. However, while it's helped keep me lean, when it comes to lifting heavy (strength training), I'd tend to plateau. Since then I've significantly increasing my caloric intake, and upped protein and the gains have been ridiculous. I still keep carbs low (50g or under on training days). I've gained about 15 lbs in just under 3 months, and have made some incredible strength gains. My moods have improved and I definitely feel like my T levels have surged, although I have not measured. I have to forcefeed myself most of the time, but I am staying lean and putting on mass. These are gains I normally wouldn't have made naturally (I have a history of AAS use in my bodybuilding days) and I've been accused of juicing again.

The only thing I have to ensure is that I rest long enough between sets to replenish ATP. That's usually 3 mins minimal, and potentially up to 6-8 mins between heavy sets of squats. I'm also modifying my volume as my intensity creeps up. I may experiment with some nicotine pre-workout and see how that works out for me.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-09-11T18:46:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-326243>

**TODAY'S LESSON:** POMC arose over 500 million years ago by the insertion of the melanocortin sequences into a prepro-endorphin gene. Evidence for this comes from structural identities with other opioid precursors in both the NH2- and COOH-terminal regions of POMC.
The phenomena of pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC) as a hormone precursor emerged gradually over time as observations slowly filled in pieces of the puzzle. Long before the concept of hormone precursors was realized, the bronzed skin color described by Addison in his patient with adrenal insufficiency (“melasma suprarenale”) gave perhaps the first hints of a connection between the hypothalamic, pituitary, adrenal (HPA) axis and skin color. A similar link between the pituitary and pigmentation came from the studies of Allen in 1916 and Smith in 1916 who both noted that immersing tadpoles in pituitary extract made their skins darker. In humans, too, large doses of the porcine pituitary extract also appeared to cause pigmentation in 1954, with this active extract of the pars intermedia of the pituitary henceforth termed “melanocyte-stimulating hormone” or MSH.
In 1932, Cushing extended his clinical reports of a polyglandular syndrome caused by basophilic adenomas of the pituitary by linking this finding with adrenal hyperactivity. In the 1930s, work by Ingle and Kendall in the 1930s showed that administration of large amounts of “cortin,” a purified adrenal extract, produced atrophy of the adrenal cortex in rats. Importantly, they found that administration of the “adrenotropic principle” of the anterior pituitary was effective in preventing adrenal cortical regression following treatment with cortin. The first hints of a behavioral angle to POMC biology came from studies by Ferrari in the 1950s, when “stretching-yawning syndrome,” a bizarre crisis of muscular tone, occurred following the central administration of MSH. Many other studies assessing the effects of central α-MSH on motivational processes followed, but it was not until 1976 that Panskepp observed for the first time that this peptide decreased food intake using light alone. What else might POMC do in a modern world run by artificial light now? Today, you get to see another perspective centralized science missed.

This highlights why blaming diet is a losing proposition when it is light behind the effect. This mistake is made by many on this forum even the ones who are members.

> or could it be even 2 x times effect ?
>
> I don't have any research on this but if anyone does, please chime in. What do you reckon Jack ?

I think the concept that could at least map both FST and just fasting is "Within Day Deficit".

### Cicero106 — 2024-04-08T16:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fasted-state-training-adaptations.297/post-333398>

> [Barry said:](/goto/post?id=3494)
>
> Ninja - eating breakfast is only important if LR or have other hormonal imbalances. In terms of post training, thats where I consume my daily carbs and varying it depending on training is important e.g.
>
>
>
> Long Endurance Session - 3-5hr run/cycle
>
>
>
> Immediately after: ~50g CHO, fruit/honey with whey protein (in the summer of course ;-)
>
>
>
> HIIT session:
>
>
>
> Immediately after: protein only
>
>
>
> Important to note: you need to be keto adapted for this to work. Possibly also when adapting (remember it takes 24-36month to full adapt), its good to apply a cyclical ketogenic approach. Lyle Mcdonald has a good book on this.
>
>
>
> With all recommendations here, its important to put things in context. I have been keto adapting and doing big fasted state training for 3 years now. Its been a gradual process. My body has adapted. I used to do the same volume of training on 350-450g CHO daily.. now I'm at 50g, sometimes less but my fat intake is now huge !
>
>
>
> hope that helps

I don’t know if you will get this since your discussion was 12 years ago, but I could not find the explanation to you statement that it takes 2-3 years to fully keto adapt in the forum, so I wanted to ask if you could clarify or maybe point me to the information, although seems a little bit late for me to ask you.

Ill be doing CT while on hcg, ive done only 2 days so far, im sticking to protocol, the body is highly sensitive with hcg in it and i dont want to mess by adding oils and such, knowing what hcg already does bc of the low cals and specific foods, i just trust it, sure you might lose still but risking the hormones by adding off protocol foods is deffinately a risk, so as for hcg+CT go we'll see, ive seen posts of people on my facebook who now have problems stabilizing, or thyroid who have done rogue protocols, even hcg users who have done up to several rounds and have gotten their hands on proper info now going back to basics, i say if you want to do CT while on protocol see what happens, but as for adding off protocol things no, hcg is simply not a diet its hormonal treatment with newer information by Robin Woodall who has newer info than simeons and has been treating many patients, once again, if you want "diet" results get off hcg and do LR or simply hcg.

### rbu5@sssnet.com — 2012-03-20T02:58:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3053>

I'll report in as a guinea pig

I've been on hcg for 9 days now and have lost about 6.5#'s. Wierd thing this time was I lost during loading so now I'm not showing the huge first week losses as the usual. I think my body was so used to the lots of fats that it didn't blink when I ate all those avocados and steaks on load days. LOL

Doing the timing of Leptin Rx by eating one pc of protein within 30 minutes of waking. Nothing in between meals and 4 - 5 hrs in between. I have my veg at noon and then for dinner my protein and veggie. No fruit, since I do no sugar anyway as a rule while eating paleo and no glutin so no melba. So far so good. I have been trying to get more serious about my Cold Therapy, but like most that are hypothryoid and doing hcg, I'm not liking the cold so much. I know once I get cold adapted in a few weeks the weight will hopefully really move. I have about 25 to 30#'s I would love to lose for good and hopefully in this one 45 day round! This is my fourth go round with hcg in the last 2 yrs. I've lost about 25 to 30 pounds each time and have gained it back again.

Will be interesting to see how everyone stabilizes with doing CT and LRx with Hcg.

\[h=2\]\[/h\]

### SuzieQ — 2012-03-20T03:01:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3056>

I am doing HCG and CT... I am one week in.

I am following protocol but have omitted fruit and the bread sticks.

I am increasing my proteins to make up for the lessor calories. Keeping it still around 500 a day.

I am eating just 2 meals a day. Breakfast and dinner (between 3-5pm)

I am drinking green & yerbamate tea throughout the day and one cold-dripped coffee a day.

I am doing spot CT'ing around my complete middle section 1-2x a day and 30 minutes a night in the pool up to my waist.

I have been doing CT since early Feb. and I do find it harder to "want" to do it during HCG because I am usually more chilly on this protocol. I have done HCG 3 x before. Struggled with stabilizing. I only have 10 to lose but I wanted to see if CT would help with the stabilizing part. I have hormone and thryoid issues in the midst of all this.

If you have any further suggestions as to how to make this the perfect successful round... I am all ears! Teach me!

### rbu5@sssnet.com — 2012-03-20T03:07:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3062>

> **August;3064 wrote: Oh, while I was looking for the passage re: feeling cold . . I found this, which I thought was interesting: (under the
>
>
> > Dr Simeons said:
> >
> > **The Exhaustion of the Fat-bank**
> >
> > But there is still a third way in which obesity can become established, and that is when a presumably normal fat-center is suddenly-the emphasis is on suddenly-called upon to deal with an enormous influx of food far in excess of momentary requirements. At first glance it does seem that here we have a straight-forward case of overeating being responsible for obesity, but on further analysis it soon becomes clear that the relation of cause and effect is not so simple. In the first place we are merely assuming that the capacity of the fat center is normal while it is possible and even probable that the only persons who have some inherited trait in this direction can become obese merely by overeating.
> >
> > Secondly, in many of these cases the amount of loud eaten remains the same and it is only the consumption of fuel which is suddenly decreased, as when an athlete is confined to bed for many weeks with a broken bone or when a man leading a highly active life is suddenly tied to his desk in an office and to television at home. Similarly, when a person, grown up in a cold climate, is transferred to a tropical country and continues to eat as before, he may develop obesity because in the heat far less fuel is required to maintain the normal body temperature.
>
> **
>
> > Dr Simeons said:
> >
> > Source: Pounds and Inches
>
>
>
> Whoa August, very interesting find. I never read this before and will go check out my copy of P & I. Thanks!

### LisaAPB — 2012-03-20T05:35:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3134>

I am on my 4th round of hcg, took a long break between 3 & 4 to do the Leptin Rx. I had hoped I could lose on Leptin Rx but that was not the case, but I was stable as a rock and didn't gain more than 2 lbs. When I read the first CT blog I decided to amp up my cold shower, which I'd been doing off and on for about a year, for totally different reasons! I started hcg mid Feb, and lost 10 lbs before doing a 3 day interruption, I am back on now for the last week and then I will move to a Leptin Rx style P3. I am eating 650-750 calories per day. My typical rate of loss averaged out to be .5 per day, and I am still at about that level.

The first week I followed protocol pretty strictly, the second week I started adding cream in my coffee and cut all fruit... losses did slow down about them. For this last week I am cutting the cream and adding back in some limited fruit. I have not experienced one day of crazy hunger like I did in previous rounds... I always had at least one day that was challenging. I'm cold whether I do CT or not, so i figure I might as well do the CT because I feel really good afterwards, and notice when I am warming. I get really warm in the middle of the night and kick the covers off.

### Pamela — 2012-03-20T06:48:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3155>

I've done a few rounds of HCG and am now just finishing my 3rd round. The first round, last year at this time, I lost 20 lbs and within the first 3 days of being on Stage 3 I had a gallbladder attack that took me to the hospital. At that time I knew nothing of Paleo, Primal etc. so had the GB removed and while it could have been salvaged I still am glad I had it out. After several attacks I couldn't take it any more. Then I did another round of HCG last summer and lost another 16 lbs. In Sept of last year is when I found Leptin reset so I tried it but gained weight. Was not happy. Has a physical and found thyroid antibodies high so am now on Armour but only .5 grain.

Now, I am at the very end of the 3rd round and at 160 lbs, 5"9 female. Would still like to lose another 10 lbs but I don't think it will be on HCG. I'm hoping that with the thyroid it will help stabilize my weight. I was waiting to finish the round before doing CT since Dr. K said to have a high fat meal while doing it. So this next week I will start even though I hate cold. Good thing is it is no longer winter here in Chicago and I can deal with the idea of cold water better.

I'm a little leary of jumping to the reset right off of HCG. Colleen would you recommend this?

Colleen and Jack

### colleencoble — 2012-03-20T19:57:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3388>

We've had tons of people on the forum lose successfully after surgery. My cousin also. The main problem you will have is after the round. Many surgery patients have trouble getting down enough meat after the surgery. They fill up too fast to get down the meat and fats they need. So that may be a challenge for you.

Hi, I am new here and have been reading this forum for a few days now with great interest. I did a round of HCG a year ago and lost 38 lbs and about 28 inches all over, dropped 4 sizes in clothes and thought this was the best thing ever. I kept the weight off over a three week vacation with no trouble and through the next month. After that I noticed the weight and mostly the inches came back on gradually but over the next four months I gained all but five pounds back! Grrr, not a happy camper. On Feb. 25, 2012 I decided to do another round of HCG while doing CT (ice packs on stomach for that nasty belly fat). Two weeks in I lost 12 lbs but not without feeling starved the entire time and angry that I couldn't eat and felt horrible. On March 10th (the last day of being 49 yo) I gave up HCG and started the Leptin Rx instead. Loved that I could eat again and felt wonderful. I'm now 10 days in and haven't lost any inches, my weight has gone up one, down one, up one, down one, etc. for the last 10 days. I'm considering going back on HCG and doing CT but increasing my protein and very little veggies. Is this going to screw up my brain and any progress I am making that I may not see yet? Sorry this was kind of long, thank you for any advice I can get!

### colleencoble — 2012-03-21T00:13:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3570>

I don't need brand name.  That's the typical dose but it often needs lowered with each round. Try skipping your hcg tomorrow and see how you feel by evening.

Paleowanna, if that's mixed the way doctors often do, that's 200 iu and too high for most people so that's why you were hungry.  If you do it again, we'll talk about dosing. I would start you on 150 and you might need to go down from there.

### colleencoble — 2012-03-21T00:39:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3587>

Paleowanna, that's a mistake most doctors make. They don't seem to have read the part in Pounds and Inches about too much causing the reverse effect. We see it all the time on the big forum. What exactly are you eating now?

Suzie, by afternoon and into the evening, you should be feeling no hunger. If that's the case take the dose to .4. If you have hunger and it continues into the evening, then the dose is too low and you go up to .6.

I was thrilled to get to 30 minutes today with my cold baths. It has been a long slow process for me. I will be starting HCG round two in about a week and a half with cold baths in place. I bathe twice a day anyway and at this point 30 minutes is all i got. When I started I could only manage 10 minutes but I did it 3 times a day. I am throwing off a lot of heat and I really think it is working. I have stabilized well. Leptin reset and cold baths.

While on HCG I never ate starch and had one fruit and an extra protein. By day 30 of last HCG round I dropped the other fruit and added 2 proteins in the form of a morning steak (grass fed fillet mignon) and added cold training. I lost well as much the last week as the first week.

Good luck!

### colleencoble — 2012-03-21T05:52:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3786>

Joanne, you keep mentioning her book. Are you friends with her or something? Actually any weight loss protocol can result in gall bladder issues. Our "bible' is Pounds and Inches not her book.

### colleencoble — 2012-03-21T06:12:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3793>

it's a common misconception that you are supposed to do high fat loading days. That's not really what the protocol states. He lists lots of things, not just fatty foods. Pastries, chocolate, and bread for example. Which of course we will never do.  Well maybe the chocolate!

Pounds and Inches says this: Small stones in the gall bladder may in patients who have recently had typical colics cause more frequent colics under treatment with HCG. This may be due to the almost complete absence of fat from the diet, which prevents the normal emptying of the gall bladder. '

So basically, it can cause more problems when there is an existing problem.

### JoanneA — 2012-03-21T16:26:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3887>

Hey Colleen, i mention Robins book because it is a secind hypothisis to hcg with further stydy, where alot if Simeons stuff is out dated, yet never dis credits Simeons. The study if Leptin only came into play i believe in 1994 so although Simeons had observed, used curcumference, and the process of elimination, and knew hcg worked, Robins info is some "more" information based on much study as well as observing over 1000s of patients, calories in calories out is no longer aplicable now that we know about Leptin. Ive read pounds and inches a few times and scanned it over a dozen, but now Robin was able to make more connections to the whys of some of these things (such as gallbladder) seeing it in her own studies with people, yet in her own words "more research is needed" So in the end since Simeons who we are all greatful for, we need to look into the newer research and see more done, Robin has had a few conversations with Dr Jack, im sure.

### JoanneA — 2012-03-21T20:30:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3981>

Colleen, as people are on protocol, and still have weight to lose, the idea behind instict eating is Leptin, the more fat a person has the more leptin, as leptin levels drop, hunger increases so the idea is to eat along with the hormone, once leptin rises hunger goes away, but as a person gets closer to normal weight im sure hunger obviously increases, so breakfast would probably happen at some point upon rising, as well more frequent hunger levels as well requiring more food, so the theory is eating with that hormone. Thats my understanding, i believe there is yet more studys and testing to be done. Wow!!!! Those are huge forums!!!!!

### JoanneA — 2012-03-21T20:34:02+0200

### colleencoble — 2012-03-21T20:58:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-3996>

I think it's fine not to weigh while on the hcg. But it's DEADLY to ignore Dr. Simeon's advice to weigh every day after you are off the hcg. I've seen people regain so fast it's dizzying. And Simeon warns against trusting how your clothes fit. It's easy to get off with a steak day if you stay on top of things but if you let them go, BAM, hello ten pounds. Or fifteen.

Breakfast is fine to skip on hcg. But not later as Doc has taught us.  I could easily skip breakfast every day but I don't now that I know how important it is.

### Raine02 — 2012-03-23T14:09:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-4913>

New Guinea Pig coming out of hiding with my plan and a few questions. I have been reading MDA and Dr. Kruse's stuff for quite some time. I started the Leptin Reset in September and I'm doing well on it, still having trouble loosing weight, but I can maintain rather well which is a first for me, so I consider that a win. I'm taking the next step. I ordered the HCG patches, they seem to work well for a majority of people.

My Plan: I'm expecting TOM next week and should have my patches by the time it's over. I want to start right after to get the most time in. I plan on eating a low fat protein breakfast and then the same for dinner, skipping lunch. I am going to stick with lean meat and veggies, reserving the handful of berries for the days I just need something else, hoping that won't happen, but you never know. I am currently preparing mentally and thinking of different situations I may encounter and how to deal with them. I figure if I have a plan I may escape unharmed. I want to get a round in before summer hits because we are avid travelers and it's easy to keep up with the Leptin reset while traveling, I can forsee that avoiding fats while traveling may become difficult. I've got at least fifty pounds to lose, but would be thrilled to be under 166 for the first time in my adult life. That seems to me my magic number I diet my way down, hit 166 and lose it.

I want this to go well and so have a few questions. I am currently taking thyroid medication, Cytomel, can I continue on this during HCG? I plan to cut out all the oil based vitamins such as A and D3 and the fish oil, but want to keep the L-Glutamin, adreanal booster, and any others that are non oil based, is this a good idea or should I stop them all? When TOM rolls around again, I stop the patches, but continue to eat on plan, correct? I am currently looking for a no oil foundation, any suggestions. I currently use make up from 100% pure, but my tinted moisturizer has oil in it. All other makeup I will discontinue.

Looking for flaws or suggestions for my plan.

Thanks,

Lori

### MaggieC — 2012-03-24T21:06:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-5522>

In thinking about P2, isn't the only thing that matters is that you keep your calories at or around 500? If we did that, no matter what we ate, we would have to burn the fat in our bodies to make up the deficit. Wouldn't eating fat (as we do eating primal) just prime our bodies to burn fat instead of sugar?

The Gods know I'm no scientist, and Simeon apparently got it wrong about our bodies needing carbs for our brains to work. I bet it would be worth a test to see if we could do 200 calories worth of protein and the rest fat to see how our bodies react. What do you guys think of that? Has anyone tried it? I'm wondering if Simeon said "NO FAT" to keep his patients from going down a slippery slope. For us it would make more sense to say "NO CARBS"!

I agree with you Colleen, I won't be eating apples and oranges if I go back on P2, I would rather have the protein. Maybe an occasional handful of raspberries, which is what I do now. I have to do blood work and see where I'm at before I think about going back on P2. If I have to then, I'll count fish oil capsules toward my calorie count. I think I'm better off with it than without it, just a hunch.

### trish3 — 2012-03-25T06:32:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-5702>

i should also add: my thyroid functions at the low end of normal. I am chronically frozen (long underwear Sept to June), frozen feet year-round. Why would R1 make me warm?

Day 1: 81F, day 2: 70 F; day 3:62 F; stayed at 62F for the next ten days (tub alternating with spot CT and cold outdoor walking). Today, day 13 i did a 50F tub x 30 min (lower body) no problem. ### eleveneleven — 2012-03-26T21:42:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-6526>

Hi! I've done one round of hCG before, but I managed to gain all the weight back (devastating, but...it happens). During my first round, I lost 15 pounds in 35 days (including the load weight, I lost about 20 lbs) which wasn't TOO great, but it got me pretty close to goal. I've started eating paleo and in accordance with the leptin reset, but since I need to lose about 30 pounds, I'm waiting for more hCG to get here and then I'm going to do a second round. I've already decided to eat only Simeon's approved foods but with leptin reset timing, and then add CT in at night after dinner. Because I'm going full paleo, I'm leaving out the fruit and the breadsticks. I know I'm most likely not going to be able to start until the first or second week of April, depending on when the hCG gets here, but this is what a day will most likely look like for me:

**Breakfast (within 30 minutes of waking):

8oz chicken breast

1.5 cup spinach

.25 cup raw kraut

_Calories: 273, Protein: 54g

**Lunch/Dinner (timing depends on hunger, but no later than 6pm):**

4oz sea bass

100g asparagus

.25 cup fermented vegetable (either a different kind of kraut or sugar-free kimchee)

Calories: 198_

TOTAL CALORIES - 470** (might be a bit higher or lower each day depending on protein and vegetables)

I'm excited to try this, truly. I might add in lunch with a few ounces of protein and another vegetable if I'm really hungry some days, but I'll never exceed 500 calories or go fewer than 4 hours between meals. Can't wait to see the results you guys have with hCG and CT, too!

Just over a year ago I completed 4 rounds of sublingual rx hcg and lost 35 lbs in total (I had some gains inbetween rounds). I was able to keep most of that weight off for a year. Earlier this year, I tried a rogue protocol using rx injections and lost 6 lbs before I gave up. I gained that back and am currently 15 lbs above my ldw from my first go-round with hcg.

I have been working on the LR and CT and haven't seen much in the way of appetite suppression or fat loss yet. In fact, I've gained a couple pounds. Which is why I'm considering yet another round of hcg.

I don't know if my heart is in it for another round, but I feel really discouraged, too. I'm glad I was able to keep the weight off for over a year, but I really don't want to end up back where I started, which is where I'm headed if I can't get control of my appetite

_"Evo Mama (wrote)

Keto is the only way to do CT because there were/are no carbs growing in cold climates or during winter. So, if you eat carbs and do CT, you are causing a mismatch, leading to inflammation. You confuse the body that thinks it is winter by feeding it food that is only available in the summer.

Re: fats - I did see Doc answer someone on the blog that if they were doing HCG, to stick to the protocol. I'll have to go back and look. The search function wasn't working for me earlier.

Wouldn't it make sense though, NOT to add fats while using HCG? The whole premise of the 500/cal diet is that HCG releases your own abnormal fat stores for the body to digest, so essentially you ARE feeding fat to your body. Maybe issues arise in HCG only protocols because toxins are released with all of that old fat. (Oh the lessons CT has taught us!)

I have no idea what effect MCT oil and CO have on HCG, but I would think that the body would use the readily available fats first for energy before using the Ft. Knox protected "emergency supply" of stored fats. I keep checking this thread for progress reports.

HCG+CT=double whammy on the abnormal fat cells!

I can relate. Been there too.

If you read any of MamaGrok's posts about her experiences on the LR she had to restart many times because of bingeing. However the times between bingeing became less and less until she was binge free and has been since October. She gained ten lbs on the LR and said it's the best 10 pounds she ever gained because it started her healing. She also was hungry and kept adding more fat and protein. She has never been on HCG to my knowledge.

I am back on an HCG round now because I gained back the weight I lost on HCG Last year after stabilizing and keeping it off for 6 months

because I slowly went back to my old eating habits (sugar...one bite and that's it for me).

I have done an HCG round when my head wasn't in to it and really lost nothing to speak of that round.

I wish you hope and strength.

### ChimpChick — 2012-03-29T17:30:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-8275>

> **Claudia;8154 wrote:** I'm pretty sure I read that Dr. Kruse said to continue CT in the summer. Never stop CT if you want to continue to be cold adapted. Maybe you can do it less, not sure about that.

Neither am I... Here is what I have gathered.

1-CT has to be done on a strict Keto high fat diet.

2-Eating carbs (fruit) while doing CT causes a mismatch, inflammation, weight gain.

3-In the summer Doc has eaten up to 400g of carbs during the summer solstice.

4-One summer he did not do CT, not sure if this was the summer of eating 400g?

5-Doc does CT in the sun with ice all over him in the summer.. Is he then Keto, or eating seasonal fruit?

Then to throw all us HCG'ers for a loop...

1-HCG plus CT will light the world on fire..

2-With CT more fat is required, not less.

3-That would mean that if doing the HCG diet with CT, we must eat keto plus fat..

I know everyone who has ever done the HCG diet is terrified of fat, but according to the rules of CT we have to have it in our diet.

"HCG DIETING ALERT: Eating MCT, in winter is probably not the best choice for a cold adapted mammal, because they do not help fluidity of cell membranes. This is why we see so many problems in the literature with saturated fats in humans. The results did not make sense and researchers attributed them to disease generation. They did not make sense because they were studying animals at different stages of their mammalian biochemistry. These fats also can make a cold adapted mammal gain weight when eaten off season because of this mismatch. This is a reverse analogy to the one I used in the Paleo Summit with the banana in Canada. If you want to see proof of this fat reversal ask any person who uses the HCG diet how coconut oil or palm oil work for them on protocol. In short it sucks. Why? those oils protect warm adapted mammals eating tons of carbs. This is why CO and Palm oil are tropical oils and not found in our polar regions.

When you ask a human who uses HCG for dieting, you will find they do not do well with MCT’s during their HCG protocol use for this reason. In winter, mammals prefer animal fats like ghee, tallow, lard, bacon grease, and pastured butter as the best choices. This is wired into our brain by the CD 36 receptor and the floor of the fourth ventricle in humans too. Seafood is always a good choice no matter what season we are in. Pastured meats and offal are ideal too. Evolution is dictating what we should eat not Dr. Kruse. I am merely pointing out what many in our community are blind too today. I hope to change your reality tonight with this knowledge.

HUMAN Obesity Caveat: MCT however, will help the obese human however to reverse weight loss in winter because they are a far better choice than any carbohydrate at this time. It was my number one diesel fuel I used for my own weight loss. It will also make the obese person radiate heat if they use it in fall and winter which is a good sign that they are fat burning and not sugar burning. Coconut oil and palm oil are the ideal fats for spring and summer uses for humans. This works only for reversal of a disease and not for optimal living. I have a plan for optimal living once you reverse your disease here."

Suzie, I don't think it is a problem freezing your hcg. I always froze mine since I would buy the 5000iu and use for 40 days. This round I have my syringes filled for 20 days and froze them. I take it out then night before for the next day use and put in fridge to thaw. It has not lost it's potency. So...go ahead and use what you have with you.

Also, just get right back on and eat VLC. You may gain a pound or few  in the next couple days, or not. Or you may stall. You never know with a cheat.

Good luck!

### colleencoble — 2012-03-31T05:41:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-9103>

Well, it's pretty clear from Doc's post when he was asking for questions about CT that no one really knows what is best with hcg and CT. He's looking to us to prove or disprove whether adding fat is okay or not. So I'm going to experiment and add coconut oil. The worst that can happen is I'll regain the weight in a few months. I have enough hcg to do another round if I have to. I'm leery but it's not fatal.  And I've done plenty of rounds so I'll know whether it works or not by how I stabilize because I've always done okay. Steak days always drop me down and I settle right where I should. So I'll be able to tell for sure if the fat messes with stabilization even with CT added.

I'm the type that hcg was designed for--one with a broken metabolism who can't lose any other way. One who will gain or stall with the least change to the protocol. Some can lose with adding different vegetables or different proteins but I never have been able to vary it AT ALL. So we need people like me to test it. Younger ones with better metabolisms have often been able to very it some. We broken ones have to figure this out for others.

I'm scared. LOL

### MaggieC — 2012-03-31T10:26:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-9141>

Tomorrow is vlcd1 for me. I'm aiming for 60% P, 25% fat, and 15% carbs and 500 calories. That lets me get in a breakfast with 25g protein as well as lunch and dinner if I go with lean foods. I did blood work 2 days ago so when I get those results I'll post them. I did a Leptin Reset for a little over 6 weeks.

I'm also a broken metabolism person. I manage to get down to about 143 lbs. and then I stall... and bounce back up. When I try to go back to "regular" eating (P3) my weight drifts back up to the 150s. So this time, no fruit, no dairy, obviously no wheat. LR timing, and a strict 500 calories with no coconut oil. I also planned to throw in CT as well, and I'm already freezing! I will have to start with spot icing I think on my belly and hips.

Yes, I also keep my shots frozen. They keep much longer that way. I usually do 200 mg shots, and this round I'm trying 175 mg. Thank you Colleen! I'm also a bit scared in a way. I'm tired of "failing" with hcg. This round I want to drop 25 lbs and get to where I want to be. Then I'll move on to post leptin reset eating to stabilize. If I can stick to LR eating for 6 weeks, 6 weeks on hcg will be a breeze!

### Pamela — 2012-03-31T23:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-9398>

I have a question for people here. Why would you add fat while you are on the HCG protocol? The original document by dr Simeon as well as so many people saying that any cheat will not only cause a gain but give problems stabilizing. Even adding CT to the HCG protocol seems off considering Dr K says to eat a high fat meal prior to CT.

I will say what my experiment is. I just finished a round that I did by the Dr S book. Did it for 30 days lost 16 lbs and am now at 161 at 5'8" Had very little fruit and no grassini. The last round I did was in early fall and after 6-8 weeks I was not able to stabilize. Weight started creeping back on. This time I am changing a few things. This last round was done in winter which could be a better time. 1) CT 3-5 times per week with cold baths. 2) no eggs since they can cause inflamation with Hashimotos and I did not know this on last round. 3) no dairy 4) staying ketogenic for duration of CT by testing each morning 5) doing Leptin reset protocol.

I will do the above for 8 weeks and see how I stabilize. But this is after I got the weight off. I personally would not recommend adding fat while taking HCG.

### rocketmom — 2012-04-01T02:18:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-guinea-pigs.304/post-9561>

I need some help, desperately.

I am in R2P2. My first round was great and I did well stabilizing in P3 by using paleo/LR. Total losses from R1 25 from hCG and 5 from paleo/LR.

I started loading last Saturday. On day one I lost a pound. On day two of loading I gained 1.5. After one week I have only lost my load weight and .4! I loaded well with good fats, no sugar, no starch, no grains.

R1 I did some off protocol, using Walden Farms products, did some coco delite, and even used CO to stir fry chicken and shrimp.

VLCD day one I gained .4! Day 2 no loss so I dropped using the WF salad dressing. Day 3 no loss so I dropped one of the fruits. Day 4 no loss so I dropped the other fruit.

Day 5 no loss so I dropped one veggie and added some more protein. Day 6 no loss so I added more protein eating only one veggie and no fruits, no off protocol stuff. This am the .4 loss plus the load weight loss. Crap.

I am stumped. Obviously what worked in R1 isn’t gonna work in R2. I am planning a 40 day round. I need some advice.

I have not had beef, just chicken and shrimp. I did make chicken cabbage soup with my homemade bone broth. I only eat cabbage, lettuce, celery, tomatoes and onions since I can’t gag down cucumbers, greens, chard, spinach, fennel or asparagus. (Don’t condemn me, blame my mother who served such stuff when I was a kid and it was usually canned crap with tons of sodium and no flavor. Ick,Ick,Ick,) Not had any bathroom issues, everything’s fine.

Anyone have any thoughts? I know the hcg is good, expiration date is 9/2014 and it is RX Gonakor from Mexico, and I mixed it correctly. I am not hungry but feel the need to eat at normal lunch and dinner times.

I am perplexed to say the least. I am stubborn and refuse to give up.

Any insight (or even a hang in there!) is greatly appreciated.

dennise
