### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-10-13T23:13:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifers-moving-from-leptin-reset-ct-adrenal-rx-leaky-gut-rx-ai-to-optimal.323/post-58005>

> **KiwiLauren;59330 wrote:** Jen, I've had acne increase since going on P (not before). It is any particular time of the month, or constant? I'm trying to figure mine out, too. Do you have high T? (I'm sure you've written this info a lot before, but I can't remember.)

I don't have any recent BW for T. I am guessing that T is acting high because E may have dropped. I cannot live this way. It's really bad and painful. As soon as two "heal" (as in go down but now I have a scar on my face), two more come out. I know it is linked with IR and thought it was the fruit (in season but still) but I haven't touched a grape in forever and these ones are the worst yet. They are just getting bigger and more painful.

Right now I'm day 13, so it can't be my cycle. I get no reprieve.

But what kind of acne are you getting Lauren? I'm getting the cysts.

I want to try the Gymnema. ANYONE??

Cavemam if you are reading check out the blurb on Tribulus. Talks about increasing iron absorption. Chocolate were you looking for that too?

I was googling around antiandrogens and found some herbs to try. But honestly, I am really close to going back on an oral med. I can't do this.

[http://www.soulcysters.net/showthread.php/129222-Natural-Plant-Therapies-for-Healing-PCOS](http://http://www.soulcysters.net/showthread.php/129222-Natural-Plant-Therapies-for-Healing-PCOS)

Primary Herbs

Paeonia lactiflora (White peony)

Paeonia lactiflora has been used for gynecological conditions by both Chinese and Western herbalists, and is used by Western herbalists for PCOS, hyperprolactinemia, endometriosis, ovarian failure and androgen excess. Paeonia has been shown to positively influence low progesterone, reduce elevated androgens (testosterone) and acts to modulate estrogen and prolactin. (7) In vitro, the active constituent paeoniflorin has been shown to affect the ovarian follicle by its action on the aromatase enzyme. (8) Aromatase is important for follicle maturation, ovulation and corpus luteum function, steroid hormone synthesis and the regulation of the conversion of androgens to estrogens. The biofeedback in the pituitary and hypothalamus rely on aromatase to regulate prolactin and GnRH. The daily dose for Paeonia is 4.5 to 9 mL of a 1:2 dried plant extract. (9)

The traditional Chinese/Kanpo formula known as Shakuyaku-Kanzo-To or TJ-68, which is a decoction of Glycyrrhiza glabra and Paeonia lactiflora, has been the subject of a number of clinical trials, all of which demonstrate activity in the hormonal regulation of androgens. In one trial involving eight women with hyperandrogenism and oligomenorrhea, the formula was given for 2 to 8 weeks. This combination regulated the LH to FSH ratio. Over this period of time, serum testosterone levels decreased to less than 50 ng/dL and this resulted in seven of the eight women ovulating regularly. (10)

Another trial involved 20 women diagnosed with PCOS. The formula was successful in lowering testosterone in 90% of the women, of which 25% went on to conceive. (11) It is suggested that it acts directly on the ovary, increasing the activity of aromatase, which promotes the synthesis of estradiol from testosterone, thus lowering serum testosterone levels. It also seems to regulate the LH to FSH ratio. (12)

Gymnema sylvestre (Gymnema)

Gymnema is a traditional Ayurvedic herb used as an antidiabetic, hypoglycemic, lipid lowering agent and to support weight reduction. Gymnema possibly has a trophorestorative action of the beta cells of the pancreas. (13) The plant part used as medicine is the leaf. Gymnema is well indicated for PCOS, due to its insulin modulating activity and the added benefits of reducing the elevated triglycerides associated with PCOS. Key constituents of Gymnema include saponins, especially the gymnemic acids. Gymnemic acid suppresses the sweet taste on the taste buds, so if taken before food masks the sweet sensation. Gymnema has demonstrated hypoglycemic activity in experimental models of diabetes and regulated blood sugar in hyperglycemia. The mechanism of action also includes the inhibition of glucose absorption in the intestine. The daily dose of Gymnema is 3.5 to 11 mL of 1:1 liquid extract. (9,14) Since conventional medical models are focussing on pharmaceutical agents such as metformin to control PCOS, Gymnema may prove to be one of the most significant herbs in the treatment of the underlying factor of insulin resistance.

Tribulus terrestris (Tribulus)

Tribulus is an endemic weed to many regions of the world, such as the Mediterranean, India, China, South Africa and Australia and is commonly known as puncture vine. The aerial parts, particularly the leaf, are used for medicinal purposes in the Western tradition. As a result of Bulgarian research, Tribulus has become a popular herb for the treatment of female and male endocrine disorders. (15) It acts as a general tonic, aphrodisiac, estrogen modulator and androgen modulator and is used to restore vitality, libido and reduce the physiological effects of stress. (9,11)

The Bulgarian research has identified a unique steroidal saponin class known as furostanol saponins, and extracts are standardized to contain at least 45% of these saponins, calculated as protodioscin. The leaf is noted to be higher in these unique saponins rather than the fruit or root. Other active constituents include phytosterols and spirostanol glycosides.

The tonic activities of Tribulus have been shown to act by intensifying protein synthesis and enhancing the activity of enzymes associated with energy metabolism. It increased iron absorption from the small intestines and inhibited lipid peroxidation during stress. This leads to more muscle strength and improved endurance and stamina. (9)

To ensure the desired clinical results, it is recommended to use only the Bulgarian grown Tribulus standardized to 40% furostanol saponins. It is not interchangeable with the Chinese or Indian Tribulus. The daily dose of Tribulus corresponds to extracts containing furostanol saponins as protodioscin at 300 mg to 400 mg per day. In PCOS it is best used on days 5 to 14 of the menstrual cycle to restore menstrual regularity. For more information on Tribulus, see the forthcoming review which will be published in next months Townsend Letter.

Vitex agnus-castus (Chaste tree)

Vitex beneficial for ovulatory factors associated with PCOS, in particular it has been shown to downregulate the production of excess prolactin, a condition known as hyperprolactinemia. (16) Vitex is also postulated as having antiandrogenic properties. (16) Hyperprolactinemia is related to adrenal stress and hyperinsulinemia in PCOS.

Vitex reduced the thyroxin releasing hormone (TRH)-induced prolactin release (essentially a pituitary-thyroid axis problem), normalized shortened luteal phases, corrected luteal phase progesterone deficiencies and reduced PMS symptoms in women with luteal phase defects due to latent hyperprolactinemia. (9)

Vitex should be considered first line botanical therapy for hyperprolactinemia and given for the duration of at least 3 to 6 months.

Caulophyllum thalictroides is known by the common name of blue cohosh and is native to North America. Within traditional use among the Native North Americans it was used by women as a remedy for amenorrhea and profuse menstruation, both of which are common features of PCOS. It is particularly useful to being on the menses in PCOS. It has action as a uterine and ovarian tonic and pelvic anti-inflammatory. The known constituents of Caulophyllum root include glycosides, caulosaponin and caulophyllosaponin, which are known to stimulate the uterus. Other identified constituents include N-methylcystine, taspine and thalictroidine. (9) The daily dose is 1.5 to 3 mL of 1:2 dried plant extract. (9)

Sample PCOS Formula

Vitex agnus-castus 1:2 12.5 mL

Glycyrrhiza glabra 1:1 12.5 mL

Paeonia lactiflora 1:2 25 mL

Gymnema sylvestre 1:1 25 mL

Schisandra chinensis 1:2 25 mL

Dose: 15 mL per day or 5 mL three times daily 100 mL

In a case of a PCOS patient with amenorrhea, include Caulophyllum thalictroides at a dose of 2 mL per day to help induce the menses.

When a cycle has been initiated, change to Tribulus concentrated extract, equivalent to furostanol saponins (as protodioscin) 300 to 400 mg per day on days 5 to 14 of the cycle to ensure cyclic regularity.

Dietary Modification

A review of the extensive literature specific to lifestyle factors in PCOS demonstrates that an essential treatment strategy for ameliorating the symptoms of PCOS and resolving the underlying metabolic derangements is the implementation of a low carbohydrate diet. This will tightly control blood sugar levels and resultant insulin production. High levels of insulin result in high levels of triglycerides and low levels of high density lipoproteins, which puts these patients into a high cardiovascular disease risk category. Modulating the diet not only helps the female endocrine cycle, but also serves as preventative medicine against these cardiovascular risk factors. As the insulin levels normalize, this will also improve circulating levels of SHBG, therefore limiting the problematic effects of free androgens on the menstrual cycle. (2,6)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-13T23:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifers-moving-from-leptin-reset-ct-adrenal-rx-leaky-gut-rx-ai-to-optimal.323/post-58011>

I'm trying to rule out PCOS too. Dr K said he doesn't know if I have it (but I could); my new doc says no way do I have it. I don't think acne alone is anywhere close to getting us a PCOS diagnosis. My T was just over the range - new doc isn't concerned. Who knows. I'm getting the cysts, too (painful and larger, not the normal tiny pimples) and only on my chin and upper neck. I asked in Ask Jack (mine seems to be perfectly timed with ovulation) and he said it was hormone surges. Which makes me wonder for those of us on BHRT... since we know it's a hormone chain, when we take one we will naturally effect all the others. So it's really hard to work out what is happening. I also wonder about the liver. But sort of like you, I'm not sure what I can do... been eating keto epi paleo, drinking heaps of green tea and dandelion tea, taking lots of tumeric, AI, circadian, etc. I should probably read the Leptin Part Deux blog again. (I've also noticed my hair has a lot more natural oils lately... so face and hair are very 'teen'. However, wrinkles and energy do not match that picture.)

I try to remember healing is non-linear. But it's awful bumpy, too  I do however have a history of zinc deficiency and I've added back in supplementation (and also been dabbing it directly on my face). It seems to have helped. Are you going to be on the Spree Chat today? I know Gretchen is dealing with acne, too... maybe we can have a group think?

### Inger — 2012-10-14T12:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifers-moving-from-leptin-reset-ct-adrenal-rx-leaky-gut-rx-ai-to-optimal.323/post-58074>

Jennifer. I am sorry about your acne.. it sucks. My skin likes no dairy.. except of maybe raw organic goats milk from old brand goats. I can buy it here and drink it occasionally. Butter, cream, cheese.. makes me not pretty. I wonder if you would suffer less if you went HARD CORE with your food and light and dropped all hormones? That your body get a chance to regulate itself slowly? I am thinking of yucky foods like MHS, raw hearts and stuff. Rotten meat. Candles only after dark, triple protection when using computer evenings.. dark sunglasses when going to parties or shops or stuff after sunset. Yeah.. I know but I bet you would be happier with your outlook fast! Look, I can tell you it works for me that way at least. I kinda need to choose.. to please my palate.. or looking good. I cannot have both. Sure I eat delicious stuff too like salmon tartar and oysters and sashimi but they are well choosen and on purpose. Now I am used to stuff like raw lambs hearts carpaccio and like it a lot but it might be you need to get used to it first.

I am thinking when doing it the natural way it goes smoother... without too much turmoil because in the foods are all the stuff you need for detox and healing.. Now, it takes longer maybe? But the trip is funnier..

For skin I have been smearing bone marrow from reindeer (I bet any other would do as well) on my face and then some hawthorn berries on my face in the evening..lol Very natural, very cheap and I can tell you, I like it! I bet you could use any wild berries!I just had no money to buy the argan oil I was using so I thought.. eating the reindeer for breakfast, why don't use the marrow for my face instead? I sometimes make a peeling with some epsom salt, honey and olive oil too. I leave it on for a while after too, before rinsing. Makes the skin feel really good!

About fruit.. I would not eat any. I cannot, I can eat only wild grown berries and feel real good. Strawberries, any cultivated berries are not so good for me. The apples from our garden too do me no good. I will not eat these before I am very healthy.. maybe.

Just some thoughts.. might not be the right for you but I thought I could as well share what works for me...

### indigogirl — 2012-10-14T17:28:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifers-moving-from-leptin-reset-ct-adrenal-rx-leaky-gut-rx-ai-to-optimal.323/post-58108>

Jennifer, you might want to look at spironolactone to counter the cystic acne effects of T.

Spironolactone is used to treat many different disorders, from high blood pressure to fluid retention. Although the U.S. Food and Drug Administration doesn't recognize spironolactone as an acne treatment, it is often prescribed off-label to treat hormonally influenced breakout in women.

This medication isn't a first line of defense against acne breakouts. It is only effective against acne caused by hormonal fluctuations. But it can be especially helpful for women with hormonal disorders that trigger acne or who suffer with other problems, like unwanted facial hair.

I get cystic facial acne when I take T or DHEA. I took spironolactone while on T and DHEA and it prevented me from breaking out. I stopped taking all 3 because I felt like why was I taking a drug to counteract the side effect of 2 other drugs? I guess for me the T and DHEA benefits didn't outweigh taking a drug to counteract their effects.

You need a prescription for spironolactone.

Cold baths and FO, however, are on the menu.

thanks Inger....I wish I could not be so self conscious about it but I pretty much think ppl are staring at the monsters on my face. How do you get the bone marrow out? Before or after you cook it? Not sure I could get reindeer around here but maybe something else.

I stay away from fruit like the plague now and dairy has been out for so long, except ghee. I cut the ghee now but I'm pretty sure my estrogen is tanked again. Although the girls are still hanging in. Last time the e tanked, so did my bra size. Now I'm thinking about going up AGAIN...hormones are crazy weird. ### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-10-15T05:23:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifers-moving-from-leptin-reset-ct-adrenal-rx-leaky-gut-rx-ai-to-optimal.323/post-58246>

> **indigogirl;59546 wrote: Jennifer, you might want to look at spironolactone to counter the cystic acne effects of T.
>
>
>
> Spironolactone is used to treat many different disorders, from high blood pressure to fluid retention. Although the U.S. Food and Drug Administration doesn't recognize spironolactone as an acne treatment, it is often prescribed off-label to treat hormonally influenced breakout in women.
>
>
>
> This medication isn't a first line of defense against acne breakouts. It is only effective against acne caused by hormonal fluctuations. But it can be especially helpful for women with hormonal disorders that trigger acne or who suffer with other problems, like unwanted facial hair.
>
>
>
> I get cystic facial acne when I take T or DHEA. I took spironolactone while on T and DHEA and it prevented me from breaking out. I stopped taking all 3 because I felt like why was I taking a drug to counteract the side effect of 2 other drugs? I guess for me the T and DHEA benefits didn't outweigh taking a drug to counteract their effects.
>
>
>
> You need a prescription for spironolactone.
> Thanks for the info, Indigo...trying to avoid Rx but....
>
>
>

# Thread: What is better, showers or baths?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 43
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### laertes — 2012-03-20T18:37:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3317>

I just wanted to ask if anyone knows if showers or baths are more effective in CT?

As far as I understand, it's more about making the body think it's freaking cold than to actually cooling.

I, for one, can lie in the bathtube for 40 mins at 50Ã‚Â° F, no problemo. But when I try to shower at 50Ã‚Â°F, I'm a-screaming, raving, cursing the inventor of showers, I can hardly go on for 10 mins - besides, I'm out of curses at that point of time. Is that just a psychological thing?

### MJ* — 2012-03-20T18:43:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3326>

> **laertes;3340 wrote:** I just wanted to ask if anyone knows if showers or baths are more effective in CT?
>
>
>
> As far as I understand, it's more about making the body think it's freaking cold than to actually cooling.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> I, for one, can lie in the bathtube for 40 mins at 50° F, no problemo. But when I try to shower at 50°F, I'm a-screaming, raving, cursing the inventor of showers, I can hardly go on for 10 mins - besides, I'm out of curses at that point of time. Is that just a psychological thing?

I wonder if the shower is harder to do because of the air...even though it's not necessarily windy in a bathroom the air is hitting your cold wet skin and air could contribute to your discomfort, like the wind chill factor. I have not done a bath yet, just face dunks and cool showers, but I can't bring myself to make the shower cold. I think baths will be easier...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3328>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3352>

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-20T19:21:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3356>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-20T19:21:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3357>

I've found showers to be the most difficult! I'm ice packing and going to try to go to bath . I've been forcing myself to cold shower - and I seriously scream and jump and can't make it 2 a moment past 2 mins.

I'm still going with goal of keeping skin at 50-55 for as long as possible however that works.

Once spot icing starts, is there any benefit to continuing face dunks? Is there a topical effect directly on the skin?

### ealachan — 2012-03-20T19:36:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3377>

Spot icing doesn't bother me, immersion doesn't bother me. Luke-warm showers don't bother me (and are, in fact, my norm). COLD showers SUUUUCK, unless it's 90 degrees out...and even then, I have to be pretty frickin' desperate to turn the shower knob all the way to "cold".

### donkjellberg — 2012-03-20T19:41:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3382>

I have gotten use to putting the shower all the way cold. I feel by body tense up a bit and and get a similar feeling to my pool immersion. Bally's in my area has a cold pool. I may just have to join again for the convenience of taking a dip a few days a week. Now that was hard when I did it. My breathing choked up, and I could not do it for more than a few long minutes. I need to find out what the temperature is.

### JC2K — 2012-03-20T20:16:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3394>

I do both currently. Start off with warm shower to clean off, then start turning it cold and plug the tub. I letting it fill the tub turning colder every few minutes (to adjust) until finally at full cold (roughly 15 min). Then soak with blue ice in the tub for another 30 min or so depending on how long the magazines last.  That way I'm not wasting my water.

The shower stings more (and I feel like a roaster, turning around every 30 secs to a minute to get the cold shower on another bodypart), but neither's really been bad for me personally. I've noticed that as I adjust, I can turn down the cold faster, but haven't gotten to the "full cold OMG!! blast from the start" stage yet. I imagine I could, but I don't really see the need for the pain yet.

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-20T20:26:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3400>

I find the baths to be much more relaxing, so I use the cold showers in the morning, which give me a great burst of energy (adrenaline?), and the baths at night, which prepare me for sleep.

Both the cold baths and showers are getting easier. If I had to pick one to do all the time, it would be bathing.

I venture to guess that baths might be more efficient because your whole body is submerged and therefore a greater surface area of skin (and thus more receptors) are experiencing 50 degree temps from the water, however this doesn't take into account how cool air on wet skin affects those receptors. Since I'm not sure, I'm just going to continue to do both.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-20T20:33:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3404>

I bow to anyone who's able to do a cold shower. I can sit in a tub of floating ice for an hour or more, but can't seem to stand 60 seconds of cold water coming out of that shower head. I don't understand why, I only know it's like some kind of torture device that makes me want to scream.

This is one of those times when I think a poll function on the forum would be interested - we could see what percentage of readers here use a bath v. a shower, and then discuss how we've come to that.

### shilohman — 2012-03-20T20:41:53+0200

Clabbergirl, do you take bitter melon? My guess is as you develop more BAT, then a cold shower will become a piece of cake. Since there isn't a way to test for how much BAT we have, the best test that I have found (may be wrong on this) is the reduction of shivering = increased BAT.

### Clabbergirl — 2012-03-20T20:48:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3417>

I don't take bitter melon (but I was in the process of ordering some as I got this response). I hope I get a lot of perks from more BAT. I still haven't had shivering no matter how cold or how long I stay in. I've gotten close to shivering, and had goosebumps and cherry red skin, but no shivers at all. I've got a lot of weight to lose though, has to be throwing that process off somehow.

### JC2K — 2012-03-20T20:51:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3420>

The test they use for BAT is a PET scan to check metabolism/areas (brown fat has higher metabolism than white fat). $3k plus for a whole body scan, so pretty much no way to test for most of us.

### Laney — 2012-03-20T20:52:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3422>

After about 4 weeks, I can go from bed to straight tap water without much of a shiver. I'm good in 1-2 minutes or so. Not sure about the water temp, but must be lower than 10 degrees Celsius or so at this time of the year. I can comfortably go on but I stop around 20 min so as not to waste too much water. Immersion-wise, I typically feel like getting out in 50-60 minutes although I could press on if needed. Immersion feels generally harder because I keep hands in all the time and feet most of the time; in the shower, as I twist around, the skin at parts takes some rest until it adjusts, after which it's just... water. I likely feel better in the shower because I have to twist around. But as I mentioned, the water waste for long durations gets too much, so I opt for the full tub instead (actually shower for a while and when the level is good I lay me in)

### David — 2012-03-20T22:27:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3496>

Cold showers were quite a challenge at first. The "OMG! This is a SHOCK!" moment still happens, and I image one can never get past that. But, I find that a slow process works for me: taking a cool shower and scrub first and then standing aside to turn the water to cold, followed by something like a vertical face dunk (water stream hitting only the top of my head and face) \[to get the 'dive response'?\], for a few moments before turning and letting the first shock of the cold water hit the back of my neck and my back. Water temp varies 63-64F, so it isn't terribly cold and I can easily stay under the stream for a half hour.

I tried icing my chest and midsection 30 minutes yesterday, noticed no problems.

### JC2K — 2012-03-20T22:40:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3504>

As a side note, I find humming through chattering teeth in the shower part is entertaining. Not sure it's 30 minutes plus entertaining though.  Magazines make the soaking part go by faster too. The whole idea being to distract yourself from the cold vs concentrating on it.

### Sharon — 2012-03-21T01:09:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-3615>

No can do cold showers. Words come out of me that I didn't even know I knew.

### freezin4areason — 2012-03-21T22:58:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-4060>

> **eva;3850 wrote:** i havent got a tub so ive had to do just cold showers. usually 15-20 minutes sometimes 2 x a day. ive started to love the initial shock actually.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-21T23:10:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-4066>

stand next to your AC vent naked and set temp at 50 degrees......i do this twice a week. I am doing it now as we speak in my speedos.

### DaveJF — 2012-03-22T18:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-4478>

> **MJ\*;3349 wrote:** I wonder if the shower is harder to do because of the air...even though it's not necessarily windy in a bathroom the air is hitting your cold wet skin and air could contribute to your discomfort, like the wind chill factor. I have not done a bath yet, just face dunks and cool showers, but I can't bring myself to make the shower cold. I think baths will be easier...

I also find showers "feel" a lot colder than the baths at the same water temp. I believe there is solid physics behind the reason: a thin insulating film of water is built up in the tub, where movement is limited, and also the moving water of a shower carries away heat faster. With a shower the insulating film can't be sustained, as it would be in a tub. I've confirmed this as best I could with the thermometer I have - my skin temp matches the water temp in the shower but not in the tub.

I've read elsewhere (Ray Conise I believe) that hypothermia will be delayed, for someone falling into cold water, if they limit movement.. There have been studies carried out on that. The ultimate CT may be swimming in a large body of cold water - I believe also that Ray Cronise mentioned he has been experimenting with that concept recently.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-03-23T22:41:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5149>

My water outa the tap right now is 49 F. I guess I should be grateful, right?

I've been doing cold water immersion in the tub for almost 2 weeks now. I did my 1st attempt at a cold shower today - wow, different experience! The 1st 3 minutes were tough. I shivered immediately, my feet and hands were icy, and I wasn't a happy camper.

At about 8 minutes, I thought, I'm done...then the discomfort eased and I ended up stayed in for 20 minutes. Eventually, I got bored (normally I read in the tub), so I started doing some isometric flexing, squats, and that yoga pose where you balance on one leg. When I got out I felt...well, pretty darn awesome, sleepy, but calm and strong. I liked being able to move around in the shower and pump my arms up and down, etc.

Another shower plus is you can alternate face and body. That's harder to do in the tub and still keep your hands and feet out (hands and feet "in" ends my session too fast).

### Adria — 2012-03-24T00:21:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5189>

I can only do cold at the end of warm shower. Especially since I take showers in the morning, cold early in the morning just freaks me out. I do face dunks and cool off after the shower.

Baths and ice packs in the evening are best way for me.

### Powder — 2012-03-24T02:34:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5251>

I take 2 showers a day as cold as I can get them, and fit in baths with ice as I am able. The showers seem to get the skin cold much quicker, but it is more topical, as I don't shiver after like I do with a bath.

### differentstory — 2012-03-24T19:26:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5483>

> **sharon;3640 wrote:** no can do cold showers. Words come out of me that i didn't even know i knew.

hysterical!

### differentstory — 2012-03-24T19:32:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5487>

> **Powder;5284 wrote:** I take 2 showers a day as cold as I can get them, and fit in baths with ice as I am able. The showers seem to get the skin cold much quicker, but it is more topical, as I don't shiver after like I do with a bath.

That's awesome! What results have you seen? How many minutes a day are you doing CT?

### youthfuliz — 2012-03-24T20:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5519>

I'm in love with my cold showers  Just done with one - 30 minutes, from 75F down to 66F. The water wasn't too cold for me, and I was actually singing most of the time. Very stimulating.  This is my 7th cold shower and I am able to stay in longer each time. I want to enjoy it so I don't make the water too cold or the time too long. So that I'll be eager to walk in the cold shower next time. I did two cold bath too, which is easier. I'm doing showers these days since it's the TOM. I'm glad that I end up like the showers so much.

By the way, my skin is becoming more elastic and soft  Will definitely keep doing this! Right now every other day, may step up to 5 times a week. Thanks Dr. Kruse, such an easy way to stay young!

### LinD — 2012-03-24T20:57:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5521>

> **Adria;5221 wrote:** I can only do cold at the end of warm shower.

Same here, Adria.

I started off with cold baths. I tried to do showers, but found it much harder. However, the end of shower cold is no longer as hard as it was in the beginning.... even so, it is not likely I'll practice cold showers as a means to CT. I prefer the cold baths and the spot icing.

### Jim — 2012-03-24T23:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5568>

I was doing cold showers before the whole CT thing started based on this blog ([http://gettingstronger.org/2010/03/cold-showers/](http://http://gettingstronger.org/2010/03/cold-showers/)). I find baths easier too however I can do cold showers if follow a particular order. I point the shower head down so that I only get my legs wet. Then I lean over and wet my hair to shampoo it. After that I wash my legs then lather up my arms and chest. Then I rinse off the shampoo off my hair and the rest of my body. By this time my legs, arms and chest have adjusted to the cold. Once that happens I turn around and back into the shower to have the water run down my back. That is the worst of it for me. After just a few seconds I am fine. No screams or curses. I think the extra time I take gives my body a chance to get the whole BAT thing going.

### David — 2012-03-24T23:47:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-5572>

I agree, Jim. Showers seem to get easier as time goes on, but the first encounter between back and water is always stunning!

### Inger — 2012-03-28T10:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-7636>

I did cold showers until yesterday as we have no tub. Then hubby got me this as a gift;

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I have placed it outside in the backyard. I fit in it up to my neck easily when I sit down.

Now, how is it different to showers? A lot. I am shivering a lot more after! I have been in it for 4, 5, and 10 minutes. After the 10 minutes yesterday evening I shivered like crazy for half an hour. The strange thing is, I do not feel cold at all when sitting in it! Just the first seconds.. then my breth slows down and I feel goood. I just have to take it slow I guess, and not do more that 5 minutes twice a day for the first week.

The water is real cold, as it is still on the freezing point here nights (Finland).

I love it! SO much easier than shower I say!

### TheKid — 2012-03-30T18:33:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-8827>

At present I have only a shower available as my bathtub at home is just too small for me. The shower is kept constantly between 50-55 degrees. It continues to suck. The intial shock continues to make me gasp despite doing this for past 4 weeks almost. I thought I was just not cold adapting, but based on other people's experience, it seems that what I feel is consistent with others. And I do measure my skin temp, and it does reach the target range.

The problem is that it is hard to get all of your skin down to target range because of the lack of full body coverage in the shower. Basically, you always have to rotate and move around in order to keep hitting various parts of the body; and most of the time, my leg coverage suffers most and I doubt my legs reach the target temp range.

Interestingly, Jack just answered a question of mine on this very topic over in the comment section for CT7. I asked which was preferable: A shower that's 50-55 or a cold dip that's closer to 60. His response was that he always prefered immersion due to the overall coverage you get. So I may be switching to a cold dip soon. I'll let you all know how that feels after 4 weeks of showers.

### Pardus — 2012-03-30T19:33:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-better-showers-or-baths.324/post-8852>

> **TheKid;8878 wrote:** At present I have only a shower available as my bathtub at home is just too small for me. The shower is kept constantly between 50-55 degrees. It continues to suck. The intial shock continues to make me gasp despite doing this for past 4 weeks almost.

Agreed, cold showers are pretty uncomfortable. Up north here in Canada, the water still comes out pretty cold during the winter at around 40-50 degrees. I've been taking daily cold walks and cold showers for about 5 weeks.

I find that the initial shock of a shower can be reduced if I spend a couple minutes doing face dunks as a warm up (cool down?) beforehand. Then when I get into the shower, I stick my face under the stream first while holding my breath, using the dive reflex to counteract the gasping. I've tried the showers with feet and legs first as a comparison to this method, and noticed that going "head first" method seems to be a little less shocking. You pay more attention to the cold on your face because it's more sensitive, and comparitively the cold on your body isn't as bad.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Shivering
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-03-20T23:56:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3555>

So is shivering something you strive for? Or do you want to get to the point where you don't shiver any more? And what about shivering only after the bath?

I am intrigued by AKMan's NST (non-shivering thermogenesis) posts.

### AKMan — 2012-03-21T01:42:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3629>

Ok, here's another one to get you motivated, then! It is kind of mouse-based, but I think it applies to us as well.

Alterations in nonshivering thermogenesis are presently discussed as being both potentially causative of and able to counteract obesity. However, the necessity for mammals to defend their body temperature means that the ambient temperature profoundly affects the outcome and interpretation of metabolic experiments. An adequate understanding and assessment of nonshivering thermogenesis is therefore paramount for metabolic studies. Classical nonshivering thermogenesis is facultative, i.e. it is only activated when an animal acutely requires extra heat (switched on in minutes), and adaptive, i.e. it takes weeks for an increase in capacity to develop. Nonshivering thermogenesis is fully due to brown adipose tissue activity; adaptation corresponds to the recruitment of this tissue. Diet-induced thermogenesis is probably also facultative and adaptive and due to brown adipose tissue activity. Although all mammals respond to injected/infused norepinephrine (noradrenaline) with an increase in metabolism, in non-adapted mammals this increase mainly represents the response of organs not involved in nonshivering thermogenesis; only the increase after adaptation represents nonshivering thermogenesis. Thermogenesis (metabolism) should be expressed per animal, and not per body mass \[not even to any power (0.75 or 0.66)\]. A ‘cold tolerance test’ does not examine nonshivering thermogenesis capacity; rather it tests shivering capacity and endurance. For mice, normal animal house temperatures are markedly below thermoneutrality, and the mice therefore have a metabolic rate and food consumption about 1.5 times higher than their intrinsic requirements. Housing and examining mice at normal house temperatures carries a high risk of identifying false positives for intrinsic metabolic changes; in particular, mutations/treatments that affect the animal's insulation (fur, skin) may lead to such problems. Correspondingly, true alterations in intrinsic metabolic rate remain undetected when metabolism is examined at temperatures below thermoneutrality. Thus, experiments with animals kept and examined at thermoneutrality are likely to yield an improved possibility of identifying agents and genes important for human energy balance.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-21T01:45:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3631>

So... does that mean slow and steady wins the race? Get cold adapted slower and shiver less? (vs my method which is shiver for hours after the bath, be miserable and crave carbs (which is possibly because it's activating metabolism as opposed to BAT?)? I'm too cold to think properly. Or maybe not cold enough?

### AKMan — 2012-03-21T01:50:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3634>

And this one...basically this says that normal amounts of brown fat contribute to 5% of your metabolic rate. Just think, we can significantly grow our BAT through cold treatments, so in effect increase our metabolism.

The incidence of the metabolic syndrome has reached epidemic levels in the Western world. With respect to the energy balance, most attention has been given to reducing energy (food) intake. Increasing energy expenditure is an important alternative strategy. Facultative thermogenesis, which is the increase in energy expenditure in response to cold or diet, may be an effective way to affect the energy balance. The recent identification of functional brown adipose tissue (BAT) in adult humans promoted a renewed interest in nonshivering thermogenesis (NST). The purpose of this review is to highlight the recent insight in NST, general aspects of its regulation, the major tissues involved, and its metabolic consequences. Sustainable NST in adult humans amounts to 15% of the average daily energy expenditure. Calculations based on the limited available literature show that BAT thermogenesis can amount to 5% of the basal metabolic rate. It is likely that at least a substantial part of NST can be attributed to BAT, but it is possible that other tissues contribute to NST. Several studies on mitochondrial uncoupling indicate that skeletal muscle is another potential contributor to facultative thermogenesis in humans. The general and synergistic role of the sympathetic nervous system and the thyroid axis in relation to NST is discussed. Finally, perspectives on BAT and skeletal muscle NST are given.

### AKMan — 2012-03-21T01:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3637>

> **KiwiLauren;3656 wrote:** So... does that mean slow and steady wins the race? Get cold adapted slower and shiver less? (vs my method which is shiver for hours after the bath, be miserable and crave carbs (which is possibly because it's activating metabolism as opposed to BAT?)? I'm too cold to think properly. Or maybe not cold enough?

ha ha I know I haven't answered your question! I think nobody really knows what is better, but just getting cold without shivering is probably more do-able long-term, therefore more likely to be 'better', if only because you can keep on doing it. If you can freeze your butt off and keep doing it, that would probably get you there faster.

All the studies you see discuss activating the BAT at chilly--not super cold temps, so I'm sure non-shivering is OK. Whether that means shivering madly is better, I do not know.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-21T01:58:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3641>

I've read in detail almost all the studies - plus I've been watching this whole thing unfold since Jack started this series. I would advise to go slower. You should not be "real shivering" for the most part.

You may be a great candidate for the bitter melon, taken 2 hrs prior to CT. Also make sure you have eaten prior, and that you drink icy cold water (this makes the core start to heat).

From a long term perspective, you sound miserable! The ones who seem to be keeping it up long term are doing it as slow as THEY need to. There do seem to be some positives to be experienced along the way. Hours of shivering after seem to be making you miserable. Nobody will hit you if you back up a step and make sure you are adapting!

### Evo Mama — 2012-03-21T02:00:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3642>

I'm not sure where (on the boards or the blog), but I def read somewhere that less shivering means you are getting cold adapted, but I think someone also said that Dr K said shivering is good. If you are miserable for so long after, I'd try dialing it back a bit. On the blog, the good doctor does recommend taking things slowly.

### Mikey7 — 2012-03-21T02:05:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3649>

Each person have a different shivering factor. Some don't shiver that much and some do. I've definitely shiver after and during full immersion.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-03-21T02:18:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3665>

> **Cavemam;3666 wrote:** I've read in detail almost all the studies - plus I've been watching this whole thing unfold since Jack started this series. I would advise to go slower. You should not be "real shivering" for the most part.
>
>
>
> You may be a great candidate for the bitter melon, taken 2 hrs prior to CT. Also make sure you have eaten prior, and that you drink icy cold water (this makes the core start to heat).
>
>
>
> From a long term perspective, you sound miserable! The ones who seem to be keeping it up long term are doing it as slow as THEY need to. There do seem to be some positives to be experienced along the way. Hours of shivering after seem to be making you miserable. Nobody will hit you if you back up a step and make sure you are adapting!

I definitely eat right before (fat and protein) but I start shivering a bit as soon as I drink the ice water! (Albeit, it's a lot of ice, but still....). I would totally take the bitter melon seeing how I've already drank the Koolaid, but I cannot source it in New Zealand. I tolerate the ice packs pretty well but the baths leave me cold for hours and hours, so I think I'll take everyone's advice and dial it back. Thank you!

### tweety — 2012-03-21T02:37:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3682>

hmmmm.... I never shiver. I have only done about 5 baths. No idea what the temp is, but I dump in a medium load of ice (its not 20lbs, but its more than small) and I wear an ice pack on my torso. I am cold, and my skin is cold when I get out. But I feel warm immediately after and my legs, which I don't submerge, burn and radiate warmth. I sleep well but I notice my I usually wake up at some point in the night and my feet are freezing, which is odd because they never go in the water either.

I have been on the Leptin rx forever, but really didn't see any signs until three weeks ago( when I gave up dairy finally- stubborn, right?). So I am having a hard time accepting that this is easy for me. No signs of benefits of CT yet, but its just easy. Makes me think my water is probably a lot warmer than I think!

Can't wait until the thermometer gets here, because I will finally find out if my water is really warm or if I am adapting quickly.

### chocolate — 2012-03-21T05:00:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3774>

I shivered horribly an hour after a ridiculously cold pool. I was perfectly comfortable in the pool, very strangely. I thought it must be because of carbs. I see the insulin jumping up in diabetics from protein. I thought that was where I was getting it. idk enough yet. I should reread the liver blog.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-03-21T14:24:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3837>

I measured my tub last night, up to 20 minutes immersion.

My well water is 50 F in the tub.

I have only shivered once, and it was my very first 5 minute immersion bath. After that, I have not shivered for cold showers, immersions, icepacks - not sure if that is good or bad.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-03-21T18:57:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3938>

My first bath I shivered violently (but also overdid it, probably hypothermic), and since then I haven't really shivered. I've been lowering the temperature of the water slowly every day. Today I had the weirdest sensation of everything inside me shivering whilst I was in the bath, and now my skin feels as though it's crawling. Is this a good thing, or not? I've also never shivered from the spot icing, nor from the cold showers - just this weird feeling following today's bath.

### meyoolia@gmail.com — 2012-03-21T20:30:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-3982>

Bath number 12 last night (11 & 12 were at 48F) and as long as I can bop around and keep moving for 20 minutes or so afterward, I only spend the first 10-15 post-CT minutes shivering now. The first few dunks, I shivered madly for over an hour or so afterward - yay progress! And, science geek that I am, I started measuring pre and post CT water temps: #9, raised it from 50-53F in 40 minutes...#10 same... #11, 48-52F in 35 minutes... and last night, 48-53F in 40! I'm HOT! No actual tub shivering, just some 'buzzing' and goosebumps now and then. (and, yes, once I did measure immediately post and 40-minutes post tub temps - no change)

### Groketteliveson — 2012-03-21T21:09:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4006>

What's weird is some days I shiver and then some days I dont.

### Phoenix — 2012-03-21T21:52:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4020>

I've done 5 baths. The first 4 made me miserable pretty much all night long. Couldn't sleep because I was freezing, then couldn't stay asleep because of hot flashes.

So I decided to dial it down a bit. Last night I upped the water temp by about 10 degrees. So I took a 65 degree bath. And yes, I was shivering even in 65 degrees. But I warmed up before bed, and didn't have any hot flashes at all last night.

I read somewhere that Dr. Kruse said that 65 degrees is OK. You'll reach optimization, just more slowly than at 55. I plan on very slowly taking my water temp down to 55. I was pushing it too hard and too fast. So I'm really glad that I decided to warm things up a bit, rather than just quitting because I hated it so much.

### Croak — 2012-03-21T22:03:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4029>

I need a good couple hours after a soak before bed if I want to go to sleep "on time". CT soak too close to bedtime results in a racing mind for a good hour after lying down.

### differentstory — 2012-03-21T23:17:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4070>

> **Croak;4055 wrote:** I need a good couple hours after a soak before bed if I want to go to sleep "on time". CT soak too close to bedtime results in a racing mind for a good hour after lying down.

It's amazing how different we are. I fall asleep within 30 minutes of CT, so I have to do it at night. CT seems to exhaust me.

### Croak — 2012-03-21T23:25:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4073>

> **differentstory;4096 wrote:** It's amazing how different we are. I fall asleep within 30 minutes of CT, so I have to do it at night. CT seems to exhaust me.

Keep in mind I've been doing this for over six weeks now.

I added a second (well, first) session in the morning starting yesterday, and I'm bright-eyed and bushy-tailed for hours. Skipping lunch the last two days has been harder though, the morning soak (30 minutes, ~42F water) induces hunger a couple hours earlier than usual. And of course, with the evening soak, I wake up ready to eat a horse.  That's going to make CRON harder for awhile, if I go that route.

All that said, if I allow enough time between my soak and bed, I sleep great (but I was already sleeping great before that too, thanks to the LR).

### Jack Kruse — 2012-03-21T23:27:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4076>

> **Groketteliveson;4032 wrote:** What's weird is some days I shiver and then some days I dont.

normal.....still happens to me after 6.5 yrs

### differentstory — 2012-03-22T16:47:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4423>

> **Croak;4099 wrote:** Keep in mind I've been doing this for over six weeks now.

Skipping lunch the last two days has been harder though, the morning soak (30 minutes, ~42F water) induces hunger a couple hours earlier than usual. And of course, with the evening soak, I wake up ready to eat a horse.  That's going to make CRON harder for awhile, if I go that route.
>
>
>
> All that said, if I allow enough time between my soak and bed, I sleep great (but I was already sleeping great before that too, thanks to the LR).

That is so awesome! I was wondering why we were calling it "croak soak".  I get it now.

So how are you doing?? Can you share any of your CT achievements? And how often do you do CT??

I feel like 90 percent of this forum is at the same place in their CT journey. Right now I am a crabby hormonal mess. I am hoping that goes away soon.

### Croak — 2012-03-22T17:39:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4442>

I'm male, 47, 5'10"-ish, just at 270 pounds when I started doing CT sessions. I CT twice a day now, after breakfast, and after dinner, for half an hour in the mornings, and up to an hour (depending on how good my book is) at night. Cold water out of the tap is around 42F.

I started with my water in the 55F range, and tried just climbing in the first day. I discovered the first benefit of CT right then and there...I can jump a lot higher than I thought. Sadly, the landing wasn't as well executed. So that's why I came up with the "boil a frog" method.  I did that for the first couple weeks. Never mucked around with ice packs.

Now, I simply fill the tub with straight cold water, climb in, grab the Kindle and relax. I keep my hands and most of my arms dry but my feet in the water. I have a standard modern tub (i.e. too damned small), so my lower body is covered, and I slouch enough to get my most of my back in the water, knees peeking out. For the first minute or so, my lower legs and feet are painful, but it's not excruciating.

Weight loss is proceeding, about 2 pounds per week on average (was 255 this morning), and I'm not doing any painful calorie restriction, though I'm certainly eating below maintenance, and ketogenic.

Body comp has visibly improved as well. And the reason I keep my feet in the water is to soak my shin areas, which had horrible pitting edema that has very nearly cleared up. The pain from walking that caused the edema (or associated with it) has also almost completely gone.

Once out of the tub in the evening, it takes me a couple hours to fully warm up (and this is the point where trying to sleep is futile), but there's really no discomfort during this period, just cold skin. Shivering during or after the soak is rare and quick these days, even though I'm doing longer soaks in colder water, and my skin temp is for sure getting down below 55F.

I get dressed and walk to work not long after my morning soak (which is filling now, btw) and by the time I've walked the 20-25 minutes to the office, my soaked areas are close to normal temp, thanks to being covered and the exercise, even though it's 30-40F outside.

### differentstory — 2012-03-22T20:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4598>

> **Croak;4470 wrote:** I'm male, 47, 5'10"-ish, just at 270 pounds when I started doing CT sessions. I CT twice a day now, after breakfast, and after dinner, for half an hour in the mornings, and up to an hour (depending on how good my book is) at night. Cold water out of the tap is around 42F.

Shivering during or after the soak is rare and quick these days, even though I'm doing longer soaks in colder water, and my skin temp is for sure getting down below 55F.

Thank you so much for the post!!!! That totally made my day.

Just had to say too that I was sitting in the doctor's office waiting to have labwork when I saw your post. Unfortunately, I laughed before I could stop myself and then other patients started looking at me funny.

So nice that you can walk to work!

### nonchalant — 2012-03-23T16:56:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-4985>

I'm so tickled, I just had to change my name. This is Sally, now officially Nonchalant.

I woke up a bit early this morning, and I was thinking about CT. I was trying to figure out why I look forward to my cold baths. I know some don't. Maybe it was because I started with warmer water? Anyway, the baths seem to give me a psychological lift. It's subtle, so I'm really not sure. But I think about my next bath quite often, and prepare gladly for it. Stepping into the water feels just like stepping into a pool, in the middle of summer. Cold, but thrilling.

Anyway, I was still in bed, thinking about the baths, when my left thigh began to gently shiver/tremble. So strange! I wasn't the least bit chilled. It continued, until that knee and hip and even up into my back was shivering. So neat!! I was smiling big time, there with my sleep mask on. My right knee joined in for a very short time, but it all subsided within 30 minutes. A few minutes later, I was tossing aside the sheet that had been covering me.

Go BAT, go!! I just couldn't wait to start my day today!

I'm only up to 35 minutes, at 50-55F. The last 3 baths have been at 35 minutes, guess I'll go for 40 next time.

### Mikey7 — 2012-03-23T17:43:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shivering.332/post-5004>

Felt good today! 50 minutes and after that I usually shiver a lot but not today! I know its not a linear process but it feels good not shiver like a mad man.

### Shijin13 — 2012-03-21T15:23:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-3861>

I didn't use it on the LR - but I have it in the morning now w/HAB on the post Leptin RX...

now that said - when I was doing the LR I was using the following: [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread18090.html](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread18090.html) in my coffee daily...

In all honesty - I'll never go back from BP coffee...right now I'm trying to finish up the coffee I have and plan to order some BP beans from BPExec.... I use ghee- I make from kerry gold unsalted butter...If I can't have bp coffee I'll just have it black from now on....

that said if you don't have adrenal problems - there shouldn't be a reason why you can't have a cup of BP coffee - also if you read up on what makes good coffee beans - on BPExec's site: [http://www.bulletproofexec.com/what-dr-mercola-didnt-say-about-coffee-brains-and-muscle-video/](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/what-dr-mercola-didnt-say-about-coffee-brains-and-muscle-video/)

good beans won't give you the shakes or anything of the sort...what gives you that is the mold and associated toxins in low grade beans - its one reason why stabucks coffee effin sucks - they buy the lowest grade beans, and burn the crap out of it...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-03-24T17:48:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-5446>

> **donkjellberg;5121 wrote:** I tried it yesterday and it tasted good! Going to add cocoa and cinnamon to the mix today. Everything can be optimized, lol.

I add the 99% dark chocolate, cinnamon AND tumeric. It's slightly yellow, but tastes mostly of chocolate and cinnamon.

I also add a lump of the grassfed raw heavy cream instead of butter. I figure it's practically butter anyway since I have to have a sturdy spoon to get it out of the jar. It does make me wonder how they get it out of the cow!

### Cody — 2012-03-24T18:58:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-5470>

Bulletproof coffee is the recipe, good coffee, blended with pastured butter and coconut oil.

The coffee product that is sold on the bulletproof exec's website is called [Upgraded Coffee](http://http://www.bulletproofexec.com/?a_aid=4f7b0d43e59bb). And my ten pounds showed up this morning. Too late for my morning coffee, but I'm going to hit Trader Joe's and World market and see what kind of cinnamon I can find to grind up with the beans...

### Valorie33 — 2012-03-24T19:12:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-5478>

If you don't want to do the coffee it is excellent using Green Tea. I used to have coffee every morning, after tasting the bulletproof way with Green Tea I ended up converting to tea every morning very easily. Now coffee is a treat for once or twice a week.

### Lexi — 2012-03-24T19:29:58+0200

### Valorie33 — 2012-03-24T19:49:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-5494>

I just use the butter, [coconut oil](http://http://store.nutiva.com/aff/7563933154C0D4013687DE48F5051500/index.html), cinnamon and stevia. I make butter from heavy cream, usually about 7 oz, and add 7 oz of coconut oil, lots of cinnamon and some stevia to the mixer and made a tub full that lasts about a week if I am the only one using it. I use two tablespoons in my morning coffee.

It goes much faster when my bf and son get in on it. My bf has had to cut down as it makes him too full to actually eat and he is trying to gain weight.

### Cody — 2012-03-24T19:58:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-5504>

I ended up making a batch of coffee. Just had to try it. And it's still early in the afternoon yet.

I mixed a half a pot of black coffee with 2 TB coconut oil and 2 TB butter. Delicious!!!

I thought that maybe the effects that this guy listed in his blog were marketing drivel and placebo, but thought it was worth a shot. But this coffee really does impact you the way he says.

Well worth the money. My 10 lbs should last me the rest of the year.

jealous! I've got a ways to go but changes are happening... took me a while to get where I'm at.... I'll be there, its just a matter of when...

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-03-27T23:12:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bulletproof-coffee.340/post-7361>

> **Shijin13;7382 wrote:** jealous! I've got a ways to go but changes are happening... took me a while to get where I'm at.... I'll be there, its just a matter of when...

Don't be jealous, you will get there. Trust me it has not been a cake walk. I am in the health and fitness field. I was a heavy kid, I yo-yo ed my whole life I have been super lean and super heavy (for me) I have used all the tricks and knowledge that I have but the weight would always come back. This last time I bloated up to 210 lbs from 180 that I maintained for years. I hold my weight in the middle so with a little clothing magic most people can not tell when I gain or lose until it is over/under 30 pounds.

In October 2011 I was 210 at 27% BF I am now 175 at 11.5% I started paleo(again) in Oct but this time I found the Dr. and implemented the Leptin Reset. I knew about leptin and hormones but never really applied the theories to myself. This time I did and BOOM!!!! The other day I ate a couple of cookies from fresh market. In the past they were my favorites and I would eat a box in a sitting. I bought two out of nostalgia and ended throwing one away because They just didn't taste good to me anymore. eating to me now is just something I have to do. I much rather chew on a chunk of meat......

Now I am LS and IFing moderately and find it easy and I do not think about food anymore. I just have to master CT which I am getting benefits from. I have one bathroom on my condo and it is a shallow tub. So I do what I can with packs and I bought a cold water circulating machine and large pad. which works pretty well. I live in Fl so the weather is already hot so no rivers or cold lakes around lol.

# Thread: Weight Loss on Leptin Reset -- for men
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 34
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BenG — 2012-03-21T17:52:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-3913>

I noticed the following in the original Leptin Rx that I wanted to clarify from any guys who have been doing this for a while:

"A. Men will notice quick weight loss.

B. Women will notice mood changes first (calmer/sleepy) and their sleep will improve. Their clothes will fit differently but weight may not change drastically initially because of effects on the pituitary. This will change too if they continue moving forward. "

My question for the guys...did you weight barely move for the 6-8 weeks and then drop off quickly around the time LS came up? I'm noticing some of the symptoms, (and I'm CTing), but the initial weight loss I had I think was just because I happened to be eating less via a reduced appetite. OTOH, I don't think I'm fully sensitive yet (I'm at week 7 now).

I'm thinking that once full LS is acquired in the brain, liver, and muscles, then fat loss occurs rapidly. But during the first 6-8 weeks (on average) the fat loss is little to insignificant, then like a switch, it drops off quick?

Anyone who has been on the reset a while..could you tell what really happened?

Thanks

### Croak — 2012-03-21T20:13:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-3971>

I was stable for weeks (early December through end of January), Then it just started dropping, and coinciding with several signs (sleep, temps, etc). After a couple weeks of that I started doing daily CT, and it's still dropping at about the same rate, roughly 2 pounds per week on average.

I wouldn't call that rapid, but it's not far off what Mark Sisson calls "effortless". I do stay below 25g carbs (often WELL below that), but don't avoid dairy (make my own whole milk kefir, eat hard cheeses). I also ate a helluva lot larger BAB the first several weeks, and took awhile to actually get the fat intake up, I suspect I was getting too much protein early on and not enough fat.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-03-21T21:07:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4004>

I'm at about the same # of weeks as you. I have been fairly strict with the exception of dairy. Big change was cutting all rice and white potato. Also the high protein breakfast. I have snacked nearly zero but did not do a lot of that before. Protein may be a little low as my usual breakfast is 4 quality eggs and 3-4 good sized pieces of thick bacon.

I have lost about about 12lbs in a fairly consistent manner. Weight loss wasn't really a goal although I have some stomach fat I'm now motivated to shred. I'm considering moving into post reset but this isn't torture and I don't want to be "wrong" that I'm sensitive. For that matter I may have been sensitive to start with. However, I had bad rT3 and hs-CRP labs in October that indicate inflammation. Just had my blood drawn last week again so we'll see where I'm at...

Hang in there.

### AKMan — 2012-03-22T02:29:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4161>

Hey Ben - I started on Paleo/Primal Blueprint before i knew about the Leptin Reset, but I pretty much did the same thing. I stopped snacking, started eating a big, low-carb breakfast when I got up and skipped lunch most days.

It took a month or two, but soon enough I quit being hungry all the time. I was losing weight right from the start even without counting calories or carbs. The only carbs I was eating were fruit and veggies--no potatoes or rice.

When Jack started posting about the LR in late summer, I was like--'hey, that's what I did!' I saw all the signs he talked about (no cravings, losing weight, better sleep, not sweating like a pig). I started following his post-reset plan as soon as he came out with it and it has worked like a charm.

Back in 2010, I weighed 250 lbs. I now weigh 168lbs and have a 6-pack and feel awesome. My weight came off slowly but surely and I never had 2 months in a row where I gained back.

Not sure if this helps, but that's my story and I'm sticking to it!

### David — 2012-03-22T16:24:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4398>

Like AKMan, I was eating a Primal Diet for some time before encountering this site and the Leptin RX. Eliminating grain, sugar, dairy and legumes had a definate result: I lost 35 pounds fairly rapidly. Since starting Leptin Rx I have noticed the lack of cravings, better sleep and change in sweating, but my weight loss is not so noteworthy. CT has made for a bit more energy, I wake at 6AM or so every day and I am never tired enough to hit the sack before 10PM. Oh, and I never feel the urge any longer for an afternoon nap. Last week, for the first time in 14 years, I cleaned out the garage. (Found my favorite pocket knife!)

### Cody — 2012-03-22T19:29:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4534>

This is a great question.

I've lost no weight from the Leptin Reset. However, keep in mind that my starting weight was 456 pounds and I've lost 140 pounds so far on low carb and PSMF (100 grams of nothing but protein per day).

So my hormones and metabolism are probably FAR from optimal.

My most recent round of PSMF, I lost 20 more pounds. I got down to 310 lbs (I'm 6'4") and I was using ephedrine along with PSMF.

I felt crappy and constantly cold. My skin was extremely dry and flaky at all times. Thyroid issues anyone???

I decided to do CT and use the Leptin Reset at the same time.

I gained 10 pounds in about 2 weeks. It's ALL MUSCLE!!! Seriously awesome.

My weight is starting to come back down now, I'm at 318.

The first time I tried the Leptin Reset was many, many months ago, and I gained weight then as well, but didn't know about CT (none of us did) and wasn't sticking out 2 months of weight gain (or more) to see myself healed.

Leptin Reset combined with cold showers, sleeping in 40 degree temps at night, and many hours of home-made ice vest wearing a day has SIGNIFICANTLY helped here. See that Avatar photo up there in the left hand corner? I'm thinking that CT and Leptin Reset diet can get you to his level of muscle WITHOUT THE STEROIDS!!!

### tweety — 2012-03-22T20:38:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4590>

Woman here, but asking for my guy. Are hormones and stress enough to stop someone completely from seeing success? He has about 60lbs to lose, definitely low in testosterone, 42 yrs old... ton of stress and bad sleep. His appetite is fairly nonexistent, but we are thinking its due more to stress than true appetite because on the weekends he gets very hungry. However, he is really responding positively to CT....

stress reduction is a work in progress, and we're hoping the CT straightens out hormones and thyroid.

thoughts?

### David — 2012-03-22T20:49:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4596>

From all I have been able to absorb on this site, tweety, the answer is yes. Sleep problems, overweight, hormones and stress seem to be a very difficult to handle set of circumstances, but it seems that you have come to the right place!

### AKMan — 2012-03-23T02:56:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4773>

> **tweety;4618 wrote:** Woman here, but asking for my guy. Are hormones and stress enough to stop someone completely from seeing success? He has about 60lbs to lose, definitely low in testosterone, 42 yrs old... ton of stress and bad sleep. His appetite is fairly nonexistent, but we are thinking its due more to stress than true appetite because on the weekends he gets very hungry. However, he is really responding positively to CT....
>
> stress reduction is a work in progress, and we're hoping the CT straightens out hormones and thyroid.
>
>
>
> thoughts?

Get him to straighten out his diet and sleep before worrying about anything else. A strict primal diet, no snacking, and plenty of sleep in a dark room. Get a couple weeks of this under his belt, then start exercising some (pushups, pullups, easy stuff to do). See where this gets him in 2-3 months, my bet is he will be a new man, then if he wants to get even better in shape try cold and exercise more. If he doesn't respond in a few weeks, the leptin reset might be in order.

### BenG — 2012-03-23T07:08:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4871>

> **David;4624 wrote:** From all I have been able to absorb on this site, tweety, the answer is yes. Sleep problems, overweight, hormones and stress seem to be a very difficult to handle set of circumstances, but it seems that you have come to the right place!

I asked Dr. K about weight loss rate, and he said it depends on Vitamin D and Testosterone level. In my case, in 1 month of CT, my T went from 224 to 336. Dr. K said it takes about 3-6 months of CT for T to really go up, so I figure fat loss will accelerate as CT continues to improve hormonal profile.

Regarding Vitamin D. I was 7 ng/dl in December. Been Supping 15k-20k a day, so it's 83.3. At least something has improved. Onward!

In Dr K's case, he said his T went from 300's to 800's in 3 months in his radio interview.

### donkjellberg — 2012-03-23T09:26:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-4879>

> **AKMan;4187 wrote:** Hey Ben - I started on Paleo/Primal Blueprint before i knew about the Leptin Reset, but I pretty much did the same thing. I stopped snacking, started eating a big, low-carb breakfast when I got up and skipped lunch most days.
>
>
>
> It took a month or two, but soon enough I quit being hungry all the time. I was losing weight right from the start even without counting calories or carbs. The only carbs I was eating were fruit and veggies--no potatoes or rice.
>
>
>
> When Jack started posting about the LR in late summer, I was like--'hey, that's what I did!' I saw all the signs he talked about (no cravings, losing weight, better sleep, not sweating like a pig). I started following his post-reset plan as soon as he came out with it and it has worked like a charm.
>
>
>
> Back in 2010, I weighed 250 lbs. I now weigh 168lbs and have a 6-pack and feel awesome. My weight came off slowly but surely and I never had 2 months in a row where I gained back.
>
>
>
> Not sure if this helps, but that's my story and I'm sticking to it!

Same thing with me but I lost 130 pounds in about a year. Went from 310 (BMI 40) to 180. I have been stable for a year now and want to get to optimized. So, I eliminated my raw kefir, curds and whey, and cheese. Now I am acclimating to CT (two weeks) and have noticed I feel like a bad ass and with a defense system no pathogen can breach. In addition to just feeling stronger, healthier, and more upbeat and energetic. I am aware of the placebo affect and this is not that. Something has changed but I can't put my finger on it.

I don't have any health problems (anymore lol) and my BMI is 23. So, there isn't much fat to lose. I believe what I have will slowly burn as I increase my adherence to CT protocol and the other related factors (X, hint hint) over time.

### Croak — 2012-03-25T18:58:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-on-leptin-reset-for-men.343/post-5867>

I'm seeing the scale drop steadily (did my weekly weigh-in this morning, -4.6lbs for the week!). On Monday I started doing a combination of two-a-days or double length soaks, for a total of at least an hour daily in 40f-ish water, which is what I attribute to the more than doubling of my average loss rate this week.

Seeing the numbers going down faster is sweet, but I'm becoming a bit awestruck at the rapid body comp and capability changes. I can see my ribcage again. I can see my special purpose _without sucking in my gut right after getting out of my soak_! Pushup, pullup and setup gains (returns?) have been rapid, so much so that I think I'd actually pass a Marine PFT (though the ability to do 18-20 minute 3 mile runs like I did when in the Corps 25 years ago is probably not coming back). Tone and definition are visibly returning.

Pitting edema on shins is minor now, and there is no pain at all when walking...it's just gone.

Took my motorcycle out for a total of 8 hours yesterday, in a combination of heavy traffic, twisty mountain roads, and high-speed highway blasts. It's a "naked" sport bike (Aprilia Tuono for those that care) and that much saddle time in those conditions (and SAD diet) would usually wipe me out and leave me feeling it the next day, even in late summer when I'd been acclimated to it for months.

Yesterday, got off the bike, stretched a little and was fine, with energy to spare. Did dinner, an hour CT, my evening calisthenics, got up this morning and feel great, so great that I'm shortly going back out on the bike again. Oh, and of course, I didn't need to turn on the heated gear even when blasting 70+ MPH down the highway for an hour (to get to and from the good roads), even though outside temps were around 45F.
